- **Date:** 2008-01-01T14:55:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com>`

```
On Jan 2, 10:51 am, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> On Fri, 30 Nov 2007 15:32:07 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> contradictions.

'The' is the definite article. Your use of it in "the creation story"
implies that there is just one, or that you only know of one. There
are, in fact, hundreds of 'creation stories', all of them are just
stories. Each of them originated in small territorial areas as may be
expected from tribal story telling and exageration of ancestor's
adventures.


> I believe that enshrining evolution as the best
> explanation is every bit as narrow minded as forcing "Intelligent
> Design" as the only explanation.

I am not sure evolution is 'enshrined'. Unlike religions, science has
no problem in changing when evidence shows that it must, granted it
may take some time.

'Evolution' is the observable changes that have occured and continue
to occur. For example: the evidence is that once, more than 60 million
years ago, the world was dominated by large dinosaurs and the only
mammals were small shrewlike creatures.

Today the birds are the only descendants related to dinosaurs, and the
large animals are mammals.

That is 'evolution', that is the word that applies to those changes.

You are probably arguing about the _causes_ of evolution. There may
still be some deniers that say, for example, that dinosuars never
existed and the fossils were created in situ, or perhaps that
dinosaurs all died off in 'the flood', it seems that some even claim
that dinosuars still existed much later, such as 14th century.

'Evolution' is not an 'explanation', it is the term applied to the
observable changes.


An 'explanation' may be derived from observations as 'Natural
Selection', or 'Genetics', or 'Hybridisation', or 'Mutation', or
'Sexual Selection', or 'Inheritance of Aquired Characteristices
(Lamarkism)', or combinations of some of these (Lamarkism is no longer
accepted in general).

Alternately an 'explanation' may be derived from dogma or mythology,
or simply invented without any observable support.

The so called "Intelligent Design" purports to be both an explanation
of evolution and a denial that it occurs, or even occurred.  However,
it is not an explanation of anything at all merely stating 'goddunnit'
while pretending that it isn't about any 'god' at all.


> Both explanations require
> assumptions which may have absolutely no basis in fact.

The only 'assumption' of science is that what we can observe is what
there is. If we cannot detect it or measure it and it does not behave
like what we can observe then it is 'supernatural' and not part of
science.
```

There is debate as to whether dinosaurs (or at least some of them)
were warm or cold blooded and whether some (all) laid eggs.  I don't
follow the issues closely enough to evaluate the snippets I have heard
on such radio programs as "The Current", a news magazine show and

exists does not mean that one has to believe the creator is good,
omniscient, omnipresent or omnipotent.  The creator only has to be
able to bring about the creation and modify it.

that I am skeptical about.  The amount of extrapolation and number of
assumptions that have to be made lead me to believe that the best of
the explanations are reasonable but can be completely off the mark.

"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote in message 
news:13o7m6p78hhsn4b@corp.supernews.com...
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Some religious traditions, such as mine, hold that there is neither good nor 
evil. The universe is amoral. Events occur and the Universe doesn't care; it 
just keeps on obeying the Laws of Physics and making and destroying stars...

What we perceive is what affects us, and it is our considerations about it 
that define good or evil. (Or things in between).

As someone much wiser than I once wrote: "Why, then, 'tis none to you; for 
there is nothing
either good or bad, but thinking makes it so: to me it is a prison."

Pete.
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>god to be found in the Old Testament as cf the New Testament.

I do not see "inconsistencies" there. I see "further and more detailed
revelation" there. The Bible itself calls the sacrifice of Jesus the
"New Covenant". It could hardly be new if it were identical in every
respect, now could it? The Bible declares "Here is a New Thing", and
you find fault because it is not identical to the "Old Thing". Pardon
me, but such "arguments" border on blind and stupid. :-(

Based on our discussions, Alistair, and correct me if I'm wrong, but my
impression is that you haven't spent that much time studying the Bible,
and certainly not with an open mind. What would you say if someone who
had studied say, calculus, as much as you have studied the Bible, with
a similar attitude toward it, then concluded "calculus is full of
contradictions and inconsistencies, and obviously wrong." I suspect you
would put about as much weight to their statement as I do yours here,
and rightfully so. ;-)

You constantly misquote and misrepresent what I post here, making it
clear to anyone who cares to check, that your attitude is far removed
from an open, unbiased mind. E.g. you kept insisting on bringing up
Christianity and the Bible in the GW threads, when I wasn't basing any
of my arguments on those things. That is "cheap shot" strategy, hardly
a strategy one should take who is confident in posessing good evidence
and logic to support their position. :-)
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> you have written on the matter is poroof of that assertion.

Three points:

1) I know it's hard to tell from there, but I'm not GWB. ;-)

2) I have never used any argument from the Bible or Christianity or any
related source against GW. All my objections, therefore all my arguments,
against GW are based on the science, or lack thereof, logic and common
sense.

3) You cheerfully ignore the fact that I already said to you in an email
that, not only do I *not* object to GW on a Biblical basis, but that I can
see how GW, or something like it, might be construed from the Bible. (See
quote below from my email to you of Nov. 11, 2007.)
----------
This issue has nothing to do with religion, as far as I'm concerned, and I'm
puzzled that you keep bringing it up. In my opinion, God might very well let
mankind "stew in his juices" so to speak, if in God's judgment mankind was
sinning by being poor stewards of the earth. I don't have a problem with
that. :-) There is even Scripture to suggest that at some point God is going
to do just that, at least in some regions (Revelation 16:8,9).
----------

So, no matter what GWB or anyone else on the planet does or has done, you
have *absolutely no* reason to suppose (let alone berate and accuse), that
*I* am arguing against GW because of any religeous reason. You have, in
fact, quite a bit of evidence to the contrary.

If you have facts, bring them on. If you have an argument, make it. If you
don't, then don't. But please stop haranguing me for a non existent issue.
It's demonstrably false and intellectually dishonest. Thanks. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-08T13:47:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brQgj.43706$Mu4.33211@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com> <19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8ol5o39rpdcjq6es2n32j8bbt6gltibkee@4ax.com> <5ug8jsF1hf0vaU1@mid.individual.net> <KeLgj.43551$Mu4.35745@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <UyOgj.4424$M24.1169@newsfe17.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> your argument, not mine.

Not based on my incipient thoughts above, but there is what I believe to be
a clear and logical way for "evil" to come about, even with a wholly "good"
Creator. Assume for this discussion that the Bible is correct, and consider:

Either created intelligent beings (e.g. angels & humans) are deterministic
or they are to some degree stochastic. If they are deterministic, then an
all-knowing and infinitely intelligent God can determine from their creation
what any being will do. If this is the case, then there is no true free will,
and no sin, because a being who sinned would simply be doing what it had to
do in that situation, from a deterministic standpoint. There would be no
point in chastising or holding such a being accountable for "bad" behavior,
because that is meaningless for a deterministic being. Therefore, it is most
logical to assume that created intelligent beings are somewhat stochastic,
and not deterministic, if the Bible is correct.

Further, if God created intelligent beings for fellowship, then (at least
from a human perspective) this would seem to be a more satisfactory situation
than fellowship with a bunch of automatons, even if they are intelligent.
There's a wonderful richness in not being entirely certain what one's
companion might do. I believe this characteristic was intentionally designed
into human male/female psyches for just this reason. The Bible is clear that
the husband/wife relationship is analogous to the Christ/Church or
Christ/Christian (i.e. God/human) relationship.

Okay, free choice and accountability now make sense. A somewhat stochastic
intelligent being is capable of making real choices, even choices that are
not possible to (from a "predictive calculation standpoint") anticipate,
perhaps even doing things its creator did not want and might not anticipate
(see my "corollaries" comment below). This opens the door for sin to come
about without God creating it. As far as I can see, it's very logical and
consistent with the Bible. It's certainly consistent with observed human
behavior. :-)

There are some interesting corollaries to this chain of thought, concerning
whether God always knows *everything*, and the nature of time and eternity.
If anyone is interested, I'll post them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-09T07:04:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13o9hivfs712a8f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com> <19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8ol5o39rpdcjq6es2n32j8bbt6gltibkee@4ax.com> <5ug8jsF1hf0vaU1@mid.individual.net> <KeLgj.43551$Mu4.35745@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <UyOgj.4424$M24.1169@newsfe17.lga> <brQgj.43706$Mu4.33211@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13o8ct31b3skr0a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
>>
>
> Then how do you explain the prophecies in the Bible that predict
> future events?
>

That's easy.

To the Jews who wrote the Hebrew scriptures, "prophet" had - and has - a 
distinctly different meaning than was later attached.  A synonym for 
"prophet" in the Jewish tradtion is "one who threatens." A Jewish Prophet is 
one who predicts, not guarantees, and all prophecies are conditional.

A good example is the story of Jonah. Jonah, as a prophet, told the people 
to straighten up or God would smite them. Whether the people feared God, or 
were somewhat impressed by a bleached, wrinkled, man walking out of the 
mouth of a beached whale yelling "REPENT!," they got their shit together and 
were not destroyed.

To the Christian, a prophet is one who can see the future. Interestingly, 
most religious predictions lack a date certain, so they can never be 
definitively proved.

In many cases, two religions (here Christians and Jews) can use the same 
word (prophet) and be talking about completely separate things.
```

"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:
> Charles Hottel wrote:
>>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> things.

Actually, I think Charles was referring to the mechanism, rather than
the definition.

But the meaning of "prophet" is neither of the above. From Harper's Bible
Dictionary:

    prophet (Heb. nabi�, Gk. prophe�te�s), a person who serves as a
    channel of communication between the human and divine worlds.

Put another way, a "prophet" is someone who speaks for God. Because
the people the prophets were speaking to were often doing bad things,
prophecy was often "straighten up or else." But not all prophecy is
about the future. Often it is information or instruction. And it is
often positive, not negative. There are a large number of passages
in the Old Testament, for example, about coming blessings, such as
the Messiah and eventual salvation of the righteous.

In New Testament Christianity, there are three forms of "prophecy."
There is the office of prophet, one who is specifically called to
that ministry (Ephesians 4) by God. There is the motivational gift
of prophecy, which might be thought of as a personality profile
(Romans 12). And there is the charasmatic gift of prophecy, the
actual utterance of a prophetic "word", which any Christian might
experience (1 Corinthians 12).
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> Judaism also put to death a number of prophets. The Old Testament
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> lies much better than the truth. Still clearly true today. :-)

Uh, no. God said specifically the REVERSE, put 'em to death EVEN IF what 
they say comes true.

"If there appears among you a prophet...and he gives you a sign or a 
portent... even if that sign or portent that he named... comes true... As 
for that prophet... he shall be put to death;... (Deut 13:2 et seq).

It's not what HAPPENS that determines the truth, it's what is SAID that's 
dispositive. God doesn't expect us to tell the difference between miracles 
and illusion. If miracles were the test of divinity, we'd all be worshiping 
in the House of Siegfreud & Roy.
```

```
"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> & Roy.


Wow! By such selective quotation one could "prove" that anyone said anything!
This has nothing to do with a "test of divinity", because divinity isn't the
issue. A person being a true prophet of God is the issue, the criteria I
related was that if any prophecy ever given by a prophet failed, they be put
to death as false. What you selectively quoted above is *not* a contradiction
to that, but another thing altogether. Examine the whole passage:

 Deuteronomy 13:1-5:
 1"If there arises among you a prophet or a dreamer of dreams, and he gives
 you a sign or a wonder, 2"and the sign or the wonder comes to pass, of
 which he spoke to you, saying, 'Let us go after other gods'-which you have
 not known-'and let us serve them,' 3"you shall not listen to the words of
 that prophet or that dreamer of dreams, for the Lord your God is testing
 you to know whether you love the Lord your God with all your heart and with
 all your soul. 4"You shall walk after the Lord your God and fear Him, and
 keep His commandments and obey His voice, and you shall serve Him and hold
 fast to Him. 5"But that prophet or that dreamer of dreams shall be put to
 death, because he has spoken in order to turn you away from the Lord your
 God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt and redeemed you from the
 house of bondage, to entice you from the way in which the Lord your God
 commanded you to walk. So you shall put away the evil from your midst.

This passage is saying that if someone comes along and claims to be a
prophet, but tells you not to follow God, then he is a false prophet, put
him to death, regardless of what appearances are. It would be an oxymoron
if God instructed people to believe a "prophet" who told them not to follow
God. This passage simply clarifies that. This has nothing whatever to do
with putting them to death if they prophesy something that doesn't come
true; it's two entirely different issues. Anyone could claim something will
happen, then it could happen by accident or trickery. Charlatans do that
all the time today. But no charlatan is going to *always* be right, even if
they're saying things that appear to agree with God. That is why God said
they were false if they were *ever* wrong, when claiming to speak for God.
Prophets weren't required to be infallible in their everyday affairs, only
when they claimed to speak for God.
```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

>What you selectively quoted above is *not* a contradiction
> to that, but another thing altogether. Examine the whole passage:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> all your soul.

This Creator of the Universe sounds pretty insecure to me... If He wants 
people to love Him so much, why isn't He gentler with them? Nobody likes a 
bully...

4"You shall walk after the Lord your God and fear Him, and
> keep His commandments and obey His voice, and you shall serve Him and hold
> fast to Him.

Oh sure, a lifetime of "love" based on fear, subservience, dependency, and

The miracle to me is not that Church attendences are declining, but that 
ANYBODY buys this nonsense...

 5"But that prophet or that dreamer of dreams shall be put to
> death,

Yep, no messing with Jehovah... gotta love Him.

>because he has spoken in order to turn you away from the Lord your

The Supreme Being needs to remind us how beholden we are to Him...

>to entice you from the way in which the Lord your God

>
> This passage is saying that if someone comes along and claims to be a
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> when they claimed to speak for God.

Speaking for myself (NOT for God...) I predict that it will be a hard row to 
hoe getting educated, intelligent people to subscribe to this transparent, 
simplistic, and barbaric philosophy. Sure, some will. Others will pretend 
to, but by far the majority simply will not...

FOOTNOTE: Normally, out of simple respect, I would refrain from posting the 
above. But if someone thinks it is OK to quote long passages of what I 
consider at best to be a folk tale, as if it was irrefutable evidence for 
their persecution of "false prophets", well, it gets what it deserves.

Here's the deal:

Quit proselytizing your particular religious beliefs here (and especially 
extensive passages of your religious texts), and I'll quit pouring scorn on 
it.

This is a Usenet forum, not Sunday School.

Pete.

(or lack of it).

"putting to death" (or otherwise maltreatment in the name of Religion) of

Pete.

- **Date:** 2008-01-11T19:32:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:SsidnY-Wy9todhvanZ2dnUVZ_qHinZ2d@comcast.com...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> church.

How else could Judson have responded to Jerry's hack job?  It's a fact 
that, literarily or literally, the words recorded in Deuteronomy do not 
say what Jerry selectively quoted - God did not say to kill *all* 
prophets.  (Knowing Jerry, I don't think there was malice, just humour. 
  But, still, in what way could Judson have corrected that without 
arousing your disdain?)  If the answer was "he can't," then that's no 
good - you can be ridiculed, but not defend your position?

> This is a free forum and it is great to see all topics being discussed here 
> (even taboo ones like Sex, Religion and Politics), but it is NOT a 
> springboard for Evangelism or proselytizing a particular religious belief 
> (or lack of it).

I just thought it was odd that you picked *that* one to jump all over. 
I thought it was one of the least-proselytizing posts in the whole thread.

It's certainly your right to post whatever reply you wanted - I just 
know that, in the past, you've made posts that you regretted.  Now I 
know that this wasn't one of them.  No harm, no foul...
```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:9pqdnaMudZo-uxXanZ2dnUVZ_o-mnZ2d@comcast.com...
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

Hmmm... no response.  I'll spell it out for you: It is becoming tiresome.

>> I object to long quotes from scripture (anybody's... I'd be pissed off to 
>> see the Koran extensively quoted here, or Bhagavad Gita...) when the 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> How else could Judson have responded to Jerry's hack job?

He could have quoted references rather than text advocating the execution of 
people who are not of the Faith, and there was no need for the instructional 
tone. I don't mnd hearing someone's personal opinion but not the Party Line 
and I won't be preached at.

Furthermore, while I have good tolerance for the religious beliefs of 
others, that tolerance stops when they advocate the putting to death of 
people or the torturing or stoning of them, for anything that is NOT a 
criminal act.(And even then, I would be wary about it....) We have enough 
individuals waiting for the chance to be "Thought Police".

If your religion says you must harm people for what they think or believe, 
then I suggest you keep that part of it to yourself.

I see NO DIFFERENCE between true and false prophets. (They are both nut 
cases and harmless.)

Waving Holy Writ around in support of the violence required by this all 
merciful, loving , supreme being... it just made me spit the dummy.


>It's a fact that, literarily or literally, the words recorded in 
>Deuteronomy do not say what Jerry selectively quoted - God did not say to 
>kill *all* prophets.  (Knowing Jerry, I don't think there was malice, just 
>humour. But, still, in what way could Judson have corrected that without 
>arousing your disdain?)

He could have registered his disagreement, the reason for it and a 
reference.

Instead he chose to get into a pulpit.


> If the answer was "he can't," then that's no good - you can be ridiculed, 
> but not defend your position?

The answer was not, and never would be from me, "he can't", so the predicate 
is not applicable.

My issue is having Scripture that advocates violence against "unbelievers" 
waved in my face and presented as being an ultimate truth, when it is at 
best arguable, at worst anachronistic nonsense.

>
>> This is a free forum and it is great to see all topics being discussed 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> thought it was one of the least-proselytizing posts in the whole thread.

Just shows our different viewpoints, doesn't it? :-)

>
> It's certainly your right to post whatever reply you wanted -

Thank you. I'll continue to do so.

>I just know that, in the past, you've made posts that you regretted.  Now I 
>know that this wasn't one of them.  No harm, no foul...

I can think of maybe two posts in over 8000 across 11 years that I have 
regretted. I apologised unreservedly for them at the time, and do not now 
regret them.

This was not one of those and you seem to have edited some of the reasons I 
gave as to why it aroused my scorn.(I repeated them above)

I'm not sorry, and therefore can offer no apology.

Any further such posts will get the same treatment, if I see them.

Pete.
```

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:9pqdnaMudZo-uxXanZ2dnUVZ_o-mnZ2d@comcast.com...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hmmm... no response.  I'll spell it out for you: It is becoming tiresome.

But it's not necessarily directed at you.  (Well, unless you are a false 
prophet in 3,000 B.C. - heh...)

>>> I object to long quotes from scripture (anybody's... I'd be pissed off to 
>>> see the Koran extensively quoted here, or Bhagavad Gita...) when the 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> and I won't be preached at.

As he put not quite so nicely, that was a historical text, in no way 
indicative of the beliefs of *any* (that I know) mainstream or fringe 
Evangelical organization today.  Throughout the Bible (which you know, 
as you've read it through), God changed His techniques with different 
people.

>>> This is a free forum and it is great to see all topics being discussed 
>>> here (even taboo ones like Sex, Religion and Politics), but it is NOT a 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just shows our different viewpoints, doesn't it? :-)

Heh - well, knowing the context, maybe that helps it not be so 
distasteful to you.  Maybe not.

> This was not one of those and you seem to have edited some of the reasons I 
> gave as to why it aroused my scorn.(I repeated them above)

My apologies if that changed the history - I was just trying to avoid 5 
pages of quotes with one or two lines of response.  :)  I didn't mean to 
prolong it, and I really don't have a horse in this race.  Knowing the 
context, and the history, I knew that Judson wasn't advocating doing 
that today, and so your ire seemed misplaced.  It obviously wasn't.

When you think about it, there are *lots* of things in the Old Testament 
that wouldn't make sense today - I doubt that many armies would silently 
march around a city for 7 days if they wanted to destroy it.  :)  But, 
at one time, those were God's orders.  Would a woman who lost her 
husband go live with her mother-in-law?  Rarely - but that's what God 
led Ruth to do.
```

> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

There is nothing wrong with debating your personal beliefs here; it is 
healthey for all concerned.

>
>> This is a Usenet forum, not Sunday School.
>
> Fine, then it shouldn't be a "bash Christianity" or "push atheism" forum,
> either.

>I'm quite happy to leave the subject alone. But I won't sit by and

beliefs here (irrespective of whether they are religious or not :-)) they 
are likely to be attacked...

> keep pushing atheism, then act all offended when someone takes issue.

I don't "push" atheism and I couldn't care less whether anyone here takes 
issue with it. It is a personal belief I happen to hold.

I'm not arrogant enough to think I should convert the rest of the world to 
it. I respect their right to be wrong :-).

What offends me is when Scripture is quoted in defence of torturing and 
killing people, as a justification for it. And Evangelism of any kind 
(including atheism) is quite out of place here.

Please see my response to Daniel.

Pete.

>How about when US patriotism is used in defense of torturing and killing people in
>Guantanamo Bay? Romney said he wanted the prison size doubled, which got cheers and

>In this case The People are immoral, and it has nothing to do with religion. 

Absolutely.   I keep seeing arguments about how bad people are who
have this religion or that one or no religion.   But I look around and
see people of all categories with members doing good and with members
doing evil.     Sometimes religion or patriotism or some other -ism is
used as an excuse, but in the real world we see the behaviors
nevertheless.

patriot does evil or good doesn't mean that his whole group is evil or
good.

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> Absolutely.   I keep seeing arguments about how bad people are who
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> good.

The point of Christianity *is not* that Christians are good, but that they
have been forgiven. Christians are expected to endeavor to be good, but
the Bible makes it clear that Christians will sin on occasion (e.g the book
of Romans). It is faith in Jesus as Savior, not good conduct, that makes
one a Christian, and forgiven.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-15T10:31:08+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<13o9hivfs712a8f@corp.supernews.com> <L06hj.44163$Mu4.29579@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <17d66e6b-41d9-467f-ae4c-1bc9629490f8@e10g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <AMahj.69384$L%6.54404@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <13ocasvqbc8ak39@corp.supernews.com> <3Fqhj.30190$L27.1835@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5uns9tF1j30lfU1@mid.individual.net> <dqOhj.51297$N67.44049@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <5uqg38F1jhl04U1@mid.individual.net> <378go3tnhko793u45ebi4snptotcr90tik@4ax.com> <d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
Only the guilty need forgiveness, and even then, there are much better 
solutions than "forgiving" them. Let them take responsibility for what they 
did and make it as right as it is possible to make it... for example. Let 
them show by action they are truly contrite and have worked to make amends. 
Reparation and genuine apology to victims would be a fundamental part of 
this.

Much better than "forgiveness" which is a supercilious concept, implying 
superiority of one party over another.

As mentioned elsewhere in this forum a little while back, the major personal 
problem I have with Christianity (and I'm offering this in a spirit of 
debate, not trolling...) is that I never asked or wanted Jesus to die for my 
sins. The horriffic things the Romans did to this man I wouldn't want on my 
account.

Mainly for that reason, if I HAD to have some belief system (other than the 
one I DO have), it would be Buddhism.

Gautama Buddha never claimed to be God, never offered forgiveness, simply 
said: "There is suffering in the world. The suffering can be stopped if you 
do these 8 simple things."

And his last words to his disciple Ananda were:"Work out your OWN salvation, 
with diligence." (My emphasis).

I have never had a problem with the teachings of Christ (which are sound 
common sense, just like many other teachers who lived within 800 years of 
him, before and after); I have a problem with the "Organisation", 
Christianity. Founded by Paul (Saul of Tarsus), who was a martinet and 
probably mentally ill, with his own stamp on it... fire and brimstone and 
the promise of eternal torture if the line is not toed... It worked for a 
very long time; now people are waking up. (You can blame education and 
better "tools" for thinking...)

I know there are millions of quiet, decent, Christian people who find 
inspiration through their faith, just as there are millions of Buddhists and 
other followers of great teachers. I wish them all well. But I would much 
rather see all of them standing on their own two feet and dealing with life 
on this planet, rather than relying on the glory to come in the next life 
somewhere else...

Pete.
```

"tim Josling" <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au> wrote in message 
news:13op382d4rbkb22@corp.supernews.com...
> On Tue, 15 Jan 2008 10:31:08 +1300, Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tim Josling

I am familiar with the teachings of Lao Tze and Daoism.

At the current stage of my life I'm not looking any more (3 decades is 
enough to be puzzled... :-))

Right now, although I don't have 100% answers for some of the questions I 
originally set out to solve, I have workable probabilities that are enough 
for me.

Here's a summary :-):

Who am I?  = > 90% (Philosophy and Behavioural studies are inexact 
sciences... :-))
What am I? = 100% (Natural History provides very good answers.)
Where am I? = 100% for location (Astronomy), but there is still a lot of 
information on the nature of the Universe that is yet to be processed and is 
still coming in...
How am I? = >90% (Health checks are encouraging... :-))
When am I? = 80% (evidence about the nature of time is still coming in...)
Why am I? = 75% (There may actually be no reason at all...Randomity could 
account for it. :-))

Pete.
```

>problem I have with Christianity (and I'm offering this in a spirit of

minority of His other children to avoid being tortured forever - as
long as they believe in this odd mechanism.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 41)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-15T12:14:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h41qo3h290f2ku9d7p773m6iccekot5cfo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5uns9tF1j30lfU1@mid.individual.net> <dqOhj.51297$N67.44049@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <5uqg38F1jhl04U1@mid.individual.net> <378go3tnhko793u45ebi4snptotcr90tik@4ax.com> <d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net> <8olpo35moau9aeif47tqgagkkhlr1c3dqu@4ax.com> <Zq5jj.44404$L27.42997@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <130qo35pnv8oghgbsgh8fvrjecbg4ddg77@4ax.com> <yn7jj.44455$L27.43996@bignews9.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Tue, 15 Jan 2008 12:58:43 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>It just strikes me as incredible hubris for a human to define what morality
>"should be" for deity. Especially considering what a terrible job humans
>have been doing in that area down through history.

If it's moral for deities to allow the vast majority of His children
to be tortured beyond all understanding forever and ever - then that's
a morality I will never strive to emulate.

Morality for people is easier to define and talk about - using a human
language.    Doing good is moral.   Causing or allowing pain for no
lasting benefit is bad.   That's human morality, and I'm human.

My post was also talking about logic - the mechanism that required an
omnipotent being to allow humans to torture His son so that some
people can avoid a much worse torture.

So you want me to consider the terrible job humans have done about
morality - by whose standards?   By the standards that accept
everlasting torture as being right and proper?  

Accepting the premise that we have screwed up does not mean I will
accept those standards.
```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> a morality I will never strive to emulate.

I believe you. But that has nothing whatever to do with divine morality.
The vast majority of His children have chosen their own way instead of
God's way. Your comments here are a prime example of that, yet from
from the same person comes indictment against God.

> Morality for people is easier to define and talk about - using a human
> language.    Doing good is moral.   Causing or allowing pain for no
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> people can avoid a much worse torture.

And what uniquely qualifies you to make such determinations? Do you
also approve natural law? What do you do about the ones you don't
particularly like?

> So you want me to consider the terrible job humans have done about
> morality - by whose standards?

Do you know of a standard for which human morality is good?

>  By the standards that accept
> everlasting torture as being right and proper?

Perhaps an everlating Holy God who created us in the first place is
in a better position to determine that.

> Accepting the premise that we have screwed up does not mean I will
> accept those standards.

It means you have lived your total existence in a debased, immoral
world, and wouldn't know holiness if it bit you in the armpit. If the
Bible is correct, then God is pure and holy in a way that we can't
begin to understand. Would you have the criminals set the rules for
the judicial system? You wouldn't do that, even at a human level,
yet you are recommending that for God. No doubt the criminals in
our judicial system would feel the same way. :-)
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

- **References:** `<378go3tnhko793u45ebi4snptotcr90tik@4ax.com> <d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net> <8olpo35moau9aeif47tqgagkkhlr1c3dqu@4ax.com> <Zq5jj.44404$L27.42997@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <130qo35pnv8oghgbsgh8fvrjecbg4ddg77@4ax.com> <yn7jj.44455$L27.43996@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <h41qo3h290f2ku9d7p773m6iccekot5cfo@4ax.com> <F58jj.44473$L27.14435@bignews9.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Tue, 15 Jan 2008 13:47:54 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>It means you have lived your total existence in a debased, immoral
>world, and wouldn't know holiness if it bit you in the armpit. If the
>Bible is correct, then God is pure and holy in a way that we can't
>begin to understand.

I'd rather be good by my standards than evil by my standards.   Those
are the only standards I have.    You make the claim that God has
different standards and therefore mine are wrong.   

Trouble is, you won't convince me to believe in and love God by
telling me that my belief that torture is bad doesn't apply due to my
failure to recognize Holy standards.     

Maybe your standards are to allow your children to be tortured for no
benefit when you can do something about it.   If that makes you holier
than me, go ahead feel smug in your superiority.     Anticipate
looking down from Heaven at our wailing, and expect that some of your
loved ones will be there with us.   

>Would you have the criminals set the rules for
>the judicial system? You wouldn't do that, even at a human level,
>yet you are recommending that for God. No doubt the criminals in
>our judicial system would feel the same way. :-)

If we all are criminals (sinners), then we all benefit by
understanding and accepting the rules.    Not just those who happen to
be brought up in the Right families.
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:F58jj.44473$L27.14435@bignews9.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> world, and wouldn't know holiness if it bit you in the armpit.

I don't consider the world I live in to be debased and immoral. Certainly it 
contains a very few such individuals, but there is nobilty, decency, and 
kindness as well.

Maybe one's view of God is coloured by one's view of the World.


>If the
> Bible is correct, then God is pure and holy in a way that we can't
> begin to understand.

It isn't, so the predicate doesn't follow.


>Would you have the criminals set the rules for
> the judicial system?

If there were no criminals we wouldn't NEED a judicial system. If every one 
of us was responsible and considerate, we wouldn't need to have laws; people 
would just do "the right thing" by everybody else.

So, why didn't God create us perfect?

Seems like He screwed up and we have been carrying that guilt for ever.

Or else He INTENDED strife and conflict between good and evil and we are 
just puppets playing out a cosmic morality play for His amusement.

Doesn't matter how you slice it, there is no way I can see that would make 
me want to be part of what you currently perceive as God.

Pete.
```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> So, why didn't God create us perfect?

He did, but we chose to sin. Pete, has anyone ever forced you to lie? Yet
I would bet my life that you have. Is that from your perfections, or from
your flaws? Every time you ever lied, or did any other bad thing, you had
a choice. You did (like me and everyone else) what you did because you
*chose* to do so. God gave us free will; it is we who choose to sin.
Don't blame God for that; blame us. The real question is why God didn't
blast us all into oblivion a long time ago.
```

news:wlajj.127$1f.26@bignews9.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
>> So, why didn't God create us perfect?
>
> He did, but we chose to sin.

If we were capable of sin then we weren't perfect, so He didn't create us 
that way...


>Pete, has anyone ever forced you to lie?

No. Not that I can recall. In fact, people have a pretty hard time forcing 
me to do anything :-)

I wasn't bullied at school (the bullies went for easier targets), never 
bullied other kids (or had any desire to do so), and on several occasions 
prevented or stopped bullying that was going on.

On the rare occasions when I have lied, it has been in full knowledge of 
what I was doing and why I was doing it.

I am not easily coerced.:-)


>Yet
> I would bet my life that you have. Is that from your perfections, or from
> your flaws?

You'd certainly win the bet. However, it is not necessarily in self interest 
that I have done this. I don't see it as coming from flaws OR perfections... 
Humans are complex creatures.


>Every time you ever lied, or did any other bad thing, you had
> a choice.

That's a sweeping statement. Sometimes there was no other acceptable choice. 
As for doing "bad" things, I learned long ago that this is non-survival 
behaviour (there are always consequences) and best avoided. I derive no 
pleasure out of being bad, so I stopped it. It is just as easy to be "good" 
as to be "bad" and being "good" makes the world a better place. Simple 
common sense really...


>You did (like me and everyone else) what you did because you
> *chose* to do so.

Please don't project your character onto me. We are not alike.


>God gave us free will; it is we who choose to sin.

And yet the Church teaches that even a new-born baby is born in sin, as a 
consequence of what Adam and Eve did in disobeying God's command.

I don't buy it.

I believe people are basically good. I further believe we get fucked up by 
the interactions we experience in living and growing. How we deal with those 
experiences determines whether we can sleep easily or not, and whether we 
enjoy our time in this place. I try to learn from bad stuff and put it 
behind me; I don't blame Satan or God for what happens to me. I believe all 
of us are responsible for the condition we are in; the sooner we start 
taking that responsibility for ourselves, the sooner our lives can be 
restored, rehabilitated, and continued pleasurably and happily.


> Don't blame God for that; blame us.

In work and in life, I try not to be judgemental and I don't "blame" 
anybody.  Shit happens. We deal with it and learn from it. Blame and 
punishment are equally counter productive, in my view. Someone screws up on 
my watch, they take responsibility for that screw-up and, as a team we 
assist them to make it right. Nothing is achieved by blame, except 
undermining of confidence and consequent demotivation.

Because I hold the above belief, I am immediately not predisposed to any 
religion that pivots on guilt and blame.


>The real question is why God didn't
> blast us all into oblivion a long time ago.

Where's the fun in that? :-)

Pete.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> If we were capable of sin then we weren't perfect, so He didn't create us that way...

Capable means free, not flawed. How could God create a being with
completely free will who *could not* sin? If they *could not* sin,
they wouldn't have true free will. When you open the door to freedom,
you open the door; it's as simple as that.

>>Yet
>> I would bet my life that you have. Is that from your perfections, or from
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> flaws OR perfections... Humans are complex creatures.

That's rationalization. Deceipt is deceipt.

>>Every time you ever lied, or did any other bad thing, you had
>> a choice.
>
> That's a sweeping statement. Sometimes there was no other acceptable choice.

Ah! Acceptable to you. I.e. you chose what you preferred, even at the
cost of deceipt. A murderer chooses to kill because it is their preference.
Both break the "rules" of "good conduct" because it is their "acceptable"
choice. It is only a difference in magnitude, not kind.

>>You did (like me and everyone else) what you did because you
>> *chose* to do so.
>
> Please don't project your character onto me. We are not alike.

I didn't say that. I said that you make your choices, and everybody else
makes theirs, me included. In that, we're all alike; not in the particular
choices we individually make.

>>God gave us free will; it is we who choose to sin.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't buy it.

There's that free will again. :-)
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:heajj.105$1f.42@bignews9.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

And they are just as nopble and just as decent as they ever were.

Evil?

If someone believes that he is doing good by strapping dynamite to himself 
and exploding it in a crowded marketplace, in the certain knowledge that his 
subsistence family will receive vast wealth ($US20,000), and he himself will 
be rewarded with 70 virgins in Paradise, how is that evil?

It is not the person who is evil. It is the indoctrinated belief system, and 
the economic circumstances in which he lives, which gave him that viewpoint. 
That is what is "evil"..



>Have the events of the last 100
> years (or 10 years) not made that clear? People today are commining 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> never met.

I don't dispute that. I dispute whether it is evil or not, because this 
depends on your viewpoint. It is certainly misguided.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 44)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-15T18:21:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<13ocasvqbc8ak39@corp.supernews.com> <3Fqhj.30190$L27.1835@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5uns9tF1j30lfU1@mid.individual.net> <dqOhj.51297$N67.44049@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <5uqg38F1jhl04U1@mid.individual.net> <378go3tnhko793u45ebi4snptotcr90tik@4ax.com> <d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net> <8olpo35moau9aeif47tqgagkkhlr1c3dqu@4ax.com> <Zq5jj.44404$L27.42997@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <130qo35pnv8oghgbsgh8fvrjecbg4ddg77@4ax.com> <yn7jj.44455$L27.43996@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4or9F1kopo3U1@mid.individual.net> <heajj.105$1f.42@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4sfhF1kcru0U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> And they are just as nopble and just as decent as they ever were.

True, which doesn't say much.

> Evil?
>
> If someone believes that he is doing good by strapping dynamite to himself and exploding it in a crowded marketplace, in the 
> certain knowledge that his subsistence family will receive vast wealth ($US20,000), and he himself will be rewarded with 70 
> virgins in Paradise, how is that evil?

He does not have the right to the lives of those other people; they do not
belong to him to do with them as he wishes.

> It is not the person who is evil. It is the indoctrinated belief system, and the economic circumstances in which he lives, which 
> gave him that viewpoint. That is what is "evil".

People choose. There are examples of children being reared to the
highest standards of behavior who rejected it and did very bad
things. There are also examples of the reverse; of people being reared
by brutes, who overcame that to be model citizens. Training and
environment are only influences; though they can be very powerful,
they are not deterministic. Anyone who has reared children knows
this. You can't make your children come out well, you can only be
as positive as you can. You don't have to teach children to lie or
to be selfish; they do that naturally. You have to teach them *not* to
do those things, and it's an uphill struggle.

The bottom line is, people choose their way. It's all about free will,
and what the human heart chooses to do, and the human heart has a
very strong propensity toward bad, not good. Even "good" people
struggle against their nature (and that includes Christians). Few
people have never wanted to punch someone out, even if they've
never done it. The human heart is by nature selfish, prideful and
deceitful. Humans have to struggle to achieve true generosity, humility
and honesty. That's why so relatively few get there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> and honesty. That's why so relatively few get there.

Speak for yourself.

I believe the majority "get there".

I see examples of kindness, goodness, decency, nobility and even heroism and 
self-sacrifice every day in ordinary familes, with ordinary people, living 
ordinary lives.

In my travels I have never ceased to be amazed by the kindness of strangers 
and I have recirocated it to the best of my ability.

I am not blinded to the evil in the world, but I don't wallow in it either.

We obviously live in different worlds.

You say God created us perfect, but gave us free will, so we are not, but 
that doesn't alter the fact that we were created perfect, and it is only our 
innate badness that makes us choose to be bad and so need the salvation of 
God, but God knew when He created us what the outcome would be because He's 
omniscient, but he can't be because he doesn't know what choice we'll make 
with the free will which He gave us... and so on ad nauseum.

It is a totally incoherent argument that defies all rules of logic.

It's like the old Russian riddle: "Is God so powerful He could create a 
stone that even HE cannot move?"

Pete.
```

> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>

that wants its own way, even at the price of others. Your grandchildren
are the same, as is every other human being on the planet. We *learn*
to do better; it's not part of our natures to do so on our own. Some
children become good or bad more easily than others. That is a trait
that varies with the individual human heart, though it's present in all
of them.

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-21T15:18:59+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net> <8olpo35moau9aeif47tqgagkkhlr1c3dqu@4ax.com> <Zq5jj.44404$L27.42997@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <130qo35pnv8oghgbsgh8fvrjecbg4ddg77@4ax.com> <yn7jj.44455$L27.43996@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4or9F1kopo3U1@mid.individual.net> <heajj.105$1f.42@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4sfhF1kcru0U1@mid.individual.net> <Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com>`

```


"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com...
> Howard Brazee wrote:
>> On Tue, 15 Jan 2008 18:21:17 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> of that?

I think that's kind of dirty pool, Daniel.

Kids don't necessarily do these things because of an innately sinful or 
wicked nature. It is part of a growth process where limits must be explored. 
Do you consider your kids to be wicked and liable to burn in Hell because 
they occasionally try and buy you off with a lie, or hit a sibling when 
fighting over toys, etc.?

Of course you don't. And they are not.

This whole doctrine of "Original Sin" and all of us being born in it is long 
overdue for review.

I can't see sin in a newborn baby; I see a blank page waiting to be written.

Parents have much to do with what will be written there, and if the parents' 
outlook is negative, how can you expect the children to be otherwise?

Sin (like God) is an invention of man.

As if that weren't bad enough, are we now to saddle our children with it 
from the moment they draw breath?

I don't think so.

Pete.
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net> <8olpo35moau9aeif47tqgagkkhlr1c3dqu@4ax.com> <Zq5jj.44404$L27.42997@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <130qo35pnv8oghgbsgh8fvrjecbg4ddg77@4ax.com> <yn7jj.44455$L27.43996@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4or9F1kopo3U1@mid.individual.net> <heajj.105$1f.42@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4sfhF1kcru0U1@mid.individual.net> <Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> overdue for review.

But, for those of us that believe it, it certainly explains the behavior 
described above.  Pushing limits is one thing, but lying and direct 
disobedience is quite another.

> I can't see sin in a newborn baby; I see a blank page waiting to be written.
> 
> Parents have much to do with what will be written there, and if the parents' 
> outlook is negative, how can you expect the children to be otherwise?

It's not negative at all.  How can I fault my child for being born with 
the same sinful nature that his mother and I were also born with?  :) 
It's just something we have to deal with.  We train their behavior until 
they grow old enough for us to also train their hearts.  Hopefully, by 
the time the heart is trained, we don't have to be training anymore - we 
give guidance on how to view situations based on principle, and let them 
make decisions.  If we feel that it's the wrong one, we'll explain to 
them why we feel they could have made a better one, and they learn.

There's also a concept in Scripture called the "age of accountability." 
  It's not a magic number, per se, but it's the age at which they have 
matured enough to understand the concept that they are sinners, that 
Jesus has paid the debt for that sin, and that they can accept this 
payment.  Babies that die aren't condemned to hell.
```

- **References:** `<d6tmo3h8shsn29ant62dsb5mv51ti06qc3@4ax.com> <d7Mij.50461$Mu4.2341@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5v22ktF1kchsdU1@mid.individual.net> <8olpo35moau9aeif47tqgagkkhlr1c3dqu@4ax.com> <Zq5jj.44404$L27.42997@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <130qo35pnv8oghgbsgh8fvrjecbg4ddg77@4ax.com> <yn7jj.44455$L27.43996@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4or9F1kopo3U1@mid.individual.net> <heajj.105$1f.42@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5v4sfhF1kcru0U1@mid.individual.net> <Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
> I was always under the impression that unbaptised babies go to hell.

The Bible clearly teaches that a person must be aware of right and
wrong, and to choose wrong, to have guilt. Babies are innocent
because they have not reached what is sometimes called "the age of
accountability" when they become aware of such things.

The reason to baptize babies is to come before the Church and publicly
commit them to God, not for salvation. It is what happens in a person's
own heart that condemns or excuses them, not what others may do for
you or to you. Other people can influence you, but they can't determine
your fate.
```

member of Christ and a temple of the Holy Spirit. By this very fact

- **References:** `<Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> Sorry I was not clear.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> broken relationship with God and that is what I call sin.

Uh, not exactly. The Ten Commandments came WAY after Adam & Eve. Besides 
which, there was no penalty for violating any of the Ten.

That's not to say there wern't commandments before Moses, there were, such 
as circumcising on the 8th day and not eating meat*. Jewish scholars say 
there are 613 direct commandments, 365 positive one ("thou shalt do") and 
248 negative ones ("thou shalt not").

As a test of Biblical knowledge, what was the first commandment God gave to 
mankind? Do you know? Answer below:

------
* Only Noah and his decendants were granted permission to eat meat. Think on 
it. Everybody up to the time of the flood was a vegetarian! And the world 
was so hoplessly corrupt, degenerate, and wicked that God had to destroy it 
and start over. There is some possibility that residual debauchery in the 
world comes from eating plants. After all, plants are not food; plants are 
what food eats.








--- Answer to quiz
Be fruitful and multiply.
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:apUlj.63805$N67.14101@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> obeying. :-)

Well, OF COURSE, we're gonna do the EASY ones... :-)

Story I heard was that a year after the ark landed on Mount Ararat, Noah was 
going around to make sure that all the animals had complied with God's 
commandment.

There were Mr. and Mrs. Lion with a fine brood of cubs, the dogs had pups, 
the birds had chicks, and everybody seemed to have been busy doing what God 
required.

Except the snake.

Noah asked him the reason he had ignored God's command.

Said the snake:"I'm an adder and I can't multiply."

Tenderly, Noah picked up Mr. and Mrs. Snake and carried then into his own 
log cabin, where he placed them on the kitchen table.

Some time later they produced several offspring and Noah went to 
congratulate the snake.

"I thought you were an adder and couldn't multiply....", said Noah.

To which the snake replied: "Even an adder can multiply if he has a log 
table...".

Boom! Boom!

Pete.
```

On Wed, 23 Jan 2008 21:11:27 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>* Only Noah and his decendants were granted permission to eat meat. Think on 
>it. Everybody up to the time of the flood was a vegetarian! And the world 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>what food eats.

It proves that carnivores are all in-bred. 

The great flood was probably the Permian-Triassic Extinction Event, 250 mya, which killed
off 96% of marine species and 70% of land animals. Animal development WAS in big trouble
all through the Permian era. 

>As a test of Biblical knowledge, what was the first commandment God gave to 
>mankind?
>Be fruitful and multiply. 

How fitting. That's the ESSENTIAL commandment, because the purpose of life is evolution.
Other commandments are nice-to-haves. God issued the first commandment to prokaryotes.
```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> commandments.

You said "e.g" (for example...) Does this imply you admit other rules?

>If Adam and Eve had followed all the rules we would all be happier today.

For me to agree with that, I'd have to believe that events described in 
Genesis are true and actual fact, and that there really WERE two individuals 
named Adam and Eve, created directly by God from dust and a rib bone. To 
believe that, I would have to accept that the Earth is less than 6000 years 
old (calculated from the genealogies in the Pentateuch). Obviously, it is 
impossible for me to do that, as the evidence refutes it. So here we must 
part company. (No hard feelings... :-))



>They did not and that resulted in a broken relationship with God and that 
>is what I call sin.

I understand.

Thanks for the clarification.

Pete.
```

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-24T08:43:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> before days, it must be a different definition than literalists claim.

You're right Howard, many people do that. Unfortunately, it doesn't
work. Genesis is too specific (evening and morning, etc.) of a "day"
being one rotation of the earth. There are also other problems with that
interpretation. But there are much deeper problems than that. If one
takes the Bible as simply a figurative account, they have destroyed any
benefit of the Bible. If it's not reliable, then who is to say what part
is reliable and what part isn't? The situation devolves to a simple human
relative value system, no more, no less. Worthless and pointless for
knowing God and obtaining eternal life. If Jesus was not Who He claimed
to be (the Messiah and Savior, the Son of God) then he was a liar or
insane; certainly no one that should be admired or followed. The logical
bottom line is that if the Bible isn't what it claims to be, the reliable
and true Word of God, then it is a lie, and what nitwit would stake their
faith and eternal destiny on a known lie?
```

- **Message-ID:** `<hS2mj.57509$Mu4.17465@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <6uahp3pt8algufod3m7j6qoc723fi8p22t@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> actually count?

All the believers I know accept all of the Bible as true.

> But nobody believes that Jesus is a Greek letter.   How much of the
> Bible symbolic Truths and how much is literal Truths has been debated
> since it was written.

There is symbology in the Bible, without doubt. But I have never seen
a significant point that could not be resolved by simply considering
the local and Bible contexts. God is not so stupid as to be unable to
commuincate what He wants. The problem was never that. The problem is
people not wanting to believe what the Bible clearly says, like eternal
punishment for unrepentant sinners. You just won't believe it; not
because the Bible doesn't say it, but because you don't want to believe
it. In essence, you let your own judgment overrule the Word of God.
That is the central problem of humanity: humans want things their way,
not God's way. We can accept and submit to God on His terms, or reject
Him. We do not get to set the terms, any more than we can determine
natural law. What incredible arrogance, to presume to dictate to God
what His terms are or should be!

How does God respond to such human arrogance? Consider the following
passages from both Old and New Testaments:

Psalm 2 (NKJV)
  Why do the nations rage,
  And the people plot a vain thing?
  The kings of the earth set themselves,
  And the rulers take counsel together,
  Against the Lord and against His Anointed, saying,
  "Let us break Their bonds in pieces
  And cast away Their cords from us."
  He who sits in the heavens shall laugh;
  The Lord shall hold them in derision.
  Then He shall speak to them in His wrath,
  And distress them in His deep displeasure:
  "Yet I have set My King
  On My holy hill of Zion."
  "I will declare the decree:
  The Lord has said to Me,
  'You are My Son,
  Today I have begotten You.
  Ask of Me, and I will give You
  The nations for Your inheritance,
  And the ends of the earth for Your possession.
  You shall break them with a rod of iron;
  You shall dash them to pieces like a potter's vessel.' "
  Now therefore, be wise, O kings;
  Be instructed, you judges of the earth.
  Serve the Lord with fear,
  And rejoice with trembling.
  Kiss the Son, lest He be angry,
  And you perish in the way,
  When His wrath is kindled but a little.
  Blessed are all those who put their trust in Him.

John 3:16-19 (NKJV)
  "For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten
  Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but
  have everlasting life. For God did not send His Son into
  the world to condemn the world, but that the world through
  Him might be saved. He who believes in Him is not condemned;
  but he who does not believe is condemned already, because he
  has not believed in the name of the only begotten Son of God.
  And this is the condemnation, that the light has come into
  the world, and men loved darkness rather than light, because
  their deeds were evil."
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:hS2mj.57509$Mu4.17465@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> it.

Or because it is inconsistent with the concept of a loving God.


>In essence, you let your own judgment overrule the Word of God.

As my own judgement has served me very well for the extent of my lifetime, I 
see no good reason to abandon it now.

If God wants to overrule my judgement, then, as Supreme Being, He resumably 
has that power. So far, He hasn't exercised it. I don't expect Him to, any 
time soon...

In the absence of any indication from God, I'll continue to reason things 
out for myself, as I have found this to be more successful than the 
alternative. (Letting other people reason things out for me...)

> That is the central problem of humanity: humans want things their way,
> not God's way. We can accept and submit to God on His terms, or reject
> Him.

It's a no-brainer. And it isn't personal. I don't reject God because I'm 
"anti-God" or wicked or evil; I do so because the "evidence" fails to 
persuade me.

>We do not get to set the terms, any more than we can determine
> natural law. What incredible arrogance, to presume to dictate to God
> what His terms are or should be!

What incredible arrogance on the part of a petty, petulant, bullying, object 
of worship to decide that there is no need to justify Himself to us.

We did a very bad job on creating this entity and it's time we fixed it.

In fact, it's time we dropped the imaginary friend altogether.

<tedious (although quite beautifully written) quotes snipped>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-24T17:14:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13pi6ufal6ur97d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>
> You're right Howard, many people do that. Unfortunately, it doesn't
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> destiny on a known lie?

Uh, Jesus never said he was the Messiah. He never said he was the Son of God 
(meaning biological rather than tribal). The Council of Nicea decided all 
that, along with the concept of the Trinity, in AD 325.

As for the Bible: it is almost irrelevant what it says. It is almost 
irrelevant what God said! What is important, or what SHOULD be important, is 
what God meant.

People have devoted their lives to studying Biblical texts. Here are some 
obvious things that one should not take seriously:
1. Exaggeration. Solomon had "treasures without end." Obviously they were 
not infinite.
2. Attribution: The Bible may accurately report the words of an actor, but 
could the actor have been lying? Sure he could. Heck, even God told a little 
fib to Abraham.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 60)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-25T13:26:48+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5vsomaF1ncrjoU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> knowing God and obtaining eternal life.

Very good. We agree... :-)


> If Jesus was not Who He claimed
> to be (the Messiah and Savior, the Son of God) then he was a liar or
> insane; certainly no one that should be admired or followed.

Too black and white.

Did he actually say he was the Saviour or that his sacrifice was to save 
Mankind? I can't find it.

Seems to me other people said that after he was dead (and risen, if you 
accept the Resurrection).

Please don't quote more scripture, but perhaps some references I can check 
in context, might shed more light on this. Any verbatim sources you know of, 
including the Gospels, where he is reported to have actually made these 
claims (rather than the writer presenting his own opinion) would be very 
acceptable.

If you are right and Jesus DID make these claims, it is also possible that 
he may have been mistaken, not wickedly seeking to deceive...

There was certainly SOMETHING special about this man; I would not dismiss 
all of the things attributed to him in the Gospels. I just don't accept it 
was supernatural, and I also believe much has been exaggerated and spun over 
centuries.

>The logical
> bottom line is that if the Bible isn't what it claims to be, the reliable
> and true Word of God,

Again, does the Bible itself claim to be the true Word of God?  Didn't the 
people who wrote it and the Council of Nicaea decide that?

As you know, it is a collection of ancient writings, describing events in 
the ancient world which can be independently verified in many cases. 
However, there are just as many ancient texts that were EXCLUDED from the 
Bible (and had the same credentials as what was INCLUDED) that we have to 
ask: "So, WHO decided what was the "reliable and true Word of God"?" (And, 
if you're anything like me, you then follow it with: "What agendas did these 
people have? What was their angle...?")

Is it unreasonable to suppose that if it WERE "the reliable and true Word of 
God", God would have put His imprimatur on it somewhere?... maybe it would 
light up every time you opened it :-), or there would be some other 
manifestation that this is a divine book, representing the will of a Supreme 
Being who is Lord of All...

No, it is a work of Men. Written by Men across centuries, in answer to the 
needs of Men.

> then it is a lie, and what nitwit would stake their
> faith and eternal destiny on a known lie?

Again, too black and white. It isn't necessarily a lie in the sense of a 
deliberate deceit. The nature of Truth changes. What might be an acceptable 
explanation to a primitive people 3500 years ago, may not be seen as "truth" 
today.

I believe there is much of value in the Scriptures of most of the World's 
great religions. It isn't about a two-valued Logic system: True or False, 
Black or White, it is about what is useful and helpful relative to other 
alternatives; a many-valued Logic system.

Pete.
```
