```
tlmfru wrote:
>
> Well, come on.  You've mentioned ONE document so far (I am lumping
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You are correct in that the paragraph in Josephus mentioning Jesus is 
universally accepted to be a gloss.

A more interesting question is, out of the thousands of pages in the 
Josephus' work, why did the author of that paragraph, either Josephus or 
someone else, put the paragraph where it is? Immediately after the paragraph 
mentioning Jesus, is the scandal involving the Temple of Isis. In a nutshell 
(short version):

A dude named Mundis was smitten by a married woman named Paulina. He offered 
her 200,000 Attic drachmas for one night's play. Mundis was refused. As a 
result of this rejection, Mundis determined to kill himself, but his 
housekeeper, an evil woman named Ide, devised a plan. She conspired with the 
priests of the Temple of Isis.

The High Priest of the Temple approached Paulina and told her the God 
Annubis wished to sup with her! Paulina quickly assented. The appointed 
night came and Paulina went to the temple and dined alone. The lights were 
put out, the priests retired, and the great doors locked.

Thereupon Mundis, disguised as the god Annubis, lept from behind the 
curtains and had his way with the willing Paulina all the night through.

On the third day thereafter, Mundis met Paulina in the market and said: 
"Nay, Paulina, thou hast saved me two hundred thousand drachmas, which sum 
thou mightest have added to thy own family; yet hast thou not failed to be 
at my service in the manner I invited thee... I rejoice in the pleasure I 
reaped by what I did when I took unto myself the name Annubis."

The mortified Paulina told her husband. Her husband told Tiberius.

Tiberus ordered the priests crucified, as well as the housekeeper Ide, the 
temple torn down and the statue of the god thrown in the Tiber.

Mundis was only banished inasmuch as what he did was done out of love.

I have my own thoughts about the placement of this story; you may have 
yours. At least one thing is clear - human nature hasn't changed much.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 66)_

- **From:** "roger.pearse@googlemail.com" <roger.pearse@googlemail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-30T17:42:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5d268cc5-fd67-4026-b00e-d7aa2eb658c2@c23g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13pieuh6qdoqqb5@corp.supernews.com> <3uomj.60016$vt2.37057@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <fb6cd888-dae4-4116-ae76-a4e842156b6d@q39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <vROnj.68395$N67.7431@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <EjSnj.23248$E01.2704@newsfe22.lga> <GS_nj.62829$vt2.45599@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Jan 30, 1:22 pm, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> > Judson McClendon <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> than any other comparably ancient event.

I think this needs to be nuanced rather more.  It's broadly true.  But
there are several different things mixed up in this, which I think I
should clarify.

Firstly the life of Jesus of Nazareth is indeed very well documented,
because of the existence of 4 contemporary biographies.  It is better
documented than any *comparable* figure of the same period; i.e.
relatively obscure provincial figures.  It has better *literary*
attestation than some emperors! But... it is not better documented
than some of the major figures of the immediately preceding period.
And figures who left traces such as coins and inscriptions are of
course not comparable; we have nothing like that for Jesus (or indeed
for most important figures apart from emperors and officials).

If we ignore archaeology, consider the emperor under whom Jesus
flourished; Tiberius.  Our major sources for all the events of his
reign are Tacitus, Suetonius, Cassius Dio and Josephus.  All these
write ca. 100 AD or later, but are perfectly good sources, and we get
a far better picture of his reign and policies from these than from
contemporary sources written in fear of Sejanus' praetorian thugs.  In
addition we do have contemporary literature of various sorts.  So
there is good documentation, of sorts.

But surely we are not comparing an obscure Jewish peasant with the
master of the world?  If so, surely we commit a canard?

> The documents are vastly more numerous, and much closer to the
> actual events. For example, there are
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> was in living memory of them.

This is a truly common confusion (on all sides, I might add), between
the *texts* that were written in antiquity, and the physical
handwritten copies (=manuscripts)that exist today of them.

The above paragraph is taken from some literature discussing the
evidence that the words written in the bible in ancient times have
reached us, responding to the objection that since all copying
involves error then we can't actually possess a copy of the bible word
for word anyway (which objection involves obscurantism).  And it is
absolutely correct; there are far more and earlier biblical
manuscripts than of any other Greek or Latin text.  There is no
mystery to this; the scribes of the succeeding period needed bibles.
The next largest group of manuscripts is of copies of texts by the
major Church Fathers, and for the same reason.

But this has nothing to do with the number of *texts* that describe
events.  Do I make the distinction clear?

> It is uncommon for even hostile secular critics who are knowledgeable
> of this evidence to question that the Gospel accounts of Jesus' life
> are accurate.

I can assure you that there is no detail of Christian origins that has
not been denied, misrepresented, lied about or distorted over the last
200 years.  Trust me on this!

But what I think you mean is that educated people of all religions,
including atheists who hate Christianity, do not try to assert that
the texts are so corrupt that we don't even know what they said.  I
think this is true, although one atheist bible scholar is currently
trying very hard to put this idea out.

In short I think that the above contains overstatements.  What we can
certainly say is :

1.  that the text of the New Testament that we have is a good copy of
the original -- better, anyway, than any copy of any other ancient
*literary* text -- and good enough for all practical purposes (whether
that includes theological or divine purposes is for people to whom God
speaks to say, not us :-) ).

2.  that the number of literary texts is more than adequate to detail
the life, career and teaching of this obscure but remarkable man, and
is even corroborated by stray mentions in surviving classical
literature, as far as they go.

I must admit that I don't quite understand why people get into
difficulty with all this.  The same sort of thing could be said of
Alexander of Abuteichnos, who invented a fake deity Glycon in the 2nd
century (documented by Lucian, and with coins of the cult), although
the evidence for him is much less.  The question is whether the claims
of this person are true.  On that, there might legitimately be more
than one opinion.

All the same, I weary of the silent presumption that all of us can
continue to live by whatever system we currently do, but only the
Christians have to prove their position.  Rationally that must be
nonsense.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13q2a4pcpk98p48@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13pieuh6qdoqqb5@corp.supernews.com> <3uomj.60016$vt2.37057@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <fb6cd888-dae4-4116-ae76-a4e842156b6d@q39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <vROnj.68395$N67.7431@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <EjSnj.23248$E01.2704@newsfe22.lga> <GS_nj.62829$vt2.45599@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>> So, Judson, you've stated that the birth of Christ is one of the
>> best-documented events of the time.  You've yet to justify that
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> that, then there is nothing I can say that would change your mind.

Uh, no. The earliest surviving manuscript of the New Testament is a two-inch 
square from the Gospel of John, dated to mid-fourth century. That the 
Gospels themselves originated earlier - Mark is dated about 65 CE - they 
probably were transmitted orally for several centuries. Of course Mark never 
saw Jesus, so he was not a witness himself. And no early manuscript of the 
Gospels has ever been found in Aramaic, Hebrew, or Latin.

That there are no manuscript fragments from earlier times may be the result 
of the Council of Nicea creating an establishment church and that 
organization's zeal to vacuum up any non-conforming texts.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 67)_

- **From:** "roger.pearse@googlemail.com" <roger.pearse@googlemail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-31T00:56:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0e538233-7ac7-4d9f-be96-965188d78ebc@b2g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13pieuh6qdoqqb5@corp.supernews.com> <3uomj.60016$vt2.37057@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <fb6cd888-dae4-4116-ae76-a4e842156b6d@q39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <vROnj.68395$N67.7431@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <EjSnj.23248$E01.2704@newsfe22.lga> <GS_nj.62829$vt2.45599@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <13q2a4pcpk98p48@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Jan 31, 1:47 am, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >> So, Judson, you've stated that the birth of Christ is one of the
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> square from the Gospel of John, dated to mid-fourth century.

Um, by the mid fourth century we have complete parchment bibles such
as Sinaiticus!

From the third century we have more or less complete manuscripts of
most NT texts, including gospels.

The earliest surviving fragment, to which you refer, was dated to 125
AD by all the paleographers who found it, plus or minus 25 years.
That dating still holds, despite intermittent efforts by odd scholars
to try to date it later.

> That the Gospels themselves originated earlier - Mark is dated about
> 65 CE - they probably were transmitted orally for several centuries.

No, that can't be so.

> Of course Mark never saw Jesus, so he was not a witness himself.

He recorded the testimony of Peter, who was.

> And no early manuscript of the Gospels has ever been found in
> Aramaic, Hebrew, or Latin.

Not least since they were composed in Greek... :-)

> That there are no manuscript fragments from earlier times may be the result
> of the Council of Nicea creating an establishment church

No such thing occurred at the Council of Nicaea.  Christianity did not
become the state religion until 50 years later, in the time of
Theodosius I.

> and that organization's zeal to vacuum up any non-conforming texts.

This story unfortunately is not recorded in any contemporary text,
despite the "Da Vinci Code", and doesn't reflect how things actually
happened on the ground.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 68)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-31T10:59:56-06:00

- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13pieuh6qdoqqb5@corp.supernews.com> <3uomj.60016$vt2.37057@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <fb6cd888-dae4-4116-ae76-a4e842156b6d@q39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <vROnj.68395$N67.7431@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <EjSnj.23248$E01.2704@newsfe22.lga> <GS_nj.62829$vt2.45599@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <13q2a4pcpk98p48@corp.supernews.com> <0e538233-7ac7-4d9f-be96-965188d78ebc@b2g2000hsg.googlegroups.com> <13q3vk2bcucmj48@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Jan 31, 4:59 pm, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
> roger.pea...@googlemail.com wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> you remember something?

A police siren?  :-)

> >> And no early manuscript of the Gospels has ever been found in
> >> Aramaic, Hebrew, or Latin.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> And Peter or Mark spoke Greek? Oh well.

An awful lot of people out in the region speak English at the moment,
as well as their native Arabic.  In Hellenistic times everyone who
wanted to sell a camel or a postcard probably needed to know some
Greek.

Josephus knew Greek, but not to rely on, so hired assistants to polish
his composition.  These are sufficiently distinctive, in terms of the
older authors whom they mimic, as to be identifiable by modern
editors.  It's notable in that context that Peter co-writes his
epistle with Silas, whose Greek name of Silvanus suggests that he was
pretty Hellenised.

> >> That there are no manuscript fragments from earlier times may be the
> >> result of the Council of Nicea creating an establishment church
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> dogma as heretics.

Probably all a bit anachronistic; the Spanish Inquisition wasn't to be
thought of for a 1000 years.  At that date the Fathers had just come
through a bestial persecution, and some of them had come straight from
the prison-camps etc.  Many of them were simply glad to be alive!
Others couldn't get over the unimaginable idea of a Christian
emperor.

The later ideas of authoritarianism aren't what is going on there, or
not seriously.  Every movement has the right to self-definition,
without this being sinister. The use of accusations of heresy as part
of a power struggle really starts ca. 400 in the Origenist
controversies, when money and power come into it.  (The accounts of
the Council of Ephesus in 433 don't make pretty reading)  Constantine
was somewhat responsible for this, in that around the time of the
Council of Nicaea he gave the clergy wealth and legal position.  The
rest was inevitable, as people scrambled to join the new privileged
class.

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

```
On 29 Jan, 23:42, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

> > When the wall of Jericho fell might imperil your faith. Where are
> > Sodom and Gomorrah? Fire and Brimstone would not destroy stone, brick
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is only one mention of Sargon in the bible. Hardly a glowing
commendation; Isaiah 20:1, which records the Assyrian capture of
Ashdod in 711 BC.

For the record, I do not doubt that Sodom and Gommorah existed and
were destroyed by natural processes (slipping either under sea or in
to liquid mud).

>
> >> To my mind, one of the most convincing evidences for the authenticity
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> actual proof.

I suspect that the number of non-believers or atheists in the UK
during the early 1900s would not constitute a secular population but
rather a non-religious minority. Faith in the bible in the early 20th
century was high with most people believing in the bible 100%.

> In this particular case, I'm speaking of verification
> of Biblical historial accuracy and authenticity, not whether or not
> you actually believe what it says about God.

I didn't say whether I believed in god. In the 1900s virtually
everyone in the UK believed in god and the bible so faith would have
been absolute. Since then the readiness of people to believe in the
word of the bible has declined and some of the blame for that belongs
to the various religious groups espousing different doctrines and
interpretations of the bible.

The bible is not historically accurate.

> You do not believe
> that God created the workd in six days, but you have no proof
> that He did not.

Outside of the bible all proof points to a duration of 4.5 - 5 billion
years. Only the bible says 6 days and some groups argue that a day can
be as long as you want it to be. With faith so malleable, what price
is truth?

> For example, in the Biblical account of the Exodus,
> the Bible says the Jews had to make brick without straw. They have
> found archeological evidence of that. This is the kind of verification
> I'm referring to here.

Couldn't your god have put those bricks there just to provide
corroboration for a poorly written book?

>
> >> If the Bible was merely
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> and uninformed, or obtuse.

Please list all parts of the bible which have been irrefutably proven
to be true. I would be particularly interested in knowing where Lot's
wife, in the form of a pillar of salt, can be found.


>
> >> My favorite is Sargon II, which I've listed here before.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Names are wrong by whose taxonomy?

By the only means of verifying the truth of the bible: outside
sources. If a number of disparate sources disagree with the bible and
have some corroboration within themselves then the bible must, in the
absence of any verifying source, be deemed to be wrong.

>
> >> The Bible is far more than simply a bunch of words, even divinely
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> evidence, then read the book. ISBN 0-8254-2747-9

Greenleaf's standard of proof leaves a lot to be desired. Only a court
of canonical law would find in favour of the accounts of the gospels.
Two of the gospels would be thrown out for being blatant plagiarism of
another manuscript and no gospel would be admitted as evidence because
they have multiple authors and were not contemporary to the incidents
that they purport to be eye-witness to.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13q2832ro5103c3@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com> <_t1mj.57471$Mu4.46708@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <13pieuh6qdoqqb5@corp.supernews.com> <3uomj.60016$vt2.37057@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <fb6cd888-dae4-4116-ae76-a4e842156b6d@q39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
>
> What about the chronologies of the pharoahs (names are wrong) and the
> genealogies mentioned in this thread that differ from the texts that
> you would say corroborate the bible?
>

It's getting worked out. One of the more interesting endeavors was a British 
Serving Officer in India who, as an amateur, puzzled over the contradictions 
in the geneologies of the Hebrew Kings. Until he worked it out, scholars had 
dismissed the chronologies as scribal error or mistake.

Here's what this officer (whose name escapes me) figured out:
1. The beginnings of the year were different in the kingdoms of Judea and 
Israel. One group started their year on Rosh Hashannah, the other on 
Passover.
2. Some kingships started their reign with a year zero, others started 
numbering at one, some didn't start either number until the new year 
(whichever calendar was in use).
3. Sometimes there was a co-regency - a son and father ruled concurrently, 
so the twelfth year of the father may have been the third year of the son.

Anyway, applying all these fudge-factors (almost all with sufficient 
justification), the linage of the Hebrew Kings made sense.

Here's another story you might find interesting.

In the late 1920s, a chap (call him "Smith" because I forgot his name) 
graduates from Oxford with a degree in linguistics. In time, he was 
conscripted by the British War Ministry and sent to code-breaking school. He 
then spent the war years involved in de-cyphering enemy messages. After the 
war, Smith went on to become a reader in Norse mythology and ancient 
languages at Cambridge.

Shift gears

In the early 1970s, a farmer in Minnesota dug up a small boulder with Runic 
inscriptions. He loaded it into his truck and carried it to a history 
teacher at the local community college. The instructor, had no idea, but 
took some Polaroids and sent them to a professor he knew at CCNY.

Professor Brown (?) immediately mailed back: "Whatever you do, don't give 
Olaf Svenson any money! These are runes, but they don't make any sense. 
Somebody just copied random letters from an encyclopedia." When Svenson (or 
whatever his name was) was told, he took the rock back home and dumped it 
behind the barn and said "Fuck 'em all" (probably in Swedish).

Time passes.

In the early '80's, an annual convention of Norse scolars was held in New 
York (there are not a LOT of Norse scholars in the world). Professor Brown 
was the host, and invited a few (all? both?) of the attendees to his home 
for cocktails. Sufficiently lubricated, Brown pulls out his bullshit file 
and passes around the Polaroids from several years before.

Smith looks. Click-click-click! IT'S IN CODE! A simple substitution code 
that translated into something like: "Here, on the 214th day of the voyage 
of Bundy the Long Knife, I, Faustine the Brave, claim all this land in the 
name of our king...."

Serendipity. What are the chances of an English scholar trained in ancient 
Norse languages AND cryptography coming across a Runic surveyor's mark from 
Minnesota while visiting in New York?

Anyway, Smith hies himself back to England and founds a new science: 
Archeological Cryptology, perhaps a sub-discipline of deciphering ancient 
texts (hieroglyphs, Minoan B, etc.).

Also know the British Museum never throws anything away. Over the centuries, 
the museum has accumulated a lot of "junk" manuscripts, mainly from folks 
wanting money. Smith began studying these, and, sure enough, many were in 
code.

(Aside: I may have a few details wrong: The names, the sequence, the dates, 
places, and times. The facts may be fuzzy, but the narrative is correct. I 
make no apologies; I suffer from Some-heimers)

One of the more interesting decodings, to bring this all back to the 
discussion at hand, is a confusing verse in 2 Kings. Right in the middle of 
a geneology, there's a verse which says "Bananas are cheaper by the dozen," 
or somesuch. Religious scholars, both Jewish and Christian, have, for two 
thousand years, tried to deduce the holy substance and divine message behind 
this puzzling verse. Turns out this verse, too, is a simple substitution 
code. It translates as "This King is a fink."

Point is, discoveries are being made all the time, and not always from 
digging stuff up.
```

```
This description doesn't ring a bell, but it has resemblances to the
Kensington Stone story: farmer finds stone under a tree with what appear to
be runes, detailing a visit of a Nordic party nearby.  Much controversy.
But the inscription has now been pretty well proved to be a fake,
particularly because some of the runes were found only in a popular
scientific & historical publication of the same era.

Pity.  I liked the story!

PL
```

```
On Wed, 30 Jan 2008 19:12:11 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>Alistair wrote:
>>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That was the Kensington Rune Stone.  The stone was found by a
Minnesota farmer named Olaf Ohman in November of 1898 (way before your
1970 date) while a digging up a poplar tree stump on the southern
slope of a 50-foot high knoll. The stone was buried face down about
six inches below the surface, with the tree roots wrapped around it. 

The stone was sent to the University of Minnesota and then to Chicago.
It was studied by runic scholars, who interpreted the inscription to
be an account of Norse explorers in the 14th Century. Many authorities
who have since examined the stone have claimed it a forgery, but
others are equally certain of its authenticity.

The transliteration of the text is generally accepted as:

"Eight Goths and 22 Norwegians on a journey of exploration from
Vinland very far west. We had camp by 2 rocky islands one day's
journey north from this stone. We were out fishing one day. After we
came home we found 10 men red with blood and dead. AVM [Ave Maria]
save us from evil." 

The inscription along the edge of the stone says:

"Have 10 men by the sea to look after our ships 14 days' journey from
this island. Year 1362."

The stone is now in the Runestone Museum in Alexandria, Minnesota,
near where the stone was found.

You can read a complete account of the Kensington Rune Stone and the
Heavener Rune Stone and other rune discoveries in America at:

http://www.sunnyway.com/runes/americanstones.html


>Professor Brown (?) immediately mailed back: "Whatever you do, don't give 
>Olaf Svenson any money! These are runes, but they don't make any sense. 
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,

         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Steve
```

```
In view of this, I just spent half an hour scouring my library and actually
found my authority!

"The Kensington Stone, a mystery solved" by Erik Wahlgren: U. of Wisconsin
Press, Madison, 1958: LCC 58-6281.  I don't know if it has been assigned an
ISBN.

As I say, the peculiar runestones are the definitive proof pro or can, and
this book pretty well establishes the CON case.

PL
```

```
This sort of thing raises very grave doubts.  I find it just about
impossible to believe that a set of runes that, translated, come out with a
coherent message, can ALSO be a substitution code.  There have been similar
claims for the scrap of manuscript (parchment) that was found in the Oak
Island pit.  A very short message might work - three or four characters -
but not anything longer.

PL
```

```
On Jan 25, 1:31 am, "Charles Hottel" <chot...@earthlink.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
>

> Do you believe the Bible was literally dictated by God?

Who knows?

> As humans were involved perfection does not seem possible.  

Quite a lot of theological assumptions in there!  But it's fairly
obvious that I can give you a message; quite why a God should not be
able to do the same is not clear to me.

> It does seem that the Bibile has been edited i.e. changed.

Not in any meaningful sense.  But I sense a perfect storm of strawmen
about to be deployed.  The question that we should ask, surely, is why
our masters want our minds to be populated with some many excuses as
to why "Do not steal, do not commit adultery, do not lie" is not
binding.  While they keep our minds off this question, and while we
internalise what they feed us, we are unlikely to reach *any* valid
conclusions.  Surely?

All the best,

Roger Pearse
```

```
While the hadwriting is indeed upon the wall for this particular troll, it 
seems to me that the best way to deal with trolls is simply not to respond 
to their ravings.

They NEED the energy of responses.

(nevertheless, I had to smile at the above, Charlie :-))

Pete.
```

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-24T10:43:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Rf3mj.25708$3b7.3689@newsfe23.lga>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com>`

```
I have a book somewhere called something like "Antiquity and Unity of the
Human Race", put out by some Christian theologian in the late 1800's,
discussing the writings of the Greeks and others, where they give figures of
thousands of years for this or that event or lifespan.  He harmonizes this
sort of thing with Bishop Usher's 4004 AD chronology by stating that the
Greeks etc. often wrote "years" when they meant "months".

If this sort of thinking is permissible then there's nothing that can't be
proved.

PL


Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 24 Jan 2008 22:07:12 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> >For me to agree with that, I'd have to believe that events described in
> >Genesis are true and actual fact, and that there really WERE two
individuals
> >named Adam and Eve, created directly by God from dust and a rib bone. To
> >believe that, I would have to accept that the Earth is less than 6000
years
> >old (calculated from the genealogies in the Pentateuch). Obviously, it is
> >impossible for me to do that, as the evidence refutes it. So here we must
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> before days, it must be a different definition than literalists claim.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-25T12:57:27+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5vsmv9F1nvpvnU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com>`

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:8c7hp39noavlon9hqjb9vl5arntn5e2d0s@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 24 Jan 2008 22:07:12 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> before days, it must be a different definition than literalists claim.

Nevertheless, if you add up the lifespans of the genealogies described in 
the Pentateuch, starting from Adam, and add in the same for the descendants 
of Noah, then add in the 3500 years since it was written, you get around 
6000 years. It has nothing to do with the literal or allegorical meaning of 
"Days".

Even if we assumed that EVERYBODY lived several hundred years, it still 
comes out with the creation of the planet as less than 20,000 years ago.

I can't buy that, and, even if I could, there are too many other 
inconsistencies to make it acceptable. If Adam and Eve were the first people 
on Earth, how could Adam's son depart to the land of Nod and take a wife, 
after killing his brother?

The only options are:

1. It is an allegory not meant to be taken literally.
2. It is simply wrong.

Either way, I can't subscribe to it.

Pete.
```

```
news:mR9mj.59478$vt2.28516@bignews8.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> one. Duh!

Perhaps you should re-read chapters 4 and 5.

It is quite clear that Adam and Eve produced Cain, Abel, and Seth BEFORE 
they produced other offspring. At the time Cain murdered Abel, Seth was not 
born, and neither were their other offspring.

Just one of many inconsistencies in the "reliable and true Holy Word of 
God".

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

```
On 25 Jan, 00:14, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:


> > I can't buy that, and, even if I could, there are too many other inconsistencies to make it acceptable. If Adam and Eve were the
> > first people on Earth, how could Adam's son depart to the land of Nod and take a wife, after killing his brother?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 3. Adam and Eve had other sons and daughters (Gen 5:4), and Cain married one. Duh!

Incest? Sick.
KJV 12:
004:016 And Cain went out from the presence of the LORD, and dwelt in
        the land of Nod, on the east of Eden.

004:017 And Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch:
        and he builded a city, and called the name of the city,
        after the name of his son, Enoch.

The bible does not explicitly state that Cain's wife was his sister
nor does it say that she wasn't. However, if she were his sister then
it would read "And Cain took his wife and went out from the presence
of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden.

Interestingly, Cain founds a city (which presumably was populated by
more of his brothers and sisters who were enjoined by god to slay Cain
upon sight).
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden.

Not if she was already there, or went there later. The Bible does not
record the timeframe, only the sequence of the birth of the sons.
These people lived almost a thousand years; what difference if Cain's
wife was born 50 or 100 years after him? Many people marry with
10 or 15 year difference in age, equivalent to more than 150 years
difference in those days. The Bible says Adam and Eve had other
sons and daughters, it doesn't say how many, or how many their
children had, or their grandchildren. Cain's wife could have been his
neice, or grand neice. Lack of detail does not imply a contradiction.

> Interestingly, Cain founds a city (which presumably was populated by
> more of his brothers and sisters who were enjoined by god to slay Cain
> upon sight).

(Genesis 4:15)
  And the Lord said to him, "Therefore, whoever kills Cain, vengeance
  shall be taken on him sevenfold." And the Lord set a mark on Cain,
  lest anyone finding him should kill him.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> If Adam and Eve had followed all the rules we would all be happier
>> today.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> feelings... :-))

The rabbis answered a similar question when the authorship of the Torah was 
questioned. They said: "Perhaps Moses did not write the Torah. If he did 
not, then someone else did who, coincidentally, was named Moses."

Once upon a time I was introduced to the concept of "Quality Control 
Thinking." One of its principles is: "I don't care what you believe, what 
can you prove?"

Consider UFOs. I'm agnostic on their existence, but I can readily be 
persuaded: put a piece of a UFO in my hand.

With regard to God, there were 700,000 eyeball witnesses to God's 
manifestation at Sinai and an unbroken tradition relating these events. The 
repeated eye-witness testimony is just as believable as a story in the New 
York Times!

No, wait...
```

```
On Thu, 24 Jan 2008 17:07:40 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>With regard to God, there were 700,000 eyeball witnesses to God's 
>manifestation at Sinai and an unbroken tradition relating these events. The 
>repeated eye-witness testimony is just as believable as a story in the New 
>York Times!

There are histories that we "know" that aren't true.   Most of us are
familiar with Julio-Claudian dynasty of ancient Rome.   Except we are
familiar with what politicians said about them after they died, and
what authors have written about them.    There is no religious agenda
here - we're not personally threatened if we find out that Claudius
wasn't what we think of him.   



Ransom Stoddard: You're not going to use the story, Mr. Scott?
Maxwell Scott: No, sir. This is the west, sir. When the legend becomes
fact, print the legend.
```

```
Alistair wrote:
>
> So the entire population of Jews in (the time of Pharoah or the time
> of JC being born?) was 3 million - significantly greater than I would
> expect for a largely subsistence agricultural tradition.

Oh no, not subsistence agriculture. LARGE SCALE agriculture ("store up seven 
years worth of food"). Egypt was a net exporter of food (grain, olives, 
figs, oils, etc.). The Egyptians created almost immeasurable wealth with 
their harvests.
```

- **References:** `<Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <13pid9ub6bmjo90@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
>
> Love your neighbor as yourself. Turn the other cheek. Do not hate your
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> his heart. (Mat 5:27,28, TEV)

What one "thinks" is generally not against the Law (of Moses). Jesus may 
have added some new rules,  but as for following the "spirit"? Not required.

For example, consider the law of refraining from starting a fire on the 
Sabbath. How does the observant Jew follow his school's football team on 
Saturday TV? He puts a timer on the set so that it comes on just before the 
game. How does he see where to pee? He leaves the light on in the toilet. 
How does he have a hot meal? Look up "cholent." It's okay to play tricks on 
God.

Jewish law says follow the rules, not the spirit.
```

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net...
>
>
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
> So here we must part company. (No hard feelings... :-))


I would not expect you to nor was I trying to convince you.

>
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I do not have an answer for everything.  I have read some attempts to 
reconcile Genesis and science but I do not find them very compelling.  Some 
people believe evolution and dating methods are wrong or worse a great 
conspiracy (not me).  If it is a hoax it is certainly one of the most 
elaborate ones you could think of.  It seems unfair for God to allow us to 
be deceived by a seeming supernatural deception which aslo would require 
supernatural powers.  I just do not know. My left brain tends to believe 
that the earth  is very old as scientists have said.  My right brain wants 
the Bible to be true but realizes that since  humans were involved it there 
is a zero probability that the Bible is perfect.  When I read the Bible I am 
mainly looking for what it tells me I need to do to incrementally improve or 
feel better or for insights etc.  I find that the same passages can tell me 
different things at different times, perhaps because my perspective or frame 
of reference has changed. I have long ago reconciled myself to not having 
all the answers.
```

_(thread depth: 59)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2008-01-25T08:15:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6ef5b181-78d1-418b-a821-e24fd63f5bca@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <13pieb27ocnhe42@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On 25 Jan, 01:20, "Charles Hottel" <chot...@earthlink.net> wrote:

>
> I do not have an answer for everything.  I have read some attempts to
> reconcile Genesis and science but I do not find them very compelling.  Some
> people believe evolution and dating methods are wrong or worse a great
> conspiracy (not me).  

This is a simple one to resolve. The bible is wrong but, in the
context of the times in which Genesis was written, it was as good an
explanation as any for how the world came in to being. I see no
problem in following christianity and believing in science.

I do see problems with "Dogs don't have souls" (my first year
microbiology tutor). Not least because (until proven irrefutably
wrong) nowhere in the bible does it say that the beasts do not have
souls and the existence of souls in humans has not been proven by
scientific method.

Further, my boozy chum believes Genesis absolutely but that has not
stopped us sharing a few pints and a chat over the last 20 years.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<13pq0nimun62l60@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <13pieb27ocnhe42@corp.supernews.com> <6ef5b181-78d1-418b-a821-e24fd63f5bca@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <8spmj.6158$HL1.5976@newsfe21.lga> <131edb92-3959-4638-8c4b-bab99bddf6de@j78g2000hsd.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
>
> My understanding is that it is a concept derived from a Hebrew word
> meaning the corporeal body. I may be wrong about this but I understood
> that Jews had no concept of a spiritual entity seperate from the
> physical body.

Right. There is no "official" Jewish position. Speculating on such is 
specifically discouraged. The streets of heaven may be paved with gold - or 
with mud. There's nothing we can do about it. We can, however, strive to 
make this a better world. Today.

The rabbis say there are four things we should not ponder: What came before, 
what comes after, what is above, and what is below.
```

```
  And after my skin is destroyed, this I know,
  That in my flesh I shall see God,

then be eventually resurrected and given a new body. This is also
in accord with New Testament belief.
```

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** tim Josling <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au>
- **Date:** 2008-01-25T21:27:52
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13pkl2oea3e1odc@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <13pieb27ocnhe42@corp.supernews.com> <6ef5b181-78d1-418b-a821-e24fd63f5bca@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <rXpmj.60064$vt2.51752@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Fri, 25 Jan 2008 12:33:01 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> (Ecclesiastes 3:21)
>   Who knows the spirit of the sons of men, which goes upward, and the
>   spirit of the animal, which goes down to the earth?

Judson is a good example of the maxim that late converts are often the
most zealous. Sometimes after a few years they lighten up...

To take that process to the limit, look at Italy. They have been Catholics
for thousands of years, but few of them seem to take it very seriously.
For example, the lowest birth rate in the EEC, in spite of the prohibition
on effective birth control. (Don't try to tell me about the Billings
method. My daughter is living proof it doesn't work.)

There is no infallible source of knowledge. We just muddle ahead the best
we can. The scientific method is by far the best way of doing this that's
been found so far.

Judson made the point that his life turned around after he became
religious. This of course occurs with other religions too. For example,
Malcolm X turned his life around after converting to Islam. Does this mean
Islam is the one true religion?

The common element in a lot of these stories of lives turned around is
that the person stopped focusing on their own immediate pleasure and turned
their focus to something bigger than that. The something bigger does not
have to be religion. It can be your own chosen goals for example, as long
as they are meaningful to you.

Tim
```

```
<tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au> wrote:

>The common element in a lot of these stories of lives turned around is
>that the person stopped focusing on their own immediate pleasure and turned
>their focus to something bigger than that. The something bigger does not
>have to be religion. It can be your own chosen goals for example, as long

bad for the rest of us (we all can find examples).   It doesn't make
it less attractive to believe you're working for something bigger than
yourself.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-29T17:51:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xZOnj.68399$N67.49446@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<Y5cjj.57242$N67.30869@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <5vno06F1n00g8U1@mid.individual.net> <13pfjlcbb7ojdc@corp.supernews.com> <5vr2puF1ntkdrU1@mid.individual.net> <13pieb27ocnhe42@corp.supernews.com> <6ef5b181-78d1-418b-a821-e24fd63f5bca@v46g2000hsv.googlegroups.com> <rXpmj.60064$vt2.51752@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <5beba64b-c082-4510-847a-d106399ac2fd@s13g2000prd.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>proof of that?

A spirit is, by definition not part of the material universe. God is Spirit.
How would you prove, or disprove, using purely physical tools and
techniques, something that is, by definition, not of the physical universe?
Even phrasing such a proposition requires is an a priori rejection of the
Bible. Which is what I've been saying all along. You start by rejecting
the Bible, then demand proof in a framework which already excludes the
possibility. Disingenuous doesn't quite cover the deception inplicit in
that. :-)
```

```
On 29 Jan, 23:51, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> >"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> A spirit is, by definition not part of the material universe.

By your definition alone. My definition would read something along the
lines of a bogus entity purported by some to survive death. Bogus
being the import word.

> God is Spirit.
> How would you prove, or disprove, using purely physical tools and
> techniques, something that is, by definition, not of the physical universe?

Again by your definition. My definition would read something along the
lines of a bogus entity purported by some to preced the start of the
universe and to survive the demise of the same universe. Bogus being
the import word.

You really should not resort to hiding your god in extra dimensions or
outside of the universe. By definition there is nothing outside of the
universe and the extra dimensions are so small that your god would
look ludicrous if he were measured.

> Even phrasing such a proposition requires is an a priori rejection of the
> Bible. Which is what I've been saying all along. You start by rejecting
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

I do reject the bible. I also reject the Grimm Fairy Tales. Neither
represents a solid base for living life.

Why should I, as an atheist, even contemplate the possibility of
playing the game by your rules? If god exists, and has made himself
known to you, then you will have empirical proof that he exists and we
can then examine with due scientific method your proof and deduce the
probability that god exists (or more likely doesn't).
```

```
>> Do you have evidence that people in societies based upon these rules

So which societies don't have prohibitions against unauthorized (by
the state) killing?     To conclude that the 10 commandments work
better than other codes, we need to compare societies that have
differences from the 10 commandments.

The primary commandment is "Thou shalt have no other god before me".
That's one that not all societies agree upon.     Are societies that
don't place Jehovah as the #1 god less happy than societies that do?
```

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-23T10:23:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2SJlj.78567$L%6.3759@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<atiso3tl8eq1ajms077t00l4j5311h1kp4@4ax.com> <u-idndR6jsvnSw7anZ2dnUVZ_rDinZ2d@comcast.com> <5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <iijep3956tp97gd6a77rbkjf1ke1fimj1c@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> The Bible says death - but for a long time the prevalent belief of
> Christians was that it is eternal torture.   That might still be a
> majority belief.

The following is a description of "Judgment Day." I've put blank
lines to separate the first and last passages, which are of
particular interest here.

Revelation 20:10-15
  The devil, who deceived them, was cast into the lake of fire and
  brimstone where the beast and the false prophet are. And they will
  be tormented day and night forever and ever.

  Then I saw a great white throne and Him who sat on it, from whose
  face the earth and the heaven fled away. And there was found no
  place for them. And I saw the dead, small and great, standing
  before God, and books were opened. And another book was opened,
  which is the Book of Life. And the dead were judged according to
  their works, by the things which were written in the books. The
  sea gave up the dead who were in it, and Death and Hades delivered
  up the dead who were in them. And they were judged, each one
  according to his works. Then Death and Hades were cast into the
  lake of fire. This is the second death.

  And anyone not found written in the Book of Life was cast into the
  lake of fire.

One might assume that only some who are cast into the lake of fire
will be tormented forever, but the Bible gives no support for this
position.
```

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-23T09:45:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lhrep3tkarppnc6fc4plu0vj0nfbuueoc2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <iijep3956tp97gd6a77rbkjf1ke1fimj1c@4ax.com> <2SJlj.78567$L%6.3759@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Wed, 23 Jan 2008 10:23:35 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>One might assume that only some who are cast into the lake of fire
>will be tormented forever, but the Bible gives no support for this
>position.

It is interesting that this passage had the dead judged according to
their works.   Most current Christian churches teach that everybody is
a sinner and thus damned, but that some people are forgiven and saved
- not through their works, but through their belief.

I suppose people find in the Bible what they are looking for.   Those
who dislike eternal torture find that the majority of people die.
Those who want the other guys to suffer find that.
```

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> we like it or not...

Pete, surely you are not under the impression that you are conciously aware
of all your motivations? Any psychologist or psychiatrist would be deeply
amused. :-)

You have made many statements here showing serious bias, even hostility,
against things the Bible clearly teaches. Assuming for the sake of argument
that God exists and the Bible is true, you have a priori rejected the
principles established by your Creator and Judge. Reality doesn't depend on
your understanding or consent to be. But when have you ever made the slightest
hint of anything like "If I could see real evidence, I would be willing to
accept Jesus and submit my life to God?" None, zip. You are clearly not open
to or interested in that proposition, as witnessed by any number of comments
you have made right here. Open mind? I think not. Your mind is so set on these
issues, no amount of evidence would convince you to willingly submit your life
to God.

Now, prove me wrong. Just honestly go on record right here that, if you could
see real and unquestionable proof that the Bible is absolutely true and reliable,
you would be willing to accept Jesus and submit your life to God.

So, open minded or not?  I'm betting you will obfuscate, change the subject or
otherwise avoid the issue. :-)

Anybody else want to take the challenge and demonstrate you are really open,
and only waiting for evidence? How about you, Alistair? :-)
```

```
> "Judson McClendon"  <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Howard, you cleverly left out Pete's comment that I was responding to, which
would have made clear you missed my point. I had claimed non believers like
Pete did have faith in their secular belief system. Pete objected, saying he
had no faith, and that his beliefs were entirely based on evidence. I never
made such a claim for myself, and never denied faith plays a part in my own
beliefs. I do have evidence, but faith is also a part of it.

My challenge was very simply an opportunity for Pete to demonstrate his
complete objectivity by publicly stating that, if the evidence to convince
him was there, he would accept Jesus and submit his life to God. Either he
is completely objective without faith, in which case he will make such an
admission, or he is not objective and does have faith, in which case he
won't, thus proving my point.
```

```
> :-)

And you didn't get the answer you were hoping for. Even so, had that been 
true, I would have said so.

Pete.
```

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:QF6mj.57608$Mu4.38888@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon"  <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> had no faith, and that his beliefs were entirely based on evidence.

If you are going to attribute words to me, please have the courtesy to quote 
them. I do not recall stating that I don't have faith, although I certainly 
do rely on evidence to form conclusions.

> I never
> made such a claim for myself, and never denied faith plays a part in my 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> him was there, he would accept Jesus and submit his life to God.

I have publicly stated here what I believe and why.

I never claimed "complete objectivity" EXCEPT when I was researching the 
subject over 30 years. During that time I was agnostic. Now I'm not.

You have no more right to require me to change my current belief, than I 
have to require you to convert to Hinduism, as Howard quite correctly 
pointed out.

> Either he
> is completely objective without faith,

Nope. You missed the boat... that was a long time ago.

> in which case he will make such an
> admission, or he is not objective and does have faith, in which case he
> won't, thus proving my point.

Nope. There are any number of reasons why I won't play your silly game and 
not just the ones you postulate.

I COULD be non-objective and rely on faith, as you suggest, but I could also 
be objective and still have faith. It is certainly true that I have "faith" 
in the conclusions I have arrived at, but this doesn't stop me re-evaluating 
what I believe from time to time. New evidence comes in all the time.

(Can you claim truthfully that YOU do that?)

Some tme ago I posted here the percentages I currently have against the 
questions to which I want answers. Your "challenge" requires a black and 
white answer which is anathema to the processes I follow, and what is even 
worse, it would require me to adhere to one strict and narrow cult which 
abnegates all responsibility and hangs it on a man tortured to death 2000 
years ago. I already explained why that is unacceptable to me.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 64)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-25T08:55:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pLmmj.59974$vt2.46225@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <iijep3956tp97gd6a77rbkjf1ke1fimj1c@4ax.com> <2SJlj.78567$L%6.3759@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <lhrep3tkarppnc6fc4plu0vj0nfbuueoc2@4ax.com> <CuKlj.78589$L%6.21482@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5vr57sF1np9flU1@mid.individual.net> <ua1mj.57463$Mu4.16153@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <ct8hp31mgk2uhkgppb78d2pqlfq60knfrl@4ax.com> <QF6mj.57608$Mu4.38888@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5vss2iF1o1titU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> faith, although I certainly do rely on evidence to form conclusions.

Okay, let's look at it another way, which I actually prefer. What I'm trying
to establish here is that athiests believe what they do because they want to
believe it, not because there is no evidence for God. At the bottom line,
having faith in something is being willing to cling to that belief, even in
the face of what may appear to be contradictory evidence or experience. It
would be impossible to do such a thing, unless you actually want to believe.
No one would continue to believe in something they did not want to believe,
in such a situation. Thus, the underlying principle of faith is that the
person exhibiting faith does so because they want to have that faith. This
is one popular premise upon which athiests denigrate faith in God, because
it is "based on faith," implying it is not also based on evidence. The
immediate statement you made that I was responding to is this:

Pete Dashwood:
> How about those of us with open minds? We don't particularly WANT to
> believe anything...but we have to recognize evidence whether we like it
> or not...

I accept that you probably believe that statement, but I do not accept it as
true for a moment. My contention is that you (along with all athiests and
non-believers), plain and simply, do not want to believe or accept the existence
of God. You can't accept the idea that there just might be a God out there to
Whom you may have to give account and be responsible, and you are unwilling to
submit your will and life to that God, even if the proof of His existence is
undeniable. In order to clearly establish that, I offered you a challenge to
easily and quickly prove you you really do not "WANT" (as you say above) the
outcome to be one way or another. Here is my challenge:

Judson McClendon:
> Now, prove me wrong. Just honestly go on record right here that, if you could
> see real and unquestionable proof that the Bible is absolutely true and reliable,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> otherwise avoid the issue. :-)

Am I right? Let's take a look at your responses below:

> I have publicly stated here what I believe and why.

Obfuscation, does not respond to the challenge. I was not questioning
your beliefs. I was asking you to verify your statement that you did
not "WANT" to believe one way or another.

> I never claimed "complete objectivity" EXCEPT when I was researching the subject over 30 years. During that time I was agnostic. 
> Now I'm not.

Still unresponsive. It's very simple, do you "WANT" to believe
one way or another, or not?

> You have no more right to require me to change my current belief, than I have to require you to convert to Hinduism, as Howard 
> quite correctly pointed out.

I'n not requiring anything of you! I'm simply asking you to verify
your statement that you don't "WANT" to believe one way or another,
and they you are simply basing your opinion on evidence.


>> Either he is completely objective without faith,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nope. There are any number of reasons why I won't play your silly game and not just the ones you postulate.

More obfuscation and mis-direction. :-)

> I COULD be non-objective and rely on faith, as you suggest, but I could also be objective and still have faith. It is certainly 
> true that I have "faith" in the conclusions I have arrived at, but this doesn't stop me re-evaluating what I believe from time to 
> time. New evidence comes in all the time.

Great! If your "faith" is based on evidence, not "WANT", then you should have
no problem responding to my challenge, where you get to specify the quality of
the new evidence presented to you. If you're still "re-evaluating", then here
is a wonderful opportunity to demonstrate that fact. :-)

> (Can you claim truthfully that YOU do that?)

I claim that I have evidence for the truth of the Bible and the existence of
God (some of which which I've summarized here on occasion), and that I have
faith in that. I never claimed, as you did, that I do not "WANT" to believe
one way or another. I want to believe in God. And I believe you "WANT" to
believe there is no God. But you can easily prove me wrong, which is what my
challenge is all about. :-)

> Some tme ago I posted here the percentages I currently have against the questions to which I want answers. Your "challenge" 
> requires a black and white answer which is anathema to the processes I follow, and what is even worse, it would require me to 
> adhere to one strict and narrow cult which abnegates all responsibility and hangs it on a man tortured to death 2000 years ago. I 
> already explained why that is unacceptable to me.

Not so; I'm not "requiring" anything of you. I'm simply asking you to
verify the truch of your statement by answering a simple challenge. If you
were presented with evidence that you yourself deemed absolutely and
uncategorically undeniable that the Bible is absolutely true, and that the
God of the Bible exists, would you accept that evidence and be willing to
accept Jesus and submit your life to God? I say that you would not, because
you do not want to submit to God, regardless of the evidence, and that is
why you invest your faith in athiesm. I.e. the evidence doesn't matter to
you. I even gave you a very easy and simple way to prove me wrong. So,
going to prove me wrong? :-)

I'm still betting you will continue to obfuscate, change the subject and
otherwise avoid giving a direct answer, because you are determined not to
submit your life to God, no matter what. But if you admit that, then you
show your position to be based entirely on what you want, not on evidence
*to any extent whatever*. :-)
```

```
On 25 Jan, 14:55, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> Okay, let's look at it another way, which I actually prefer. What I'm trying
> to establish here is that athiests believe what they do because they want to
> believe it, not because there is no evidence for God.

No atheist that I know of has started out by believing in atheism and
refuting god. We all started with a belief in our gods (I certainly
did and so have all members of my family) and had our eyes opened (by
the wickedness and inequity of the world). Eventually, we all
renounced god based upon the lack of objective evidence.

I consider myself to have been fortunate in that although both of my
parents were atheists neither attempted to stop me from studying
religion or attempted to force me in to atheism. Unfortunately, the
same can not be said of religious people (who would discourage atheism
on the point of a sword).

> At the bottom line,
> having faith in something is being willing to cling to that belief, even in
> the face of what may appear to be contradictory evidence or experience.

If there is irrefutable and reliable contradictory evidence, produce
it. Put up or shut up.

> Thus, the underlying principle of faith is that the
> person exhibiting faith does so because they want to have that faith.

That does not explain those who know no better and those who are
forced by threat of violence to stay faithful (Muslims who renounce
their faith in Islam and convert to christianity are stoned to death
in some parts of the world).

This
> is one popular premise upon which athiests denigrate faith in God, because
> it is "based on faith," implying it is not also based on evidence.

If there is irrefutable and reliable evidence, produce it. Put up or
shut up.


> > How about those of us with open minds? We don't particularly WANT to
> > believe anything...but we have to recognize evidence whether we like it
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> undeniable.

This is easily answered:
1. There is no irrefutable and reliable evidence for a deity of any
form.
2. If there were irrefutable and reliable evidence then there would be
no such thing as an atheist.
3. Atheists refute the existence of any deity because they do not see
any irrefutable or reliable evidence for the existence of such a
deity.

If there were any irrefutable and reliable evidence for a deity it
would be for the first deity (which in my chronology would be Amun)
and not for some Johnny-Come-Lately like God or Allah. If there were a
deity then just because your philosophy recognises a deity existent
for 4000 years does not make yours the TRUE GOD. Nor do the superior
numbers of christian believers (2.8 billion?) add weight to your
argument. After all, the TRUE GOD, AMUN, is known only to a faithful
few.

Now prove to me that the christian god:
a. exists,
b. is not some puffed up pathetic Midionite storm god in new togas,
c. any more valid than the TRUE GOD, AMUN.

Please do so without refernce to pagan texts such as the bible or the
scribblings of madmen in Rome.
```

```
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:pLmmj.59974$vt2.46225@bignews8.bellsouth.net...
> Okay, let's look at it another way, which I actually prefer. What I'm
trying
> to establish here is that athiests believe what they do because they want
to
> believe it, not because there is no evidence for God. At the bottom line,
> having faith in something is being willing to cling to that belief, even
in
> the face of what may appear to be contradictory evidence or experience. It
> would be impossible to do such a thing, unless you actually want to
believe.
> No one would continue to believe in something they did not want to
believe,
> in such a situation. Thus, the underlying principle of faith is that the
> person exhibiting faith does so because they want to have that faith. This
> is one popular premise upon which athiests denigrate faith in God, because
> it is "based on faith," implying it is not also based on evidence.

You've stated on many occasions that the principal thing required of a
Christian is "faith".  Mark Twain once defined ""faith" as "believing in
something that you KNOW ain't so".  "Faith" in the face of contradictions is
insecurity disguised by stubborn-ness.  "Faith" doesn't come into truth:
truth works whether we want it to or not.  If you're acquainted with quantum
mechanics you're aware of some of the seemingly impossible phenomena: that a
single photon can go through two slots at once, and id it goes through one
and if the observation is made in the right way, can be proved to have "made
the decision" AFTER it went through the slot.  On a grosser level, it's hard
to accept that simultaneity can't exist.  But all those statements are true.

Atheism is as hard to justify as theism.  Agnostics such as myself believe
that either there is sufficient evidence to settle a question, in which case
we accept it, or that there isn't, in which case we must shelve it as
unproved.  In either case the status could change if new evidence is brought
forward.  Now if you insist that that is "faith" - well have it your own way
because I can't see any other way to operate.

Your method of argument includes ignoring questions that you can't answer.
I'm still waiting for your proof of your assertion that the birth of Jesus
is one of the best-documented events in the history of the times.
<snip>>

 Not so; I'm not "requiring" anything of you. I'm simply asking you to
> verify the truch of your statement by answering a simple challenge. If you
> were presented with evidence that you yourself deemed absolutely and
> uncategorically undeniable that the Bible is absolutely true, and that the
> God of the Bible exists, would you accept that evidence and be willing to
> accept Jesus and submit your life to God? I say that you would not,
because
> you do not want to submit to God, regardless of the evidence, and that is
> why you invest your faith in athiesm. I.e. the evidence doesn't matter to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Judson McClendon       judmc@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)


At least one person - myself - accepts your challenge.  But bear in mind
that you have to convince me by MY standards, not yours.  So if you start by
saying "Assume God exists ..." then we part company immediately.  You've got
to define God - an you'll find that a very sticky wicket - and state your
postulates, and go from there.  (If one of your arguments is that the Bible
is the word of God, then so is the Koran).

PL
```

```
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:ua1mj.57463$Mu4.16153@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
>
> Now, prove me wrong. Just honestly go on record right here that, if you
could
> see real and unquestionable proof that the Bible is absolutely true and
reliable,
> you would be willing to accept Jesus and submit your life to God.
>
> So, open minded or not?  I'm betting you will obfuscate, change the
subject or
> otherwise avoid the issue. :-)
>
> Anybody else want to take the challenge and demonstrate you are really
open,
> and only waiting for evidence? How about you, Alistair? :-)
> --
> Judson McClendon       judmc@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)

Nice try, Judson.  Anybody who is honest and of a truly open mind would
readily agree that if there's real and unquestionable proof they'd accept
your theology.  I would!

The problem is that your committment requires faith.  To the rest of us,
truth is something that works whether we want it to or not.  Pi = 3.14159...
no matter what we'd like it to be, and no matter that we've been assured by
someone important that it's 3 (including some US state legislations that
have tried) and no matter how much faith we have in that person.  If Jesus
Christ comes tomorrow in all his glory and tells me that Pi = 3, then I will
have to check it out.

If you really expect or would like others to agree with you, you're going to
have to present your propositions and let us  test them according to our
ideas of truth.  If they work as you say, then you win.  I have prayed, most
sincerely, twice in my life.  Result: nothing at all.  I have seen
innumerable examples of people desperately calling on God to save them - in
the face of a flood or an avalanche or a volcanic eruption - and I'd bet
that GWB prayed that Hurricane Katrina would miss - again, nothing.  I
repeat - your ideas have to work whether people want them to or not before
sceptics will agree with you.

PL
```

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> The problem is that your committment requires faith.

No, the problem is that non belief *also* requires faith, which Pete denied,
and why I posted my challenge. My whole point is that *any* belief system,
including secular humanism and athiesm, are matters of faith. Pete's response
will clearly show if he is truly moved *only* by evidence.

> If you really expect or would like others to agree with you, you're going to
> have to present your propositions and let us  test them according to our
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> sceptics will agree with you.

My challenge wasn't meant to persuade Pete to my point of view, but to
demonstrate that he also has faith, in his secular belief system.

My broader point being that people don't believe because they don't want
to believe; not because there is "no evidence." My challenge to Pete should
also serve to help illustrate that fact.
```

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2008-01-24T15:24:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e26fc8af-2f29-47b3-bb9e-08308da84c59@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<5vidofF1map4vU1@mid.individual.net> <6YadnY3dZvealQnanZ2dnUVZ_s6mnZ2d@comcast.com> <36c13ca1-be87-4df5-84d9-8a2408c5c59d@q77g2000hsh.googlegroups.com> <0h2lj.3895$1f.630@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <fe826dda-dbd0-4fce-b644-9e4f9704b032@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XRalj.4185$1f.2025@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <rpvbp3t9m2u33mupjeurhivn74bv7bbe6k@4ax.com> <13pd80mte6ksld7@corp.supernews.com> <iijep3956tp97gd6a77rbkjf1ke1fimj1c@4ax.com> <2SJlj.78567$L%6.3759@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <lhrep3tkarppnc6fc4plu0vj0nfbuueoc2@4ax.com> <CuKlj.78589$L%6.21482@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5vr57sF1np9flU1@mid.individual.net> <ua1mj.57463$Mu4.16153@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <Ur3mj.25709$3b7.10642@newsfe23.lga> <pO6mj.57612$Mu4.30146@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
On 24 Jan, 20:46, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

> No, the problem is that non belief *also* requires faith, ...
> including secular humanism and athiesm.

I have no religious faith. I do believe that the process of science
will prevail and wipe out the last vestiges of superstition (which is
what religion is). It is a regret of mine that I will not live to see
that day.

As to belief/faith: it is very difficult to believe in anything at
all. The human being perceives, cogitates and reacts to stimuli. Those
stimuli are past perceptions (it takes a measurable amount of time for
the stimulus to pass along nerve fibres and for a response to be
observed) which means that no human is perceiving the current moment
in time. So, do you still exist? You can not ever be certain of that.

Further, although we have debated in this group artificial
intelligence (perhaps artificial stupidity would be a better concept)
and the simulation of mice and men's perecrania and found the current
technology lacking, there is nothing to indicate that we are not,
ourselves, simulations. Cut me and I bleed. Or do I? Am I just
imagining it? What is reality? You subscribe to one version and I
subscribe to another and they are not the same. We are both hedging
our bets.
```

```
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> that day.

Sounds like Voltaire. He said in a hundred years Christianity would cease
to exist. Last time I checked, there was a Bible organization using his old
house. God does, indeed, have a sense of humor.

> As to belief/faith: it is very difficult to believe in anything at
> all. The human being perceives, cogitates and reacts to stimuli. Those
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> our bets.

Well, my point is that we both have faith in our belief systems. Neither
of us are 100% totally objective evidence driven in our beliefs. The vast
majority of non-believers are unwilling to admit this, because they much
prefer to insist that they don't need faith, they have "evidence," while
insisting believers are somehow less valid because "they need faith."
And that is absolute rubbish! Atheists are just as faith based as believers,
and in fact, are believers in a religion: Atheism. Atheism is a man-made
"god" just as they insist believers have created.
```
