```


"I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13pd7071501q989@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> have a great positive effect on my mental outlook.

That's a very important point and one of the reasons I don't go through life 
expressing my personal belief to all and sundry (except in this forum, where 
I'm really just defending what I believe against bigotry, and anyway, it 
wasn't I who started the religious posts... :-))

The only saving grace I can see in religious faith, is that it CAN bring 
great comfort to many people and can definitely help in hard times. Like you 
say, no harm is done... BUT, this is only true at a personal level. Once the 
"organization" comes in with the dogmatic party line about what will and 
will not be believed, (and any questioning is dismissed as the work of 
Satan) things start to deteriorate, and if this progresses to the point 
where anyone not in the group is sinning against God and must be punished, 
then it becomes dangerous.

I see no need to evangelize atheism; the people capable of having it will 
embrace it anyway, and those who can't, won't. I'm OK with this; the number 
of people embracing or rejecting it has no influence whatsoever on what I 
believe.

I respect totally your right to your personal belief, Charles, and I would 
defend that right, whether I agree with you or not.

> However I do think that in other cases I need to be wary of this kind of 
> faith as it can mislead me and lead to hurt.  How many people want to 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to extract money etc.?

It's certainly true that humans have demonstrated a great propensity ot 
believe what we want to believe... :-)
>
> As far as my left brain has been able to acertain there is no scientific 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> now.

I suspect He found such conversations boring and gave up on them... :-)

>It would be nice if we all could get an inspiring lecture from Him from 
>time to time that gave us some real proof.

A lecture? Why not a conversation?

Many religious people would counter that God speaks to them every day... On 
the corner of my street there is a Salvation Army church (I think they do a 
great job and support them as much as I can) and they have a little board 
outside the church designed to catch the eye of people passing down the main 
road. I am always amused at the little aphorisms they post there and 
mentally formulate my own rejoinders to them. "If you feel detached from 
God, who moved?"  (that would be all us retinas... :-) no wonder the 
faithful aren't seeing so well...). Point is that one of the signs of a 
"healthy" relationship with God, is a feeling of closeness and ability to 
converse with Him. (there is a name for such a state in Pyschology; it's 
called "delusional"... :-))

> I want to believe that there is a loving God that cares for me and for us 
> all, but I also need to be aware of how that could leave me open to 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> about each belief.  I need to know what I believe and why I believe it.

You summed it up perfectly in that last sentence. That is the very nub of 
"belief". Even attempting to come to grips with that can make you feel 
better. You may never get completely satisfactory answers, but knowing you 
gave it your best shot, provides a degree of satisfaction. The alternative 
is to accept a complete set of someone else's answers, by "faith". For an 
enquiring mind, that it is not an attractive option. :-)

>If  I do not really understand these things about my beliefs then I am more 
>open to manipulation.  If I do understand them then I am less likely to 
>fall for the 72 virgins scam and I am less likely to follow a leader who 
>wants me to murder or to do something else incompatible with my beliefs.

From reading the above, I reckon you're in very good shape, Charlie... :-)

Pete.
```

```
>
> I too have had unpleasant experiences with organized religion.  They use guilt and other things to manipulate people for various 
> purposes especially to get money or obtain follwers.

Same here. Jesus Christ created the Church, but men created church
organizations. I believe the problem is not organization per se, but that
with organization come positions of authority, and people are drawn to
them for reasons having nothing to do with God. The Church has
always been at it's purest and best when it is under persecution. I think
the reason is that there is no reason to be involved then unless you're
the "real deal," to the point of being willing to suffer for it. Persecution
drives away the chaff, leaving the wheat.
```

```
>Christ did not create any church. That was the work of the disciples.

Not according to the words of Jesus in Matthew 16:18. If the Church
were created by humans, it would be no greater than any other purely
human work. Humans are the "living stones" used by Jesus, but Jesus
builds and maintains the Church, and God's Holy Spirit breathes life
into it. The Church is not visible to the eye; it is the collection of true
believers in Jesus Christ, and man did not create or build it, any more
than bricks "build" a house.

If you're talking about church demoninations, then yes, those are
created by men, which is one of the big problems with them. But when
you see the word "church" in the Bible, it is not talking about human
church denominations, but the collection of true believers above. There
is no Biblical basis for human sectarian churchs; the reverse in fact (see
1 Corinthians 3).
```

- **Message-ID:** `<LuJlj.78555$L%6.52619@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <6cb69101-c093-4bcf-92db-63a48c570d3c@l32g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <8offo31afaa6brlaleceh07cnrgus3dft9@4ax.com> <k1Qhj.51338$N67.8724@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <tctmo31b0g4irm0qrr1v1g63efepkkm83u@4ax.com> <DnMij.50469$Mu4.455@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <giopo3tac3o2p7nj9hqc15kvat0t2drdts@4ax.com> <zA5jj.44409$L27.15584@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <8223b353-670d-41ac-9d97-078e16a0007c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <02xjj.54816$_m.48118@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <mayjj.23822$m6.23695@newsfe18.lga> <frCdnX5Mgf8efhPanZ2dnUVZ_oimnZ2d@comcast.com> <5vch9dF1m6g8nU1@mid.individual.net> <ucudnRm8_ufjRA7anZ2dnUVZ_rSrnZ2d@comcast.com> <06a8a738-9358-4539-9384-112ffccdeab3@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <13p9cf3r2k0ff9a@corp.supernews.com> <566664b8-5090-424d-ab9e-ecda626b1fb9@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <13pd7071501q989@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
>
> Faith does not have to be blind.  Jesus was not against giving evidence for faith.  He did not say anything against Thomas for not 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> with my beliefs.

I enjoyed reading your post Charles. :-)

If you examine the Bible account of the times when God made His presence
obvious, it did not produce faith, love for God, or obedience. During the 40
years Israel followed Moses in the Sinai desert, God's presence was visible
and undeniable. They got into the desert by walking across the dry bottom of
the Red Sea, seeing the water heaped up on both sides like walls, and watched
as the water cascaded down on the Egyptian army, killing them to the last man
and horse. Every single day, except the sabbath, they picked up food (manna)
from the ground. Manna spoiled if kept overnight, except when kept overnight
for the sabbath. At any time they could look at the sky and see God's presence
in the form of a protective cloud by day and a glowing cloud by night. When
the cloud moved, they moved. Often they saw God come in to Moses' tent in the
form of a cloud, and Moses came out glowing like a space alien, which scared
everybody so much they asked Moses to wear a bag over his head. There were
also many other visible evidences, like the Shekinah Glory, which the people
saw go into the Tabernacle. On the occasions when the high priest entered the
Holy of Holies, they always tied a rope around his leg, because if he went in
there without proper sacrifices, they had to drag his dead body out with the
rope. When moving the Arc of the Covenant, if anyone touched it, even by
accident, they were instantly struck dead. There were absolutely no athiests
among those people in those days; they *knew* God was real. But during that
time, the people constantly complained and there were (IIRC) ten rebellions.
There is something perverse in the human heart that, when it does not want to
believe, it will ignore or "explain away" any evidence, and believe what it
wants.

Jesus said the most important thing is to love God with everything you have
(Matt 22:37). Apparently, when God makes His presence visible and obvious it
just doesn't produce that in rebellious humanity. I suspect that is one reason
why God does not make Himself more visibly obvious. I also suspect that those
40 years when God was clearly visible were (in part) to demonstrate that fact.
```

```
something - the sea, a mountain range, the end of the desert, etc., etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-23T19:42:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f2Slj.78806$L%6.56686@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <8offo31afaa6brlaleceh07cnrgus3dft9@4ax.com> <k1Qhj.51338$N67.8724@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <tctmo31b0g4irm0qrr1v1g63efepkkm83u@4ax.com> <DnMij.50469$Mu4.455@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <giopo3tac3o2p7nj9hqc15kvat0t2drdts@4ax.com> <zA5jj.44409$L27.15584@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <8223b353-670d-41ac-9d97-078e16a0007c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <02xjj.54816$_m.48118@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <mayjj.23822$m6.23695@newsfe18.lga> <frCdnX5Mgf8efhPanZ2dnUVZ_oimnZ2d@comcast.com> <5vch9dF1m6g8nU1@mid.individual.net> <ucudnRm8_ufjRA7anZ2dnUVZ_rSrnZ2d@comcast.com> <06a8a738-9358-4539-9384-112ffccdeab3@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <13p9cf3r2k0ff9a@corp.supernews.com> <566664b8-5090-424d-ab9e-ecda626b1fb9@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <13pd7071501q989@corp.supernews.com> <LuJlj.78555$L%6.52619@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <q5Qlj.10318$M24.4790@newsfe17.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> places they'd already seen.

They weren't lost. Where did you get that idea? They probably knew where
they were all the time. What happened was (see Numbers, chapters 13 & 14),
about six months after they left Egypt, God brought them to a place called
Kadesh, near Canan the "Promised Land," prepratory to entering there. They
first sent in people to scout out the land. When the scouts returned, they
all gave glowing reports of the richness of the land. But all the scouts
except two said the people were very large and too powerful to conquer.
Because of this, the people refused to enter Canan. God said (very loose
paraphrase) "Okay, but you will wander in the desert until every person
here who refused to enter Canan is dead." That they did, and it took 40
years. All that time they stayed under the visible cloud God provided.
When the cloud moved, they moved; when the cloud stayed, they stayed. So
God determined when they (following the cloud) finally got there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 44)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2008-01-24T14:24:45-08:00

- **References:** `<19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <s_ahj.7519$q36.2315@newsfe14.lga> <C7bhj.69398$L%6.15737@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6cb69101-c093-4bcf-92db-63a48c570d3c@l32g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <8offo31afaa6brlaleceh07cnrgus3dft9@4ax.com> <k1Qhj.51338$N67.8724@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <tctmo31b0g4irm0qrr1v1g63efepkkm83u@4ax.com> <DnMij.50469$Mu4.455@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <giopo3tac3o2p7nj9hqc15kvat0t2drdts@4ax.com> <zA5jj.44409$L27.15584@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <8223b353-670d-41ac-9d97-078e16a0007c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <02xjj.54816$_m.48118@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <mayjj.23822$m6.23695@newsfe18.lga> <frCdnX5Mgf8efhPanZ2dnUVZ_oimnZ2d@comcast.com> <5vch9dF1m6g8nU1@mid.individual.net> <ucudnRm8_ufjRA7anZ2dnUVZ_rSrnZ2d@comcast.com> <06a8a738-9358-4539-9384-112ffccdeab3@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com>`

```
There was a missionary to the natives in what would become Canada, named
Jean-Paul Brebeuf.  He was captured and tortured by one group - the Hurons,
I think, but that's not important;  nice things like having a red-hot knife
thrust through his foot.  After he got away or was released, he decided that
God had been punishing him (Brebeuf) for not loving God enough, and
professed himself pleased with the experience and the lesson and loved God
even more.  He was later killed by the natives.  Apparently he was an
arrogant sod.

The point here is that being convinced of something past the point of
reason, clinging to it at all costs, leads to extreme irrationality if not
madness.  "God may be demanding ... " sounds to me like that sort of
thought.

Frank Yerby, in the notes to his novel "Judas my brother" (a marvellous
story if you can withstand the emotionally exhausting way in which it's
written) comments that theology seems to him to be a form of insanity.  I'd
say he's on to something.

PL


Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:06a8a738-9358-4539-9384-112ffccdeab3@f47g2000hsd.googlegroups.com...
On 20 Jan, 23:30, LX-i <lxi0...@netscape.net> wrote:
> Another benefit from this was increased confidence. You can think ahead
> and decide some things, but until you're actually faced with making a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sacrifice your son to prove you love me sounds like insecurity to me.
Or maybe god is just a big tease?
```

```
tlmfru wrote:
>
> The point here is that being convinced of something past the point of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

Just the reverse. The human brain is wired to search for cause-and-effect. 
Were it not, humans would have become extinct a very long time ago.

That said, some things are logically inexplicable (what came before, what 
comes after...). To NOT search for these things is evidence sufficient of 
disease or defect.
```

```
On 17 Jan, 00:10, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> <Alistair.J.L.Macl...@googlemail.com> wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> sins.

I have no sin. What does it look like? Can I use it to buy food? Sin
is an outmoded religious concept that has no place in the modern world
except where religious figureheads wish to justify their evil
practices.

> I do not want to see you or anyone else be thrown into the lake
> fire for eternity.

I won't be. Nor will I gain 70 virgins.

> If I were drowning near you, and you had a life
> saver, would you throw it to me?

No. I would be in the water too and I'm not a strong swimmer.

> I believe you would. Why would I not
> want to see you saved from a much worse fate than drowning?

Don't my personal human rights come in to this? Don't I have a right
not to be saved? Why is it that religious groups spend a lot of their
time trying to save souls (another outmoded concept)? Leave people be.

> The fact
> that you and others see that attitude as bad says a lot about what's
> in your hearts, and why forgiveness is necessary.

Stupid first then bad. Also outmoded.

> God sent Jesus to
> save people from that horrible fate. The condemnation comes from
> rejecting that loving sacrifice. What would you say if Christians,
> having received such a precious free gift from God, keept it from you
> and failed to tell you about it?

Nothing more than I would say to the Mormons, Jehovahs, Amish, ....

> Worse than not throwing a life saver
> to a drowning person. But there's no cooercion, no force, no "jihad"
> if you don't accept it.

Coercion and force may be subtle: you'll roast on a spit in hell for
all eternity if you don't believe....

>
> You apparently hate even the concept of God because of how people have
> dealt with you.

Nothing to do with other people and how I have been treated. I hate
the concept of god because I see no evidence for it's veracity but I
see a lot of evidence for how that concept (in one or another of the
many variants of the gods delusion) has caused misery and hate and
been used to justify actions that no sane human would normally
countenance.

> As the Bibls says, the human heart is deceitful and
> wicked. But instead of blaming people for not obeying God and being
> completely honest with you and good to you, you blame God.

There is no god. There is a delusion. If I were a tad more cynical I
would probably suggest that there is a conspiracy by the Illuminati to
keep people believing in god so that they will be more easily ruled.
Certainly, up to the end of the second world war the god delusion held
the masses in check.

> Jesus died
> on a cruel Roman cross *for you*,

Did I ask him to do so? No. Then why should I be grateful that a self-
deluding Jew with a megalomaniacal attitude deliberately placed
himself in such a place and time as to be arrested, tortured and
murdered by the Romans? The man wanted fame. How else could his
actions be justified. That is a rhetorical question, I do not require
an answer.

> just as much as He did for any other
> human. Nobody ever showed as much love for you as Jesus has. He made
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> free will. :-)

Bunkum. Sorry Judson but this is tosh.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:02xjj.54816$_m.48118@bignews4.bellsouth.net...
> <Alistair.J.L.Maclean@googlemail.com> wrote:
>>"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> free will. :-)

I believe you are doing what you are doing here from pure and decent 
motives, Judson.

That doesn't mean I won't jump on it when I consider it becomes dangerous, 
or simply tiresome, but, I do accept you are not one of the bad guys, and 
your intentions are generous...

I have a number of Christian friends who have expressed concern for my 
mortal soul and they do so out of kindness.  (Some of them even pray for me; 
I don't believe it has done me any harm :-))

For me the problems with your argument are the ambivalences in it, the 
fundamental position that people are evil (I believe exactly the opposite), 
and the fact that it is based on scripture which is, to say the least, 
"inconsistent", despite your protestations to the contrary.

Personally, I do not "hate even the concept of God" or even the concept of 
Religion.  (Although I confess, I'd be happy if the world got over it and 
proceeded without the need for a "spiritual crutch")

What I hate is bigotry, dogma, superior priests, "elders", or preachers who 
claim the right to interpret scripture because I am not capable of doing so 
myself, and anything Religious that advocates torture or death for not 
believing,  for questioninf, or thinking differently from the "Party Line".

Outside of that, I have no problem with it... :-)

Pete.
```

```
On Fri, 18 Jan 2008 13:44:46 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Sat, 19 Jan 2008 09:11:37 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
><dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>or The Bible or Free Enterprise or their Computer Language.


  Only the individual who has come to terms with his self can have a
  dispassionate attitude toward the world. Once the harmony with the
  self is upset, and a man is impelled to reject, renounce, distrust or
  forget his self, he turns into a highly reactive entity. He cannot
  stand apart, poised and self-sufficient, but has to attach himself
  wholeheartedly to one side or another.

  Faith in a holy cause is to a considerable extent a substitute
  for the lost faith in ourselves.

  A man is likely to mind his own business when it is worth minding.
  When it is not, he takes his mind off his own meaningless affairs by
  minding other people's business. .. In running away from ourselves
  we either fall on our neighbor's shoulder or fly at his throat.

-- Eric Hoffer
```

```
On Jan 12, 6:59 am, "Judson McClendon"

> It is also true that the documentary evidence for Biblical
> events is vastly greater than for any other events of
> comparable antiquity.

While there may be some 'documentary evidence' for many biblical
events that  does not indicate that Jehovah was anything more than a
local warlord that was deified by his followers, as was the style of
the time.

> For example, there is more evidence of Jesus crucifixion and resurrection
> than that Julius Caesar ever existed.

That is complete crap. There is _vastly_ more evidence for Julius
Caesar than the purely annecdotal 'crucifixion and resurrection'.

The several tons of manufactured 'pieces of the true cross' are only
evidence of the gullibility of believers.

> There are vastly more manuscripts,
> and they date from much closer to the actual events,
>  than any other historical events of comparable
> antiquity.

While there are many manuscripts that have been preserved they do not
'prove' divinity in any way.

The Mormons believe that god gave gold tablets to Joseph Smith. The
Rastafarians believe that Rastas was/is god and can point to their
writings which support this. Hindus have their Rigveda which predates
any Jewish writings.
```

```
news:78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net...

> >You're a drowning camel grasping at straws to explain inconsistencies

> It is also true that the documentary evidence for Biblical events is
> vastly greater than for any other events of comparable antiquity. For
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is no point in engaging in discussions about the results achieved (or
not) by faith as it satisfies no-one and results only in hardening of
positions.  Therefore I will just challenge you on the above paragraph.  In
a previous long disputation in this ng, you made the same claim; I
challenged you then to substantiate it and you didn't.  So let's try again:
I know of the Bible and the history by Josephus as more-or-less contemporary
writings.  To make such a statement you must know of a source that lists
said documents apart from these two: what is it?  (After all, somebody
responded to my repeated proddings about a list of expanding glaciers;
surely you can do the same!)  This is a part of your explanation that ought
to be subject to fairly objective analysis - so I urge you to give us your
references.

Respectfully,
PL
```

- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com> <19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8ol5o39rpdcjq6es2n32j8bbt6gltibkee@4ax.com> <5ug8jsF1hf0vaU1@mid.individual.net> <31563764-0489-40cd-8b99-a26901a37575@k39g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <207583ce-21d4-42ea-abc7-7ba352a75b1f@i72g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <19be4785-722f-449d-a994-f73ca6cd1b85@k2g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <s_ahj.7519$q36.2315@newsfe14.lga> <C7bhj.69398$L%6.15737@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6cb69101-c093-4bcf-92db-63a48c570d3c@l32g2000hse.googlegroups.com> <78Ohj.51287$N67.19283@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```
On 11 Jan, 17:59, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> Unlike yourself Alistair, I have spent considerable time on both sides of
> this argument.

I, too, have spent considerable time on both sides. I started to
question the dogma of christianity when I was 11 years old but did not
become faithless untill I was at least 18. During that period I spent
some time studying christianity (I was forced to at school and still
despise the system that allows Jews and Muslims to opt out but doesn't
allow the same rights to atheists and agnostics) and other religions
(I rather like Buddhism but just can not bring myself to believe in a
spirit or miniature white elephants). Around the age of 21 I more or
less gave up on religion, seeing it as one of the worlds' forces for
evil but did not declare myself to be an atheist until well after
hitting 40.

> I was 27 years old when I accepted Jesus. I avidly read
> science fiction from a very early age, subscribed to Scientific American
> from the time I was 10, studied physics and math at university, and was
> thoroughly inculcated in the humanism you espouse.

I don't think that I have ever advocated humanism. I consider it an
ugly term. I just think that the world would be a better place if we
ditched superstition (I know people who claim to have seen demons. I
don't know what they had been smoking at the time)

> I had never studied
> the Bible, and like you,

The bible makes perfect sense to me. It contains religious tracts
which detail the perceived history of the world (as seen by people
from around 4000 years ago who could not possibly have been able to
see the world for what it really is). As a historical document it is a
wonderful work; albeit one that is inconsistent (two accounts of
creation, inconsistent genealogies, improper translation (the Red Sea
was the Reed Sea), contradictory accounts of the crucifixion of
Christ, highly selectively edited (see the Agnostic Gospels) and
propaganda). As an historical document it details about three weeks
worth of Christ's life leaving questions as to where he was for the
rest of his life. I presume you know of the Kashmiri tradition that
Christ was in fact a wondering Indian scholar?

> it did not make much sense to me when I read any
> of it. It never made sense to me that Christians would connect verses
> from different parts of the Bible and put them together to make a point.

It is called extrapolation. That is how the injunction to farmers to
not cast seed upon stoney ground can be interpreted by narrow-minded
bigots as an injunction against homosexuals. Humans are great at
twisting stories to suit their own ends.

> What I could not see, what I did not know, and could not realize, is that
> the Bible *cannot* be understood *through the intellect alone*,

To believe the bible, verbatim, requires only blind faith. I have
encountered many people who were committed fundamentalist christians
and they had one thing in common: deeply flawed personalities. I'm
sorry if that hurts.

> any more
> than color can be understood through the intellect alone.

As one who is colour blind and has visual impairments to boot, I have
wondered how colour can be accurately described in a meaningful
fashion to people who perceive only grays. Answer: specify the
wavelength of the light. No other description has any meaning.

> Unless a person
> has color sense, you can describe color to them until your tongue falls
> out, and they *will not get it*. You either can see color, in which case
> explanation is unnecessary, or you can't see it,

An object has form (eg a cube). It's properties and form can be
described and so too can the colour property of an object. If one were
blind such a property would have little meaning but would still allow
categorisation of the object according to the colour property.


> I did not come to Christ because I believed the Bible; I hadn't read much
> of it. I asked Jesus into my life because I had a lot of serious problems
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> them.

I have no doubt but that Muslims will say the same about their
religion. Only one of you can be right. As both religions perceive
much the same it is logical to conclude that neither system is right.

> This Presence is real and tangible to the

self-deluding

> Christian. You can clearly
> sense the presence of the Holy Spirit.

Can it be measured or perceived by others? What properties does it
have? Is your experience the same as others of your faith? ....

I don't need nor do I require answers to these questions. I merely
wish to point out that your experience, although real to you, is
subjective and is no more real to another person as the ability to
perceive colour is to a blind man. I can describe colour with
reference to the real world but your experience is not verifiable.

> And as promised in the Bible, the
> Holy Spirit opens your understanding to the Bible, just like having color
> vision opens color to you.

I have already pointed out that my colour vision is imperfect. Perhaps
your interpretation of the bible and acceptance of Christ's spirit is
similarly marred?

> I began to have an enormous appetite for the
> Bible, and read it avidly every day. I began to see that the Bible really
> is one, integrated, completely consistent work.

You are not paying attention to the writings in this newsgroup.
Inconsistencies in the bible have been documented briefly here but
more information is available elsewhere. The bible is not one
integrated consistent work. It is a collection of scriptures that by
omitting other scriptures (see the Agnostic Gospels) have been brought
together to represent the views of the Roman Catholic church of about
400 ad.

> I remember the astonishment
> I felt when I first realized that I could now *see* why various Scriptures
> could be taken from different places in the Bible to make a single point.

Extrapolation to prove any point you care to make.

> It is because the Bible *is* one whole consistent work, and when the Holy
> Spirit gives one the understanding of it, it isn't arbitrary at all.
> Studying the Scriptures, I can see things myself, that are the same things
> Christians in other parts of the world also see.

There is no astonishment in that. Nazis the world over see the same
consistent message in Mein Kampf.

> It is amazing to meet
> Christians from different cultures; it's like meeting family or dear old
> friends you didn't know you had. Some people, particularly non-believers,
> usually only accent the differences; but the commonality of belief and
> worldview is astonishing.

How many different christian groups are there? Let me count the ways:
Roman Catholic
Greek Orthodox
Russian Orthodox
Anglican
Baptist
Methodist
Anabaptist
Seven Day thingummybobs (sorry can't remember their ending)
Calvinist
Lutheran
Christian Scientists
Plymouth Brethren
ad infinitum ad nauseum.

> Like different paintings by the same painter.
>
> Over time, as I studied the Bible and some Christian history, I learned
> that there are fewer things in the Bible that are criticized as error by
> non-believers today, than in the past.

You mean that archaeologists are able to confirm some of the
historical context of the writings. Confirming a few points (such as
the Battle of Khadesh) does not make much in-road into the host of
inaccuracies found within the bible.

Could you tell me one thing: why do christians go to the wrong
Bethlehem to visit the "manger" wherein christ was allegedly born?

> Over time the Bible is increasingly
> attested by other sources to be correct, not the reverse.

No, some references are confirmed. Other archaeological evidence is
deliberately hidden because it would deny the bible (see the Naked
Archaeologist series).

> No purely human
> book would exhibit this characteristic.

Try the Koran. Try the hieroglyphics on the temple of Karnakh. Try the
Hindu scriptures. Try the Sikh scriptures. If your faith is the only
true faith then these others must be purely human books and yet they
too are being proven correct.

> If the Bible were purely human,
> therefore fallible, then in such a large work, over time the number of
> known fallacies would increase, not decrease.

If the bible were purely the word of god then it would have been
written with one voice/hand and would be consistent in manner
throughout. Bible scholars (better christians than yourself)
acknowledge that the individual books are the written works of a
number of scholars with some books exhibiting the hand of more than
one person in their texts.

> For example, until (IIRC)
> circa 1930, the only known reference to Sargon II, king of Assyria, was
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> accounts.

Really? You have already shown how christians are prepared to take
unrelated texts within the bible and extrapolate them to prove/
disprove/form the basis of.. some other belief. My recollection of the
gospels is that they are contradictory and most certainly not
contemporary.

>
> Are there passages in the Bible that are difficult to reconcile? Yes,
> there are. There are several reasonable explanations for this. For one
> thing, we do not have the original manuscripts, and scribal errors

Surely the word of god can not be mistranslated? And remember, the
bible is a consistent whole but now you are saying that it is
difficult to reconcile passages (or in other words inconsistent).

> and
> changes have definitely been introduced. For another, as shown in the
> apparent Gospel chronology problems, our knowledge is incomplete. As we
> continue to study the Bible, more and more of the seeming contradictions
> are resolved, as one would expect in a genuine divinely inspired book.

Or perhaps, science is able to show us the small kernel of truth
underlying some of the bible's historical base.

> In addition, there have been a lot of "jewels" uncovered in the digging
> done to resolve the apparent contradictions. So many that theologians
> have suggested God may have intentionally permitted those problems just
> to promote deeper digging in some areas.

Just as god planted dinosaur skeletons in the rocks to encourage
palaeontology?

>
> It is also true that the documentary evidence for Biblical events is
> vastly greater than for any other events of comparable antiquity.

The Hindu scriptures document events that pre-date the biblical events
by millennia.


> For
> example, there is more evidence of Jesus crucifixion

To whit:
1. The bible;
2. A (singular) reference to Jesus in Roman documents;
3. References in Jewish documentation to christ.

Three weeks worth of a man's life. Not much to show for 33 years on
the earth.


> and resurrection

No evidence at all. Unless you mean the footprints in Kashmir
(complete with misplaced nail holes).

> than that Julius Caesar ever existed.

Codswallop.

> There are vastly more manuscripts,
> and they date from much closer to the actual events, than any other
> historical events of comparable antiquity.

I wouldn't count multiple copies of the same document. As for the
vastly more manuscripts: which ones?

> For example, there are only
> (IIRC) nine extant copies of Caesar's "Gallic Wars", and the oldest
> dates over 900 years after the events.

Don't we count the contemporary sculptures of Caesar? His conquests?

> There are literally tens of
> thousands of ancient Biblical manuscripts, and some of the New Testament
> manuscripts date from about 30 years after the events.

40 years at least. Unusually long-lived these apostles for a
persecuted sect. And I do not recall reading that any of the gospels
had been written by the idividuals whose names they bear.

>
> (Aside: anyone interested in a legal defense of Jesus crucifixion and
> resurrection should read a little book "The Testimony of the Evangelists"
> by Simon Greenleaf. Check out Greenleaf's credentials while your at it.)

Testimony of the Evangelists? That is like giving a thief the right to
re-write all witness statements bearing evidence against himself.

>
> So, the only thing I, or any other Christian, can do to answer critics
> such as yourself is tell you what we know from experience.

One man's subjective experience is another man's hallucination.

> What you do
> with that information is up to you. You can either believe it, accept
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> that's the bottom line. :-)

No contest. Rejection it is then.
```

```
On Jan 8, 3:07 pm, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> On Tue, 1 Jan 2008 14:55:52 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz>
> wrote:
…[68 more quoted lines elided]…
> evolution.

You miss the point entirely. Changes _are_ evolution.  The question
would be whether a particular change is due to 'sexual selection' or
'natural selection' or 'mutation' or 'genetic variation' or all of
these.

If you want to attribute it to some supernatural space pixie then you
will never be able to make sense of the world.


> >Today the birds are the only descendants related to dinosaurs, and the
> >large animals are mammals.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> were warm or cold blooded and whether some (all) laid eggs.

That is correct. It appears that many dinosaurs were warm blooded at
least to some degree. The evidence is that some had many details that
are in birds today. Certainly most dinosaurs laid eggs, as birds still
do.

Some fish also are warm blooded, such as Tuna, in that they can
generate heat and regulate this.

Some types of snakes lay eggs, some have live young. I don't know of
any dinosaurs that didn't lay eggs but it is possible.

What was your point ?

> I don't
> follow the issues closely enough to evaluate the snippets I have heard
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> able to bring about the creation and modify it.
Well, there's a cop-out if ever I saw one.


> >> Both explanations require
> >> assumptions which may have absolutely no basis in fact.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> the explanations are reasonable but can be completely off the mark.

What is it that you think are 'assumptions' and 'extrapolations' ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>

```
Fur, a mammalian feature can be found on duck-billed platypii but on
no other "reptile". It is possible that fur could have evolved on dbps
separately to mammals (convergent evolution) but it is more likely
that the pre-mammalian feature of incubating eggs externally was
retained whilst the dbps evolved a minor route change to the nerve
passage.
```

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-01-08T23:55:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eie8o31j1f8cn2uprbeo203aroe3mk5bb8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com> <19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8ol5o39rpdcjq6es2n32j8bbt6gltibkee@4ax.com> <40e02849-5e7d-43df-b50a-813ababa8253@p69g2000hsa.googlegroups.com> <QFDgj.20304$m6.6912@newsfe18.lga>`

```
On Mon, 7 Jan 2008 23:15:04 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:


>The current opinion on duck-billed platypuses is that they are reptiles with
>mammalian features.  This is decided on the basis of how some nerve passes
>through the skull (I'm not making this up!)

There is a whole lot of DNA research on monotremes i.e. platypus and echidna (4 species).
They are unique in having 10 sex chromosomes (9 for echidna). No other animals have more
than 4, the vast majority have 2. Only plants have numbers close to 10. 

Mammals have an XY system in which the male determines the sex of offspring.  Birds have a
ZW system in which the female determines the sex. Platypuses are like mammals in that the
male determines the sex, XYXYXYXY being male and XXXXXXXXXX being female. However, at the
other end of 4 out of 5 X chromosomes are bird-like Z sex-determining genes, and
distributed up and down the chromosomes are more bird genes than mammal genes. 

Reptiles use X/Y, Z/W and a third system that is temperature sensitive. On close
examination, reptilian sex genes are different from mammals and birds. Monotremes do not
have any reptile-like sex genes. Their X genes also do not look like those of therians
(marsupials, rodents), which is where they are usually classified. For more on this see:
http://genomebiology.com/2007/8/11/R243

Another research report says platypus genes look close to those of a marsupials (opossum,
kangaroo), but not to rodents. 
http://www.pubmedcentral.nih.gov/articlerender.fcgi?tool=pubmed&pubmedid=9720117

The reason for so much interest is that monotremes are like living fossils. They have
evolved much slower than other animals, thus give a glimple of what mammal and bird genes
looked like during the early Jurassic, 160 million years ago, rather than the late
Jurrassic, 140 MYA, when marsupials appeared.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Racial superiority / Intelligent design was Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-08T08:01:04-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zmLgj.43554$Mu4.31094@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fimmjs$r0q$1@reader1.panix.com> <5r9b19F13a7pvU1@mid.individual.net> <vrcln3p4sco8lk58d4avqp101lcnlje5np@4ax.com> <19b591ae-25ef-4159-9ca5-21e3f1720867@d21g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <8ol5o39rpdcjq6es2n32j8bbt6gltibkee@4ax.com> <40e02849-5e7d-43df-b50a-813ababa8253@p69g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
> You miss the point entirely. Changes _are_ evolution.

Not necessarily. The ability to adapt to changing conditions could have
been built in by an intelligent Creator, quite different from any evolutionary
process. We do the same thing in programs that are specifically designed
to accomodate to various conditions. Such design pardigms would be
purposely limited in scope, unlike "evolution", which is uncontrolled and
unlimited, by definiton. E.g. a "horse type" creature might adapt into
Clydesdales, poneys and donkeys, but never into a dog or giraffe.
```

```
On Jan 9, 3:28 am, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
> On Mon, 7 Jan 2008 19:46:30 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz>
> wrote:
…[73 more quoted lines elided]…
> The change between egg laying and carrying in the womb is drastic.

No, it is progressive. Some types of snakes have made part of that
change. Even some types of fish and of insects have live young.
Marsupials are one intermediate form where the egg hatches internally
but the carrying is external with milk glands.

> The
> change between single cell reproduction and sexual reproduction is
> drastic.

No, it is progressive, evolutionary. Single cell organisms will also
merge which will give genetic diversity.

> In both cases finding a plausible transition is difficult.
>

Only if you don't know how to look.


> >If you want to attribute it to some supernatural space pixie then you
> >will never be able to make sense of the world.
>
> You should read more science fiction.

Fantasy makes more sense ?
```

```
>> People like everything to fit into nice neat categories.   The
>> universe doesn't.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>'neat category' but shouldn't be ?

I wasn't talking about you specifically.  I like to put things into
categories, it makes them easier to understand - or it makes them
easier to think I understand them.    It can be useful to recognize
that things don't fit into categories as nicely as we would like.

This is obvious in such categories as race and religion.   But it even
applies to whether something is a computer language, or life kingdoms.
```

```
On 30 Dec 2007, 04:00, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
> Doug Miller <spamb...@milmac.com> wrote in message
>  So: let's block the strait of Gibraltar.  (Great for
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Armed with a degree in Marine Biology (which is not Oceanography but
did include that subject matter but knowledge has no meaning in this
group) I would be interested in knowing how blocking off the Med would
cause the Gulf Stream to deviate. The Mediterranean Outflow Current
would cease to exist and would cease to warm the NW coast of Scotland
(where Mediterranean species of plankton can be found). That would
cause a drop in land temperatures in Scotland perhaps reducing it to
the same range as experienced in Canada.

> 2) The Mediterranean, over a long period of time, would become shallower due
> to the excess of evaporation over input (from the Nile, the Black Sea and
> other sources).  It would become saltier and a large area of sea-bottom
> would become dry.

Previously the Med has dried out (there are huge salt deposits being
mined in (I think) Sicily (but it might be Sardinia).

> This in turn would change the flow of the lower reaches
> of all the rivers that empty into the Med.

They would still empty in to the Med. And dry up.
```

```
On 21 Dec 2007, 15:58, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
> In article <anzaj.8161$E01.1...@newsfe22.lga>, "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is also a hotter denser more saline current which flows out of
the Mediterranean, turns northwards and surfaces off of the NW coast
of Scotland. The net result is that the Med is more saline than the
Atlantic. The Med has also, in Doc's golden hayze, dried up completely
in the past.
```

```
>> > travel through negative time (pi-mesons and even electrons under certain circumstances...)), and because you evade the question 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> violate our understanding by travelling backwards through time.

Only because we assume that relativity precludes simultaniety. That
is an assumption I have always questioned, even when I was studying
physics at university, and apparently I was right. Just as observed
"errors" in classical Newtonian physics led to relativity and quantum
mechanics, there will no doubt be further observed "errors" until
even further refinements explain them. After all, this is the way
science works. Newton wasn't "wrong", he was "incomplete". We know
that relativity and quantum mechanics are incomplete, and there is no
unified field theory yet. Hopefully continued study of these apparent
contradictions in quantum mechanics will lead to a unified theory.
Einstein would be pleased. But IMHO, you can take it to the bank that
when the dust clears, causality will remain. It had better, because
causality is the fundamental principle on which science is based. You
can't have a "how" or a "why" without causality.
```

```
>But IMHO, you can take it to the bank that
>when the dust clears, causality will remain. It had better, because

to infer that there is a Being that wasn't caused.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 45)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-08T11:44:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8EOgj.43654$Mu4.17760@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fi4lm31hp3ui83bm2kpk3j1m80i7r4b2ed@4ax.com> <7Wwaj.46370$L%6.29618@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <009lm3t47v09v8qbti0ies5he30edeen98@4ax.com> <0Cyaj.46414$L%6.11571@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <85glm3p5fbg9qin4i0hpig28l9veg722a2@4ax.com> <wYzaj.46462$L%6.31493@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5t0melF1b47hjU1@mid.individual.net> <RkNaj.27584$_m.13063@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <41104d13-b491-410f-a32d-0446c19df7a1@e10g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XQLgj.43566$Mu4.3529@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <k747o3t4986b0926bnpf43lblevm1ph2nr@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Causality appears to work, although there is no math requiring it.

Wow, Howard, that is one of the most casually misleading statements I
have ever seen! Consider: if the math "works" it's because there is a
cause that the math represents. If there's no cause, there is no correlation,
no math, zip. Things happen, no reason, no rhyme, no "how" no "why".
Science *implies* cause. Without cause, there *can be no* science,
because there *is no* why or how. Causality is *necessary* to a degree
nothing else is necessary.

> However it doesn't make sense to use this observation about causality
> to infer that there is a Being that wasn't caused.

Duh! It's very simple, Howard. Here's one proof, of many possible:

1. If everything is caused, then there can be nothing, unless there is
   Something/Someone That/Who is outside causality and created everything
   else that exists => First Cause.

2. In the history of mankind, there has never been an observation of any
   creation that was not caused by intelligent aplication of energy =>
   First Cause is Intelligent.

3. Intelligence has never been observed in enything other than a being
   => First Cause is an Intelligent Being.

Q.E.D.

You are free to suppose differently, but you have no logical basis and no
observation or testable hypothesis on which to do so. Occam's Razor
says that, if there is a relatively simple explaination, you're done;
don't add complexity unless logically necessary.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 46)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-08T12:53:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e2l7o3pvo02kkn2e78ig5se3c8oi233297@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<009lm3t47v09v8qbti0ies5he30edeen98@4ax.com> <0Cyaj.46414$L%6.11571@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <85glm3p5fbg9qin4i0hpig28l9veg722a2@4ax.com> <wYzaj.46462$L%6.31493@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5t0melF1b47hjU1@mid.individual.net> <RkNaj.27584$_m.13063@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <41104d13-b491-410f-a32d-0446c19df7a1@e10g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XQLgj.43566$Mu4.3529@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <k747o3t4986b0926bnpf43lblevm1ph2nr@4ax.com> <8EOgj.43654$Mu4.17760@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Tue, 8 Jan 2008 11:44:38 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>Duh! It's very simple, Howard. Here's one proof, of many possible:
>
>1. If everything is caused, then there can be nothing, unless there is
>   Something/Someone That/Who is outside causality and created everything
>   else that exists => First Cause.

If there is something outside of causality, then causality can't be a
requirement for everything.

>2. In the history of mankind, there has never been an observation of any
>   creation that was not caused by intelligent aplication of energy =>
>   First Cause is Intelligent.

There are lots of observations of creation by non-intelligent causes.

>3. Intelligence has never been observed in enything other than a being
>   => First Cause is an Intelligent Being.

Quick definitions (being)

# noun:   the state or fact of existing

I don't think that word applies for things that exist outside of
causality.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 46)_

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-08T14:24:53-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M_Qgj.30178$pq.17086@newsfe24.lga>`
- **References:** `<fi4lm31hp3ui83bm2kpk3j1m80i7r4b2ed@4ax.com> <7Wwaj.46370$L%6.29618@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <009lm3t47v09v8qbti0ies5he30edeen98@4ax.com> <0Cyaj.46414$L%6.11571@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <85glm3p5fbg9qin4i0hpig28l9veg722a2@4ax.com> <wYzaj.46462$L%6.31493@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5t0melF1b47hjU1@mid.individual.net> <RkNaj.27584$_m.13063@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <41104d13-b491-410f-a32d-0446c19df7a1@e10g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XQLgj.43566$Mu4.3529@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <k747o3t4986b0926bnpf43lblevm1ph2nr@4ax.com> <8EOgj.43654$Mu4.17760@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:8EOgj.43654$Mu4.17760@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> cause that the math represents. If there's no cause, there is no
correlation,
> no math, zip. Things happen, no reason, no rhyme, no "how" no "why".
> Science *implies* cause. Without cause, there *can be no* science,
> because there *is no* why or how. Causality is *necessary* to a degree
> nothing else is necessary.
>


You are getting into some perilously difficult territory here.  There is any
amount of mathematics that appears to have no connection with the "real"
world (and I'll let anyone else supply examples): yet it "works" because it
takes a set of axioms and postulates and proceeds to generate conclusions in
accordance with certain rules.  How does this relate to "cause"?

A simpler example: if math (as a subset of science), as you say, depends on
"cause": can you define "one"?  No, you can't, neither can anyone else.  Yet
we all know what it means!  This is an example of a concept which is crucial
to our understanding of the world and is perhaps crucial to its very
existence: yet it seems to exist in and of itself, without reference or
meaning.

Another example: Godel's work has proved that we can't even prove that the
entire structure of mathematics actually works - or, more precisely, that it
is wholly and completely consistent and contains no statements that can't be
proved or disproved.

There has always been a very deep indecision between Platonic ideals and the
real world.  There's a string of five 5's somewhere in the expansion of pi -
a hundred thousand or so decimal places out.  Did it exist before pi was
calculated that far?

Things seem to be able to exist without our knowing of them; certainly they
don't have to have a cause.

PL
```

- **References:** `<fi4lm31hp3ui83bm2kpk3j1m80i7r4b2ed@4ax.com> <7Wwaj.46370$L%6.29618@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <009lm3t47v09v8qbti0ies5he30edeen98@4ax.com> <0Cyaj.46414$L%6.11571@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <85glm3p5fbg9qin4i0hpig28l9veg722a2@4ax.com> <wYzaj.46462$L%6.31493@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5t0melF1b47hjU1@mid.individual.net> <RkNaj.27584$_m.13063@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <41104d13-b491-410f-a32d-0446c19df7a1@e10g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <XQLgj.43566$Mu4.3529@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <k747o3t4986b0926bnpf43lblevm1ph2nr@4ax.com> <8EOgj.43654$Mu4.17760@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <M_Qgj.30178$pq.17086@newsfe24.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> accordance with certain rules.

You're correct, of course, but this isn't what I was referring to.

> How does this relate to "cause"?

Not at all. I apparently didn't express myself clearly. Perhaps I should have
said "no related math" or "there is no correlation between math and the real
world without causality." Better? :-)

> <big snip - don't disagree, just not pertinent>
>
> Things seem to be able to exist without our knowing of them; certainly they
> don't have to have a cause.

My point is that the only connection between math and the physical world *is*
causality. I.e. if the math accurately models the causal mechanism, then it
"works" for the real world. If there is no "cause" in the real world, then
there cannot be math to model what doesn't exist. There is no connection
between one thing and another without causality. Causality is the fundamental
mechanism through which any possible physical order could exist. If phenomenon
"just happened" without cause, there could be only chaos. There would be,
could be, no "how" or "why" or "when", because causality is the only connecting
tissue there is. Causality is *the* principal principle. Without causality,
there is no principle. ;-) How could there be natural law without causality?
Law? Effect? Without causality, there is nothing, no physical universe could
exist without causality. How could it exist with no rules, no limits, no
processes, no correlation between anything that existed or happened in it? If
it could exist, it would be meaningless chaos to the highest degree. Causality
is the one principle that *must* exist for anything else to exist in a physical
universe, even the physical universe itself.
```

```
existed before the big bang, perhaps a big collapse/compression of a previous universe.
```

```
On 9 Jan, 01:23, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Tue, 8 Jan 2008 15:13:01 -0600, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> >My point is that the only connection between math and the physical world *is*
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't think that modern physics considers time to exist but rather
that it is a construct of our minds based upon remembering the past,
experiencing the present and, by extrapolation, predicting a future.
If time does not exist then there is no need for it to start at the
moment of the big bang. However, as we do not appear to be able to
observe any moment of 'time' before the big bang then, to all intents
and purposes, 'time' begins with the big bang (and ends with the big
crunch).
```

```
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:5umkenF1ima7tU1@mid.individual.net...
>
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> "There exists a value such that when a member of the set is multiplied
with
> it, the result is unchanged."
>
> >

That need not be "one".  And you have to add a whole lot of definitions and
axioms and postulates to make the statement meaningful.


What I mean is a definition of the quantity "one", so that we can tell "one"
from "two" a priori, without invoking any operation or transformation.  My
perhaps imprecise impression of the "official" definition of "three" is
something like - Let A be a set with elements a, b, c; such that any element
selected from the set A is a, b, or c.  Then the cardinality of that set is
three.  Works fine as long as a d doesn't get somehow into the set.  Notice
that the concept "three" is implicit in the definition no matter how you
try.  Notice how much else is needed to make the statement work.  Likewise
any circular or tautalogical definition doesn't satisfy.

One of the points I'm making is that no matter how you wriggle about, sooner
or later you have to agree on a sheaf of undefined things as the basis for
your logic or arithmetic.  One of them is "one".  The deeper point is that
things can exist without definition or referent or "cause".

PL
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:8EOgj.43654$Mu4.17760@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Duh! It's very simple, Howard. Here's one proof, of many possible:

The fact that you would consider the nonsense below to constitute a "proof" 
says there is little point in debating with you.

>
> 1. If everything is caused, then there can be nothing, unless there is
>   Something/Someone That/Who is outside causality and created everything
>   else that exists => First Cause.
>
On the contrary, that simply proves that there CANNOT be a "First Cause". We 
already covered that your convenient assumption of this Being being 
"outside" the Universe is illogical and therefore not admissable.

> 2. In the history of mankind, there has never been an observation of any
>   creation that was not caused by intelligent aplication of energy =>
>   First Cause is Intelligent.

Energy is applied to things every day without intelligence being required.

Lightning strikes a tree, the tides ebb and flow, people play Nintendo...

Anyway, as no First Cause can be shown to exist, your argument has already 
failed.

>
> 3. Intelligence has never been observed in enything other than a being
>   => First Cause is an Intelligent Being.

It depends what you mean by "intelligence". You are rapidly demonstrating 
unfamiliarity with this term...

Anyway, as no First Cause can be shown to exist, your argument has already 
failed.


>
> Q.E.D.

Quite Easily Dismissed.

>
> You are free to suppose differently, but you have no logical basis and no
> observation or testable hypothesis on which to do so. Occam's Razor
> says that, if there is a relatively simple explaination, you're done;
> don't add complexity unless logically necessary.

William of Ockham would be spinning in his grave to see how you blunted his 
razor with the above apology for a syllogism.

No complexity was necessary; it was a childish attempt based on dogma and 
illogic.

"Duh!"... yourself :-)

Pete.
```

```
"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:2Y6hj.44401$Mu4.23831@bignews7.bellsouth.net...
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
[snip]
> > And what happens when your god interferes with cause and event
> > relationships? You have already pointed out that he can as he is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> would you expect, when you a priori reject God as a possibility.

It is my understanding that medical imaging can show the
presence of a tumor; but a biopsy it required to determine
whether cancer is present. To associate MRI images with
cancer, rather than with tumors, is a misstatement.

Tumors can erupt and drain without surgical intervention.
I had one on my chest for about ten years that did so,
though it took about one week, perhaps due to its size.
What appears to have happened is that the tumor finally
interrupted the flow of blood to the skin above the tumor
causing the skin to die. The skin, no longer able to contain
the tumor, can not prevent the eruption and subsequent
drainage. After the drainage was complete, the skin
regenerated leaving a scar. There has been no sign of
regrowth of the tumor.

What I have supplied here is science. I observed and
provided a theoretical explanation of a phenomenon.
This scientific explantion may apply similarly to the case
you mentioned. This does not deny divine intervention
in your friend's case; nor does the absence of an explanation
specifiic to your friend's case require divine intervention as
a default explanation.
```

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> a default explanation.


I don't remember about biopsies, though I can ask my friend. I do know
the doctors were convinced enough that it was cancer to schedule full
surgery to remove it. The MRI the day before was the last test they did
beforehand to verify it was still present. When my friend came out of
surgery, the doctor said "You got your miracle. The cancer is gone, but
the lesions are still there, indicating it was present." My friend says the
medical staff was amazed.
```

```
it necessary for the medical staff to do an mri to ensure that it was
still present? That implies to me that cancers have a habit of
spontaneously disappearing. Whether they do or not I do not know but I
do know that certain zoonoses (eq warts) will often spontaneously
disappear between booking an appointment to remove them and the
patient visiting the medical practice on the day of the medical
procedure.
```

```
[snip]

I have a brother who underwent cancer surgery last month.
(Malignant melanoma successfully removed.) The MRI on
the day before the surgery was to determine the size and
extent of the diseased tissue. The reason for an MRI may
vary depending on the type and location of the cancer.
```

```
Note that I said matter. It is possible to create matter and anti-
matter pairs and arrange for them to annihilate themselves. Your poor

(the greatest fairy tale ever written).
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<an7hj.44456$Mu4.43127@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <plnql3p2ooqdr8dm66cqjenrgfrkiulok7@4ax.com> <E2e7j.18531$N67.17873@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9ed42f70-9ea4-4abf-a064-344e8d84534d@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <kKgaj.45884$K27.35574@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fuiaj.10937$pq.7300@newsfe24.lga> <OYtaj.46273$L%6.1468@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OZSdnWW0CqZtFPfanZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@golden.net> <fi4lm31hp3ui83bm2kpk3j1m80i7r4b2ed@4ax.com> <7Wwaj.46370$L%6.29618@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <009lm3t47v09v8qbti0ies5he30edeen98@4ax.com> <0Cyaj.46414$L%6.11571@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <jxzaj.8162$E01.4821@newsfe22.lga> <t6Aaj.46467$L%6.3380@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5t0n3kF1bfuqaU1@mid.individual.net> <5mNaj.27585$_m.12145@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <454ed8d1-5f4d-4c65-a93a-b2289dc11136@v29g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <uYLgj.43570$Mu4.7325@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <348ed0fd-6507-47c6-945a-9eee60c3f37d@m77g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> in this group.

My "poor grasp of physics" is sufficient to know that what you describe
has *nothing* to do with creating things from nothing (as is the topic
of this thread at this point), and that such "annihilation" generates a
potfull of energy (at the rate of E=MC^2), just as it *took* more than
that amount of energy to form the matter/antimatter in the first place.
*Not* something from nothing! If it were, then perpetual motion, even
abundant energy from nothing, is a reality. Just create all the
matter/antimatter pairs you like *from nothing*, then annihilate them
to get all the energy you want. That what you claim?

> Also note that I did not mention energy.

That's okay, I corrected your intentional "oversight". I knew you were
trying to obscure that fact, when you tried to equate such laboratory
experiements with Creation. With energy in the equation, your ruse
becomes obvious (if there was anybody for whom it wasn't already
obvious). :-)


This reminds me of a joke my pastor told me:

A prominent scientist publicly claimed he could create life.

God appears and confronts the man "You claim you can create life all
by yourself?"

"Yes, I can" the scientist replied.

"Okay," said God, "let Me see you do it."

The scientist began by getting some chemicals to put in a test tube.

"No, No!" said God, "You have to use all your own stuff; I made that."
```

<a id="t-672e84e799"></a>
## Thread: M\`I 5-Persecu tion - t heir methods a nd ta ctics — 1 msgs

### M\`I 5-Persecu tion - t heir methods a nd ta ctics

- **From:** vmvmf@lycos.com
- **Date:** 2008-01-02T08:09:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.eiffel,comp.lang.forth,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.functional
- **Message-ID:** `<na0800020809152530@4ax.com>`

```
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
-= MI5:. methods and tactics -=
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=

They deliberately set out to harass in a way. that would resemble the
symptoms of schizophrenia, so. that any report of the harassment would be
taken as indicating. mental illness and "treated" accordingly. They never
show their own faces; they only work through. proxies, in the media, among
the public, and by manipulating people in the workplace.. Since they do not
declare their identity there is no evidence to. initiate legal action
against the security services or anyone. else. The only people you can
prosecute are the proxies. and they will deny knowledge of any conspiracy.

By repeatedly humiliating and abusing the victim, they. induced mental
illness. This is the worst form of human rights violation:. making any
statement of. the harassment appear to be symptomatic of the illness which
they cause through the harassment. That this can happen, and people. collude
by. silence, is absolutely horrifying.

From the beginning in June 1990 they set a. pattern of harassment which they
have. followed without change for the last six years. They  paint me as a
"threat" to which people must "react" (shades of. Nazi persecution methods),
while simultaneously portraying. their hate campaign on which they have
spent over a million. pounds of taxpayers� money as a "joke".

The MI5 that. breaks the law with the silent complicity of the police is the
same agency that is now seeking a role in the fight. against crime. Perhaps
the real joke is the. proposed involvement in the implementation of justice
of an organisation which commits criminal acts with secrecy and. disinterest
for the. legal process.

339
```

---

<a id="t-a26ab1330c"></a>
## Thread: M'I-5 Persecuti on , why t he securit y s ervices? — 1 msgs

### M'I-5 Persecuti on , why t he securit y s ervices?

- **From:** efefimifi@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 2008-01-02T08:39:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.eiffel,comp.lang.forth,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.functional
- **Message-ID:** `<ly0800020839160356@4ax.com>`

```
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
-= why. the security services? -=
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=

You may ask,. why do I think the "they" referred to are the security
services? Is there any evidence that there. is a single source, as opposed
to a loosely based "whispering campaign" amongst many people? Even. if there
is a single source, is there any. evidence that "they" are professional
"buggers" as opposed to amateurs, or perhaps people working. for a privately
funded. organization?

a) As to the question of a single. source versus something more fragmented;
it is quite obvious that. there is a single source from the way the campaign
has been carried. out. Since things have been repeated verbatim which were
said in my home, there must be. one group which does the watching and
listening. Since on several occasions. (mainly during travel) people have
been. planted in close proximity and rehearsed in what they were to say, it
follows that. someone must have done the planning for that, and again a
single source. is indicated.

b) So why couldn't it be amateurs? Why couldn't it be a. private
organisation, for example a private detective agency paid to. manage the
campaign. and undertake the technical aspects? Some detective agencies are
unscrupulous as has been proved on the. occasions in the past when they've
been exposed or caught; they too can have access to the bugging. technology
deployed; and there are reported cases of MI5 paying. private eyes to do
their dirty work (against. peace campaigners and similar enemies of the
state) on the understanding that if they were caught then they. could deny
all knowledge. Why couldn't that be. the case?

The main factor. pointing to direct security service involvement (as opposed
to amateurs or MI5. proxies) is the breadth of their access to the media in
particular, and the fact that the television. companies are so involved in
the campaign. The BBC. would not directly invade someone's home themselves,
since it would not be within their remit to. allocate personnel or financial
resources to do so. An. organisation of their stature would not take part in
a campaign set. up by private sources. The only people they would take
material from would be. the security services, presumably on the assumption
that if the cat ever. flew out of the bag yowling it would be MI5 who would
take the. consequences.

State sponsorship for these acts of psychological. terrorism is also
indicated by duration; support for over six years for a team. of three or
four people would be beyond. the means and will of most private sources.
The viciousness of the. slanders and personal denigration also points to
MI5; they traditionally "protect" the British state. from politicians of the
wrong. hue by character assassination, and in this case are using their
tried and tested methods to. murder with words an enemy they have invented
for. themselves.

And there are precedents. Diana and Hewitt. were alleged to have been filmed
"at it" by an Army. intelligence team which had operated in Northern
Ireland, these allegations were made by someone called Jones. who had been
on the team. His statements were. denied by the defence establishment who
tried to character-assassinate by describing him as the "Jones. twins".
Funny how if you. tell the truth, then you must be ill, isn't it? Thought
only. communists behaved like that?

Hewitt later said that he'd. been spoken to by someone in the army who
revealed the existence of videotapes of him. and Diana, and that the tapes
would be published if. any attempt was made by them to resume their
association.

2760
```

---

```
The main factor pointing to direct security service. involvement (as opposed

State sponsorship for. these acts of psychological terrorism is also
indicated by duration; support for over six years for a team. of three or
four people would be beyond the means and will of most private. sources.
The viciousness of the slanders and personal denigration also. points to
MI5; they traditionally "protect" the British state from politicians of. the
wrong hue by character assassination, and in this case are. using their

And there are precedents. Diana and Hewitt were alleged to. have been filmed

Ireland, these allegations were made by someone called. Jones who had been
on the team. His statements were denied by the. defence establishment who
tried to character-assassinate by describing. him as the "Jones twins".
Funny how if you tell the truth, then you. must be ill, isn't it? Thought
only communists behaved like. that?

Hewitt later said that he'd. been spoken to by someone in the army who
revealed the existence of videotapes of him and Diana, and. that the tapes
would be published if any attempt was made by them to. resume their
association.
```

<a id="t-9e78f81783"></a>
## Thread: M I-5 Persec ution harassm ent at wo rk — 1 msgs

### M I-5 Persec ution harassm ent at wo rk

- **From:** emfiemvf@hotmail.com
- **Date:** 2008-01-02T10:39:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.eiffel,comp.lang.forth,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.functional
- **Message-ID:** `<za0800021039495976@4ax.com>`

```
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=
-= harassment at. work -=
-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=

Once I stopped watching television and listening to the. radio at the end of
1990, "they" had. to find other ways of committing abuses. So they took what
must be for them a tried and tested. route; they get at you by subversion of
those around you. Since they. wouldn't be able to do that with my family or
friends, that meant getting at people in the workplace to be. their
mouthpieces and do their dirty work. for them.

They supplied my employers in Oxford. with details from what was going on in
my private life, and what I and. other people had said at my home and
accommodation in. Oxford. So people at work repeated verbatim words which
had been said in my home, and. repeated what I'd been doing recently. Often
the most trivial things, the ones. from your domestic life, are the ones
which hurt most. One manager in. particular at Oxford continuously abused me
for ten months with verbal sexual. abuse, swearing, and threats to terminate
my employment. After ten months. I was forced to seek psychiatric help and
start taking. medication, and was away from work for two months. I spoke
later with a solicitor about what had happened at that company;. he advised
it was only possible to take action if you had. left the company as a result
of harassment, and such an action would. have to be started very soon after
leaving.

Over. a year later the same manager picked on another new worker, with even
more serious results; that employee tried to commit suicide. with an
overdose as a result of the ill-treatment, and. was forced to leave his job.
But he didn't take action. against the company, either. Abuse at work is
comparable to that elsewhere in that. tangible evidence is difficult to
produce,. and the abusers will always have their denials ready when
challenged. And even if a court accepts what you say happened, it. still
remains to prove. that abuse causes the type of breakdown I had at the end
of 1992. In a recent case before a British court,. a former member of the
Army brought a case against others who had. maltreated him ten years
previously. Although the court accepted that abuse. had occurred, it did not
agree that. depressive illness necessarily followed, and denied justice to
the. plaintiff.

2760
```

---

<a id="t-5a549aa034"></a>
## Thread: M'I-5 Persecut ion ' B BC h2g2 o nline — 1 msgs

### M'I-5 Persecut ion ' B BC h2g2 o nline

- **From:** mefvi@lycos.com
- **Date:** 2008-01-02T11:45:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.eiffel,comp.lang.forth,comp.lang.fortran,comp.lang.functional
- **Message-ID:** `<rw0800021145253770@4ax.com>`

```

BBC h2g2. 16/Sept/1999

The BBC's h2g2 website is billed as "Earth Edition of The Hitchhiker's Guide to the. Galaxy!". Naturally, one of its pages
is about me, me, me. It starts off with the. lines; 

Every now and then something comes along. which makes you
think "Ah! That's what the Internet. is for!" Mike Corley
is one such. case.

The page itself can. be found at http://www.bbc.co.uk/dna/h2g2/alabaster/A164404 

The user comments linked from. h2g2 are less than respectful, but given the point of view my website promulgates that is
understandable. It's true that without the communications ability afforded by the internet,. which came along at just the
right time, my complaints would be getting. nowhere. But that's a two edged sword also; miniature cameras only appeared
around 1990, so. the advantage of technology is up to the adopter. Secret service intrigues, and on the other side of the
coin human paranoia, have both. been going on for centuries; what changes now is an increased technologisation of both sides. 

339
```

---

<a id="t-2c32e6a190"></a>
## Thread: Running on a mainframe was Re: working storage values — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: Running on a mainframe was Re: working storage values

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-02T11:07:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<477B707F.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
>>> On 1/1/2008 at 4:15 PM, in message
<i9iln3t2fsvp6lsmtv1mnhtkekjd4dvfg4@4ax.com>, Robert<no@e.mail> wrote:
> On Tue, 01 Jan 2008 16:20:58 -0400, Clark F Morris 
><cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>the right track with Gears, but it now supports only JavaScript. When the

> browser is your
>>>UI, you can run on ANY platform (except mainframe). You can run locally 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> does it, but the fact that he can do it at all. 

We are soon(!) going to be running DB2/LUW on Linux (SLES10) on our System Z
mainframe, accessing it (in part) from our VSE mainframe operating system. 
Communication between VSE and Linux will be using TCP/IP over
'hipersockets'.

We may at some point also move some of our WebSphere servers from Intel
Linux to zSeries Linux and, again, use hipersocket TCP/IP communication for
speed.

Frank
```
