<a id="t-23d892179b"></a>
## Thread: Java is becoming the new Cobol — 186 msgs

### Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-02T11:31:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```

The article is not making the point that Java is becoming the
corporate language of choice, as Cobol is/once was (choose one), but
that is is becoming obsolete as it is being replaced by newer
languages such as "Ruby, PHP, AJAX [sic]", and even C#.

http://www.infoworld.com/article/07/12/28/52FE-underreported-java_1.html

"""If you're a Java developer, now's the time to invest in new
skills."""

There was a quote from a quarter century ago or more that went "10
years ago there were 3000 languages and COBOL, today there are 300 and
COBOL. In 10 years time I expect there will be 30 and COBOL."

The question then is: Is Java just another fad language in the range:
Algol, Pascal, Modula2, Ada, C++, that will be replaced by the next
fad languages Ruby, PHP, C# which will then be replaced by the
next ...

Or will Java really become the next Cobol and will continue for
decades more ?
```

- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <cBSej.3950$M24.1987@newsfe17.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:cBSej.3950$M24.1987@newsfe17.lga...
>  Or will COBOL become the new mainframe?  I seem to recall that mainframes
> were pronounced dead a couple of decades ago.
>
> PL

Mainframes ARE dead in terms of doing anything interesting with them :-)

The best chance of survival they have is by attachment to the Network and 
welcoming the Web. To the extent that they do this and become powerful 
servers in a Network environment, they will have a future. The days when 
they sat at the centre of things and controlled everything are long gone. To 
that extent, the role they served decades ago is gone, so they ARE dead as 
far as that goes.

Don't hold your breath for a resurgence of COBOL, in the role which it 
served decades ago, either.

Fortress COBOL is in ruins. It has been sacked and looted. Whatever was of 
value has been incorporated into the Brave New World and the barbarians on 
their wiry little ponies have swept on...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Reaffirmation of '02 ANSI Standard (was:: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-03T17:46:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8c9fj.247172$WF3.208254@fe06.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <cBSej.3950$M24.1987@newsfe17.lga> <5u2gsbF1f3om9U1@mid.individual.net> <2dd38e51-75f6-4e35-8db8-92bf2594d844@i12g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5u42k7F1g7ju4U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:5u42k7F1g7ju4U1@mid.individual.net...
<snip>
>>> Fortress COBOL is in ruins. It has been sacked and looted. Whatever was of
>>> value has been incorporated into the Brave New World and the barbarians on
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Today is the two-week before the meeting deadline for submitting papers for the 
J4 teleconference on reaffirming the ANSI 2002 COBOL Standard.  (The ISO version 
has already been reaffirmed).  Unlike Pete, I still get emotional about the 
whole thing (especially the lack of HONESTY in the standardization process - 
from my perception).  Some days I "get over it" - but others, I think I am 
getting an ulcer.  (Also, unlike Pete, I do think that IBM mainframe COBOL is 
here for a LOT longer than until 2015, but I certainly agree that I could be 
mistaken on this.)

I am trying to work on a document o submit that basically ask WHY the ANSI '02 
Standard should be reaffirmed? It is so hard for me to write this is a way that 
will be "properly" processed by J4, but I am TRYING to make them actually answer 
the questions:

1) Is there any implementer who has given the slightest indication that they 
will ever produce a fully conforming '02 compiler?

2) Is there any implementer who has indicated that whether or not there is an 
'02 ANSI COBOL Standard will impact which individual features from the ANSI (or 
ISO) Standard they will actually implement? (Does any implementer get user input 
saying, please implement this BECAUSE it is in the '02 Standard?)

3) What COBOL programmers have indicated that the existence of an ANSI (not just 
an ISO) COBOL Standard makes a difference to what they do?  When they use COBOL? 
What they expect from their COBOL supplier?  How many programmers eve run with 
"conformance" flagging turned on for '85 - or any other - Standard conformance? 
Does this actually impact what features (extensions) they do and don't use?

4) Does the ANSI (INCITS, J4) structure have sufficient resources to actually 
"maintain" (do defect processing, etc) the '02 Standard AS WELL as developing a 
revision to this Standard?

5) In general, what DIFFERENCE does J4 see between a world with a reaffirmed 
ANSI COBOL Standard and one without it:
  - to programmers?
 - to implementer?
 - to managers selecting programming languages?
 - to end users of "compiled" applications?
 - even to the public perception of the state of COBOL?

* * * * *

Oh well, please do excuse my semi-rant.  As I stated in an earlier post.  Anyone 
wanting to comment on whether the ANSI '02 COBOL standard should or should not 
be reaffirmed should (ASAP now) send their comments to the J4 chair at:
   bobkarlin <at> karlinskorner.com

Both comments FOR and AGAINST might help J4 make the "right" decision - whatever 
that might be. (I know my opinion, but I also know that others have different 
views.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reaffirmation of '02 ANSI Standard (was:: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-04T10:38:09+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5u52u4F1ghcurU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <cBSej.3950$M24.1987@newsfe17.lga> <5u2gsbF1f3om9U1@mid.individual.net> <2dd38e51-75f6-4e35-8db8-92bf2594d844@i12g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5u42k7F1g7ju4U1@mid.individual.net> <8c9fj.247172$WF3.208254@fe06.news.easynews.com>`

```


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:8c9fj.247172$WF3.208254@fe06.news.easynews.com...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
> news:5u42k7F1g7ju4U1@mid.individual.net...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> but I certainly agree that I could be mistaken on this.)

I really would like to be wrong about this, Bill, and I have nothing but 
respect for your untiring efforts to keep ANSI honest. I know something of 
the frustration you have experienced in the past and the venom your efforts 
have brought down on you.

Nevertheless, I believe you are shovelling shit uphill if you expect to make 
progress with this group.
>
> I am trying to work on a document o submit that basically ask WHY the ANSI 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> * * * * *

These are all very good questions and are indicative of a keener perception 
than that demonstrated so far by ANSI/ISO. Obviously, BEFORE committing 
resources to further COBOL standardization, and subsequent development by 
vendors, these questions should be considered and answered. If the answers 
indicate an ongoing demand, then it makes sense for people to work on the 
committees; if they don't, then everybodies time (and money) could be better 
spent (maybe in training staff for other languages...)

Unfortunately, I believe that the people on these committees in the past 
(with the definite exception of yourself, and maybe one or two others...) 
have been there because it may look good on a CV, and not because of any 
real commitment to improving COBOL or establishing credibility for it.

But then, I have become cynical over the years while waiting for a new 
standard, have been really disappointed to see nothing useful emerging from 
these committees, and it has irritated me beyond measure to see the total 
lack of proper management and direction and the bureaucratic treacle that 
has to be waded through to get anything done. It is not surprising that the 
COBOL comunity as a whole have not thrown themselves behind something that 
they cannot see any relevance for, and which has a track record of lost 
opportunities and missed deadlines.  In other words, readers here should 
ignore my biased opinons about this... :-)

>
> Oh well, please do excuse my semi-rant.  As I stated in an earlier post. 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> have different views.)

I hope people here will respond to this.

I wish you luck in your efforts, Bill, and if you need/want help with 
documents, proofreading, etc. just contact me privately.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-04T11:04:33+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5u54fkF1gcj22U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <cBSej.3950$M24.1987@newsfe17.lga> <5u2gsbF1f3om9U1@mid.individual.net> <bs2qn3lhj5kfn52g98tbcltir30fpd0uv9@4ax.com>`

```


"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message 
news:bs2qn3lhj5kfn52g98tbcltir30fpd0uv9@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 3 Jan 2008 11:17:49 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Assembler.  No Java, no C# no soup de jour.

It wasn't meant to wound, Steve...and was said with tongue in cheek. :-)

You don't mention what you consider to be "interesting", but for me I can 
immediately think of half a dozen thigs I can't do with mainframes, but 
which are "interesting"...I'm quite sure I can could come up with more 
:-)...

1. Setting up a personal telephone answering service, complete with 
interactive voice response (IVR), voicemail, call forwarding, and camping on 
to engaged numbers. I don't want to buy this service from my Telecomm 
provider. It has nothing to do with money, just a question of principle 
(they are bastards and I dislike them intensely but have little option if I 
want a landline phone...:-)). Writing the software for this, is proving to 
be very "interesting" and I expect to have it running fairly soon on an old 
notebook which can be dedicated to the task.

2. Developing interactive Web Sites, driven by databases, using ASP.Net.

3. Running remote procedures across networks and the world. (My web server 
is currently based in San Francisco but is maintained from NZ and accessed 
globally. This is fun and VERY interesting...)

4. Experimenting with Lambdas and Query Expressions to organise and access 
data more effectively. (I don't know of any mainframe software that allows 
this, or supports LINQ style expressions, but there might be. I hope so...)

5.  Being able to analyse and repair damaged or scratched DVDs is 
interesting.

6. Connecting my mobile phone to my computer by Bluetooth and messing with 
it is "interesting"...

and a bonus...

7.  Developing ANYTHING for mobile devices like iPods and Blackberries is 
jolly "interesting"... (I haven't done this yet but it is on my list and I 
have the tools to do it...)

While I agree that all of the above COULD be achieved on a mainframe (with 
the right hardware and software connections), how much of it IS being done 
on a mainframe?

That was my point.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-03T17:42:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<opefj.37167$Mu4.3749@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <cBSej.3950$M24.1987@newsfe17.lga> <5u2gsbF1f3om9U1@mid.individual.net> <bs2qn3lhj5kfn52g98tbcltir30fpd0uv9@4ax.com> <5u54fkF1gcj22U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> ... I can immediately think of half a dozen thigs I can't do with
> mainframes, but which are "interesting"...

Over the last year I've been working with an associate to develop Visual
Studio applications to interface between the local county's Unisys A
Series mainframe and some web based services supplied by the state. We
were successful, but again and again we were chagrined to learn that
interfacing capabilities Unisys claimed and documented for the A Series
either did not really exist or were only partially implemented. For
example, the A Series can provide web services, but apparently nobody at
Unisys considered that any of their A Series mainframes would ever want
to *access* a web service on another server, because that capability
does not exist in the Unisys software. Named pipes, and a host of other
features just aren't completely implemented and/or documented.

This makes me sad, because I've worked with Burroughs (then Unisys)
mainframes my whole career, and Unisys apparently either doesn't know
how to make their mainframes truly net capable, or don't have the will
or resources to do it.

Computers are computers, and there is no reason why any mainframe could
not be made to do anything a smaller computer can do, with the proper
software. I don't know about the IBM mainframe environment, and it may
be vastly different, but don't the mainframe manufacturers "get it?"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-03T18:31:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<P7ffj.37189$Mu4.15288@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <cBSej.3950$M24.1987@newsfe17.lga> <5u2gsbF1f3om9U1@mid.individual.net> <bs2qn3lhj5kfn52g98tbcltir30fpd0uv9@4ax.com> <5u54fkF1gcj22U1@mid.individual.net> <opefj.37167$Mu4.3749@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5u5atsF1g8vq4U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are you saying it doesn't support XML/SOAP? That's all you need to access a web service.

It's been several months since we dealt with that but IIRC, it was an
inability to initiate the TCP/IP query, not formatting issues. What we did
(which was appropriate for this particular application) was use a PC
based client that queried the web service, formatted the data and sent
it to the mainframe using named pipes, which partially work, though with
wierd and unexpected issues.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-03T19:02:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CfmdnZBvadYGDuDanZ2dnUVZ_vumnZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<opefj.37167$Mu4.3749@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <cBSej.3950$M24.1987@newsfe17.lga> <5u2gsbF1f3om9U1@mid.individual.net> <bs2qn3lhj5kfn52g98tbcltir30fpd0uv9@4ax.com> <5u54fkF1gcj22U1@mid.individual.net> <opefj.37167$Mu4.3749@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Over the last year I've been working with an associate to develop Visual
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> features just aren't completely implemented and/or documented.

Wow.  I know they have some of that with some native tools on the 
2200-series, as well as a JVM (so you could pretty much make the thing 
do whatever you wanted using Java).  I believe my old office was 
starting to do a little bit of that before I left.

Of course, I know we did some stuff that caught them by surprise. 
Between Unisys and DISA*, we seemed to be responsible for finding 
documented features that either weren't coded, weren't installed, or had 
big problems.  I can think of two different bugs we "fixed" (identified 
to them, then saw it through) on their COBOL 85 compiler, and 3 with 
their relational database server.  :)

* Defense Information Systems Agency.  I'm *so* glad our current shop 
doesn't deal with them.  Until about a year before I left, our 
relationship with them had been an "us vs. them" thing.  In their 
defense, though, something changed, and they were very supportive from 
then until I left.  I think the something that changed is that they 
deployed our system with half the resources we told them we would need, 
and had to really work hard when the system came to its knees...  Heh - 
good times...  (Of course, "it's all good" would probably be the way to 
look at it.  Their skimping on hardware prompted us to look at some of 
our more processor-intensive processes, and actually gave us a reason to 
redesign a couple of areas that needed it.)

The sad thing is, for the most part, the DISA *people* I dealt with were 
nice folks - it was just the policies they were expected to enforce.
```

#### ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-03T11:11:02+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5u2gfkF1fs5ehU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```


"Richard" <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com...
>
> The article is not making the point that Java is becoming the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> skills."""

It's true. It is even truer for COBOL developers.

>
> There was a quote from a quarter century ago or more that went "10
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> next ...

Bad Question.

Algol, Pascal,Modula2 and Ada were NOT "fad languages", and neither are 
Ruby, PHP, and C#.

They simply addressed different paradigms.
>
> Or will Java really become the next Cobol and will continue for
> decades more ?
>
Java will not become the new COBOL and neither will anything else.

COBOL belongs to an era when there was no Network and a centralised 
mainframe only needed one or two high level languages to program it.

Those days are gone and COBOL is hanging on currently purely because of its 
legacy applications. Even these are being slowly replaced. The only new 
development in COBOL of any significance is  probably on less than 1% of 
sites Worldwide, and most of this will be mainframe, and procedural batch 
processing.

The trend is to replace COBOL.

Initially that will be with Java (for the most part), but the important 
thing here is not the language, but the paradigm.

Java is an OO language and OO is the basis for future development. COBOL 
missed this boat (not through any fault of the language or the vendors of 
it, but through the shortsightedness and arrogance of the COBOL community) 
and there is no way of catching up.

Once something has been refactored into objects, the languages used to 
access the objects are irrelevant; it is the paradigm that is important. 
Languages like Java, Ruby, PHP, and C# are designed around OO and are 
therefore relevant. It isn't a fad; it is here to stay. Certainly, the 
technology may move on from OO (it is already doing so with component based 
systems), but the underlying base for the perceivable future will be OO, and 
commercial sites will use whatever langiuages support this.

The idea of having ONE language is just stupid. We need different languages 
for different purposes.

Most COBOL people simply haven't realised that the Holy Source Code is no 
longer relevant. It is Holy Object code and Holy Objects that have inherited 
the Earth, and we are seeing better IT systems because of it.

There will almost certainly be newer languages that will improve on areas 
not beng well served by current ones, but they will be built around the OO 
paradigm and they will not be mutually exclusive with existing languages 
like Java.

For this reason, we are not going to see the "end of Java" anytime soon.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-01-02T17:46:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r15on39djqv6a4tgdd360rcfp0jr3b50aq@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5u2gfkF1fs5ehU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Thu, 3 Jan 2008 11:11:02 +1300, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

>"Richard" <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
>news:28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com...
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>Java will not become the new COBOL and neither will anything else.

I believe SQL is becoming the new Cobol, for these reasons:

.. It has been largely unchanged in 20+ years. 
.. It faces little competition, because database servers cannot easily be altered 
   to accept another language.
.. It successfully ignored OO additions in the '90s, analogous to Cobol.
.. Long running batch programs are being written in PL/SQL. There are whole shops, 
   mostly in data warehousing, that do 100% of development in PL/SQL, SQR and database
   utilities.
.. A recent survey of job ads found PL/SQL is second highest skill in demand by big
    companies, after Java.  It is higher than C++, C#, PERL, Ruby,  et al.
.. Attempts to replace the two dimensional relational model have not been successful in
   the commercial marketplace. 

>COBOL belongs to an era when there was no Network and a centralised 
>mainframe only needed one or two high level languages to program it.

A network INCREASES the need for interoperability. When mainframes were islands and data
was passed via flat files, it didn't matter what language the other shop was using. Now
that we pass data by setting a DbLink to the other shop's database, we must talk to it in
SQL. XML doesn't change that much because it is hierarchical rather than object model and
its schemas usually map closely with underlying database schemas. 

>Those days are gone and COBOL is hanging on currently purely because of its 
>legacy applications. Even these are being slowly replaced. The only new 
>development in COBOL of any significance is  probably on less than 1% of 
>sites Worldwide, and most of this will be mainframe, and procedural batch 
>processing.

Generally true, but a non-trivial portion, maybe 30%. of Cobol runs on Big Unix. For
instance PeopleSoft and Lawson.

>The trend is to replace COBOL.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>and there is no way of catching up.

>Once something has been refactored into objects, the languages used to 
>access the objects are irrelevant; it is the paradigm that is important. 
>Languages like Java, Ruby, PHP, and C# are designed around OO and are 
>therefore relevant. It isn't a fad; it is here to stay.

Agreed. But isn't it contradictory to say language doesn't matter while pronouncing Cobol
dead? Cobol can talk OO as fluently as other languages. 

> Certainly, the 
>technology may move on from OO (it is already doing so with component based 
>systems), but the underlying base for the perceivable future will be OO, and 
>commercial sites will use whatever langiuages support this.

Sometimes it appears they'll use whatever the Fad Language Of The Month Club sent last
month..
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-04T01:20:41+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5u428mF1f1nuuU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5u2gfkF1fs5ehU1@mid.individual.net> <r15on39djqv6a4tgdd360rcfp0jr3b50aq@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:r15on39djqv6a4tgdd360rcfp0jr3b50aq@4ax.com...
<snip>
>
> I believe SQL is becoming the new Cobol, for these reasons:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
An interesting thought. I've managed teams using SQL as a programming 
language and was imp[ressed by their tools and their productivity. I don't 
disagree that SQL could be considered "the New COBOL", but I'd be a lot 
surer of it if there weren't already moves to replace SQL as the "standard" 
for data access. Personally, I believe it WILL be replaced by things like 
Query Expression languages and functions (LINQ and Lambda functions, for 
example...), which are much more powerful and platform independent.

>>COBOL belongs to an era when there was no Network and a centralised
>>mainframe only needed one or two high level languages to program it.
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> dead? Cobol can talk OO as fluently as other languages.

No it can't.

It CAN talk OO (and I used it for nearly 10 years... it's impressive), but 
NOT as FLUENTLY as, say, Java or C#.

Although OO COBOL has all of the OO features, and even exceeds other 
languages in some regards (allowing multiple inheritance, for instance, 
which Java doesn't...), it is NOT easily assimilable or easy to write. Since 
using both OO COBOL and C# (and having dabbled some with Java) I am 
persuaded there is no comparison between writing OO COBOL and writing C# or 
Java. COBOL was never designed for OO and it suffers from its own verbosity. 
The modern languages are quicker, have better tools and IDEs, and are more 
concise and compact. It's really no contest, and I say this as someone who 
loves COBOL and used it for decades; it simply doesn't compete in the world 
of OO development.

Had it been widely adopted when it was first available, its life would have 
been extended, but it still would have succumbed to the modern languages 
eventually.

There is no need for "self-documerntation" when source is not important 
anyway. The major benefit of using COBOL hinges on source code being 
maintained. A modern component based approach does not hinge on that, so 
there is no driving reason to use COBOL.

The other thing that would have killed it is the sheer expense of 
maintaining source, when there are many thousands of lines of it to be 
maintained... It isn't economically justifiable. Plugging components 
together and reusing them is much cheaper.

>
>> Certainly, the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> month..

That is certainly true when Management is based on what they read in 
Computer Weekly and in-flight magazines :-). However, whether this forum 
acknowledes it or not :-), IT Management is generally improving, as is the 
general level of  IT literacy in the population at large. Today's young IT 
managers are likely to be Computing Science graduates with a background that 
includes some programming. They are less likely to be influenced by fashion 
than are managers to whom IT is a mystery, and who can only go by what 
everyone else is doing...

Whichever way you cut it, COBOL's time has gone. I gave it until 2015 in 
1996; I see no reason to revise that date.

Pete.
```

```


"tim Josling" <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au> wrote in message 
news:13nrm4bdaf19bab@corp.supernews.com...
> On Fri, 04 Jan 2008 01:20:41 +1300, Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> time was weak to nonexistent.

The only evidence I have is empirical.

Since moving to this paradigm I find I can develop stuff much quicker and I 
don't spend time maintaining old code.

I can't quantify it, because the difference is so dramatic I never needed 
to.

I would accept that in large organisations, if this approach is not 
carefully managed and implemented, it could lead to a loss of most of the 
gains.

However, that is true for just about any approach that isn't properly 
managed... :-)

Source code maintenance is something that consumes vast amounts of computer 
and personnel resource. We are better off with any approach that reduces or 
eliminates it. CBD does this.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** tim Josling <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au>
- **Date:** 2008-01-06T10:40:04
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13o1c04qobhnh75@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5u2gfkF1fs5ehU1@mid.individual.net> <r15on39djqv6a4tgdd360rcfp0jr3b50aq@4ax.com> <5u428mF1f1nuuU1@mid.individual.net> <13nrm4bdaf19bab@corp.supernews.com> <5uafhdF1gfiqdU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Sun, 06 Jan 2008 11:43:55 +1300, Pete Dashwood wrote:

> "tim Josling" <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au> wrote in message 
> news:13nrm4bdaf19bab@corp.supernews.com...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Fred Brooks pointed out many years ago that reuse is the only silver
bullet. If there is some software out there that does what you want for a
reasonable price then reusing it makes sense.  If that's what you mean by
components, I would agree. 

There are a lot of things that can make reuse at this component level go
off the rails, so it is not always the answer. As an example, the problem
of cross-dependencies between components is a serious problem in Java
development. In the windows world, it's called "DLL hell".

The problem with reuse is that for something to be reusable, it has to be
better documented, better tested, have formal cross-team maintenance
arrangements, etc. You don't break even until 3-4 reuses of the piece of
code / component, according to my experience and also according to the
books I have consulted.

With OO development generally, I found that productivity goes down with
the use of OO technologies. This surprised me initially. I'm not sure why
exactly it happens, but I have noticed

- Not everything is well modeled as objects. Trying to shoe-horn a
business problem into one paradigm al the time makes for clumsy, awkward
designs that can be inefficient. Example: in the java AWT, each color has
to be an object. So it you want to color a pixel, you have to create an
object first, then apply it to the pixel. Result - massive GC. I have seen
a lot of this sort of thing. 

- OO programmers tend to put things in hard code that in the past would
have been in some form of data table or DBMS. This makes for inflexibility.

- With any significant use of inheritance or cross-object interaction,
there is a large edifice of code that falls apart with a minor change. A
class you inherited from is changed. First you have to retest. Second if
they have changed something, you are up for changes to the inheriting
program.

Where objects are well modelled into the problem space, OO can be a
significant win eg GUIs.

Tim Josling
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **Date:** 2008-01-04T10:32:56-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<m3rsn39i54r3b316e35d2mhltk7oef9nav@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5u2gfkF1fs5ehU1@mid.individual.net> <h2jsn3t4bssl6ksq6ivgshtlpfjqq61gsu@4ax.com> <3Qtfj.19908$m6.14850@newsfe18.lga>`

```
>> A big plus that Java has that CoBOL doesn't is that it is cheap and
>> easy for people getting interested in programming to acquire and use.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>not sure what you mean by "acquire").

Acquiring in this case means acquiring a compiler and working
environment.

With a compiler, it is relatively easy to learn to create a business
level program using CoBOL.   But a high school kid wanting to play
around with programming will find it easier and cheaper to get what he
needs to write a simple Java program that works on his web page. Then
he makes one a bit more complex.   Incrementally he learns more and
more without having to spend money.
```

```
On Sun, 6 Jan 2008 11:47:33 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>The other point that is beng ignored is that procedural imperative languages 
>like COBOL are not relevant to today's main requirements.
>
>(In other words, even if it was free, there is little point in acquiring 
>COBOL any more...)

So what does a kid want to do that he finds out how to program to
accomplish his goal?

Maybe use XLS to play with his iTunes Music Library.xml file.    Or he
learns some AppleScript or DOS Batch processing to copy files.   Then
he tries a simple game.

If he wants to inventory his library he starts off with Access or
Bento.   Or even Excel.
```

```
pathway to New Technology. The ones who haven't done this or are not in the 
process of doing it, are unlikely to be with us for long...

goal posts so they are within sprinting distance. :-) )

dunno. All I know is that we waited 17 years for a standard.

And when it arrived, no-one wanted to implement it.

That should tell all of us something...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-06T11:56:55+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5uag9pF1h41tgU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5u2gfkF1fs5ehU1@mid.individual.net> <h2jsn3t4bssl6ksq6ivgshtlpfjqq61gsu@4ax.com> <mvotn353kn9mv2j3dvl50r34c11iavp7ru@4ax.com>`

```


"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote in message 
news:mvotn353kn9mv2j3dvl50r34c11iavp7ru@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 04 Jan 2008 08:13:36 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> 
> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> use.

There are some people doing that currently.(I admire their tenacity and 
actually wish them well, however I believe it is too late...)

Again, it is the paradigm of procedural imperative code that is no longer 
relevant. If it ain't OO, there is little room for it, except in niches like 
batch processing and even these are rapidly closing. If the Network can 
handle everything on line, there is no need for batch processing; even 
historical queries that need to scan cubes and DWs can run online as 
background processes, and, with modern approaches like LINQ and Functional 
Programming, can run across parallel processors with deferred execution, 
which an imperative language cannot support.

People here need to step back and get their heads out of the way it has 
"always been".

Don't take my word for it... expand your horizons a bit. GOOGLE on 
Functional Programming and LINQ. Watch a few videos with movers and shakers 
who ARE influencing the future.

The world is changing.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-02T16:28:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Z5mdnRlhsISag-HanZ2dnUVZ_oytnZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> 
> """If you're a Java developer, now's the time to invest in new
> skills."""
....
> The question then is: Is Java just another fad language in the range:
> Algol, Pascal, Modula2, Ada, C++, that will be replaced by the next
> fad languages Ruby, PHP, C# which will then be replaced by the
> next ...

I don't think it's a fad language.  However, I do see much Java being 
replaced on servers by C#.  Looking at the two languages, C# is Java, 
take 2 - the syntax is *very* similar, but it leaves out some of the 
verbosity of Java.  (heh - maybe it *is* the new COBOL!)

For example, if you wanted to access a property of an object which the 
property of another object which is the property of another object 
(whew!), in Java you have...

topObject.getSecondObject().getThirdObject().getProperty()

...whereas, in C#, it becomes...

topObject.SecondObject.ThirdObject.Property

(Plus, with the Visual Studio IDE, you only have to type like 5 or 6 
characters to get that second line!  :> )

Another aspect that I like is the way that properties are defined.  In 
Java, it's almost COBOL-like in the way properties are defined (at least 
in our shop).  We have the property items in the top, then the 
constructors, then the get and set methods after that.  In C#, you 
declare the get and set method as part of the property.

I see C# and Java in one camp, and PHP and RoR in the other.  I *really* 
like PHP's implementation of OO, although I'm sure it will mature more 
in version 6.  They have a "magic" function __call($method, $arguments) 
that is called (if defined) if a method is requested of an object that 
does not exist.  Using this, if you just need a simple getProperty() or 
setProperty() method, you don't have to define every single one of them!

Here's an example from a web site I've done...

public function __call($method, $arguments) {
	$prefix   = strtolower(substr($method, 0, 3));
	$property = strtolower(substr($method, 3, 1)) . substr($method, 4);
		
	if ((empty($prefix)) || (empty($property))) {
		return;
	}
		
	if ($prefix == "get") {
		if (isset($this->$property)) {
			return $this->$property;
		}
		else {
			return "";
		}
	}
		
	if ($prefix == "set") {
		$this->$property = $arguments[0];
	}
}

Voila!  :)  (Of course, a limitation of the above is that it will blow 
up if you say setFoo("bar") and $foo is not actually a property.  I'm 
sure I could put some error checking in for that, and probably will at 
some point.)

So, in answering your question, I don't see PHP and RoR as Java-killers. 
  I see C# as a serious competitor, but I believe that until Mono 
matures more, the Unix/Linux installations will continue to go with 
Java.  However, the advice given in that first quote is good.  Innovate 
or die (figuratively speaking, of course).
```

#### ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** Scott <NoSpam@Spamblocker.org>
- **Date:** 2008-01-04T23:39:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
THE biggest problem with the mainframe is that IBM never embraced a GUI 
screen. That's what it's all about. Why is everyone migrating from 
mainframe to servers? In almost every place I've worked or looked at, it
is always the same thing. It's not about COBOL. It's not about
performance. It's not about security. It's not about reliability. It's not
about efficiency. In many cases it's not even about cost. It's all about
pretty colors, fancy fonts, drop down boxes, scroll bars, point-click and
giggle. Everyone wants that "rich GUI experience" in their application and
you can't do that on the mainframe. People don't want 3270 screens and
haven't for 15 years. If IBM had embraced GUI screen application
development on the mainframe, we'd still have strong mainframes today.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-01-05T18:31:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3u00o3taumtq5a6lr6b93ektkb62cspdbb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <slcun3t0g2ikpp8cbpa5mdvu7ocphdud08@4ax.com> <420966b4-8ee1-44b7-8e5d-5b84ad2aac89@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Sat, 5 Jan 2008 11:33:55 -0800 (PST), Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Jan 5, 8:39 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Fri, 04 Jan 2008 23:39:47 -0600, Scott <NoS...@Spamblocker.org> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>several different Microsoft ones because I am used to them.

Here are packages that make WinXP look like Mac OSX, Vista, Fedora and Ubuntu. 

http://www.makeuseof.com/tag/5-packs-to-transform-windows-to-other-os/

The most impressive cross-OS tool I've seen is Ch by softintegration.com. It provides a
Unix command line prompt under Windows. Its shell scripting language is C. One can execute
C source, type C on the command line, run Unix commands and execute DOS and Windows
programs. Try it, I think you'll be impressed. 

>> Web browser HTML is almost as good as Microsoft. I say we go with Web browser and
>> forget the others.
>
>How many graphical web browsers in general use don't require an
>underlying graphical OS ?

Unix/Linux is not a graphical OS. Graphics is provided by X server. Most graphics apps use
either the GTK libraries, used by GNOME and most open source including Firefox, or the PNG
libraries used by KDE. 

The Windows kernel is not graphical, either. Graphics is provided by GDI, which is
analogous to X, and the comxxx libraries, which are equivalent to GTK and PNG. 

Mac OS X *is* a graphical OS. It doesn't use X server, despite the name confusion; its
graphical services are called Quartz. It has an optional X-to-Quartz bridge for running
apps developed for other flavors of Unix. More significantly, OS X is the only (surviving)
OO OS.

>Lynx, for example, does much HTML but I wouldn't call it 'as good as
><favourite GUI>'.
>
>In any case, what you call 'Web browser HTML' is probably not that at
>all, but is the result of JavaScript and various widget sets.

What I call Web browser HTML is defined by Standards such as

http://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2854.txt
https://www.cs.tcd.ie/15445/15445.HTML
http://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml2/

It's not home grown like "various widget sets". That's why Firefox looks the same on every
OS (where it runs).
```

```
On Jan 6, 1:31 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Sat, 5 Jan 2008 11:33:55 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >On Jan 5, 8:39 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't want to make anything look like XP or Vista, nor do I want XP
to look like Gnome or KDE.

> http://www.makeuseof.com/tag/5-packs-to-transform-windows-to-other-os/
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Why ?  I have no DOS or Windows programs that I want to run. I could
use cshell if I wanted to, but bash is enough.

I don't see any point in running Windows with a 'Unix command line'
when I can just run whatever I actually want.


> >> Web browser HTML is almost as good as Microsoft. I say we go with Web browser and
> >> forget the others.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> libraries used by KDE.

KDE uses Qt.

While Linux is just the Kernal, Fedora, Ubuntu, DSL, etc _are_
Graphical OSes.

There are OSes based on Linux that are not graphical because tey do
not include X or similar, for example some of the system rescue OSes.

Unixware is Graphical OS based on Unix. Again some Unix OSes are not
graphical.


> The Windows kernel is not graphical, either. Graphics is provided by GDI, which is
> analogous to X, and the comxxx libraries, which are equivalent to GTK and PNG.

So what point are you trying to make.  Windows XP _is_ a graphical OS
based on the Windows kernel.

Granted you can run (or will be able to I haven't kept up) KDE on
Windows.


> Mac OS X *is* a graphical OS. It doesn't use X server, despite the name confusion; its
> graphical services are called Quartz. It has an optional X-to-Quartz bridge for running
> apps developed for other flavors of Unix. More significantly, OS X is the only (surviving)
> OO OS.

The underlying kernel is Darwin which is Mach/BSD. MacOS/X is just
like Fedora or Windows XP.  A collection of bits combined to make a
graphical OS.

> >Lynx, for example, does much HTML but I wouldn't call it 'as good as
> ><favourite GUI>'.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> What I call Web browser HTML is defined by Standards such as

I took it that you were referring to the various UI (User Interfaces)
rather than just the rather static result of HTML. Granted HTML can do
links and buttons and forms, but that is not a UI comparable to
Windows or Gnome.

 http://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2854.txthttps://www.cs.tcd.ie/15445/15445.HTMLhttp://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml2/
>
> It's not home grown like "various widget sets".

Why do you think that 'widget sets' are 'home grown' ?

> That's why Firefox looks the same on every
> OS (where it runs).

Firefox itself can look quite different depending on who themes it. In
fact it can look like nothing (just a frame) except what it renders.

Often what it renders is not just HTML. If it provides an actual UI it
may be Java, or AJAX, Flash, ActiveX, Silverlight, or similar.

But you still haven't answered the question.

You wanted to "go with Web browser and forget the others.". As if you
could run Firefox on raw hardware, or perhaps just run it on a Windows
kernel without GDI.

Now, in fact there were web browsers that ran directly on DOS, such as
Caldera's Spider and Cougar, but they didn't do the UI bits either.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-01-07T17:29:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pu95o3tlfasqs4o5v41na58c9jt01gr4pn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <slcun3t0g2ikpp8cbpa5mdvu7ocphdud08@4ax.com> <420966b4-8ee1-44b7-8e5d-5b84ad2aac89@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <3u00o3taumtq5a6lr6b93ektkb62cspdbb@4ax.com> <0862bb35-d436-4118-8cfa-8937560420df@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Sat, 5 Jan 2008 23:55:04 -0800 (PST), Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Jan 6, 1:31 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:

>> >In any case, what you call 'Web browser HTML' is probably not that at
>> >all, but is the result of JavaScript and various widget sets.
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>Caldera's Spider and Cougar, but they didn't do the UI bits either.

The OS's graphics are not a single entiry, they are built in layers.

The lowest layer is basic graphics provided by X server, GDI and Quartz. Web browsers use
that layer. 

The second layer presents widgets such as menus and picklists in the style we associate
with the OS. This is  comxxx on Windows, GTK and PNG on Unix. Web browsers do NOT use that
layer, not even IE. Instead they use Layout Engines -- IE uses Trident, Firefox uses
Gecko, Opera uses Presto, Apple's Safari uses WebCore.  

The reason Opera runs on so many platforms is because Presto runs on many more operating
systems than the desktop ones above. It runs on Symbian based ones used in cell phones,
Java ME based ones in other cell phones, Nintendo Nii and some set-top boxes. 

All layout engines support HTML. For newer formats covered by Standards, they have (or
lack) separately named modules. For example, for ECMAScript (JavaScript) Gecko uses
SpiderMonkey, Trident uses JScript, Presto uses futhark. For formats not supported by the
browser, a plugin can be written to add support. Newer formats such as AJAX, Flash and
Silverlight (.NET CoreCLR) are also supported by plugins, which are sometimes installed
automagically. 

My point is that the second layer is OS agnostic. That's why Opera looks the same on a
Nintendo as it does on a Windows platform.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-07T19:27:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e9c9ffc2-7f18-42a1-a19f-0d56242216f4@e4g2000hsg.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <slcun3t0g2ikpp8cbpa5mdvu7ocphdud08@4ax.com> <420966b4-8ee1-44b7-8e5d-5b84ad2aac89@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <3u00o3taumtq5a6lr6b93ektkb62cspdbb@4ax.com> <0862bb35-d436-4118-8cfa-8937560420df@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <pu95o3tlfasqs4o5v41na58c9jt01gr4pn@4ax.com>`

```
On Jan 8, 12:29 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Sat, 5 Jan 2008 23:55:04 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >On Jan 6, 1:31 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> with the OS. This is  comxxx on Windows,

Menus and picklists are part of the Windows GDI, what are you
referring to with 'comxxx' ?

On Unix/Linux the X windows system itself can provide menus and
buttons (they don't look like much) so your attempt to divide it up
into layers will only impress someone who is more clueless than you
are.

> GTK and PNG on Unix. Web browsers do NOT use that
> layer, not even IE.

Yes they do. I look at the top of the screen and I see Firefox with
pulldown menus (File, Edit, View, ..) and toolbars and various other
things. It uses GTK for those.

I don't know what you think 'PNG' is, you obviously don't know that
KDE uses Qt and PNG is an image format (like JPEG).

> Instead they use Layout Engines -- IE uses Trident, Firefox uses
> Gecko, Opera uses Presto, Apple's Safari uses WebCore.

They _also_ use layout engines. It doesn't mean that they _don't_ use
GTK, GDI, etc.


> The reason Opera runs on so many platforms is because Presto runs on many more operating
> systems than the desktop ones above. It runs on Symbian based ones used in cell phones,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Excuse me, but I just told you that when you claimed you would get
your GUI from 'Web browser HTML'.

Having made silly remarks you now seem to have cut and pasted some
stuff to make it look as if you know what you are talking about.


> My point is that the second layer is OS agnostic. That's why Opera looks the same on a
> Nintendo as it does on a Windows platform.

Firefox looks the same on Linux and Windows because GTK runs on both
and uses the Windows GDI on that system.
```

```
On Jan 8, 8:29 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Mon, 7 Jan 2008 19:27:14 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >On Jan 8, 12:29 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, Robert, wrong again. comctl32.dll is the so called 'Common
Controls'. Buttons, list boxes, menus are in user32. In fact Windows
3.0 did not have these common controls yet still was a graphics OS
(just). These came as a separate file one could download to add to
Windows 3.1. They were standard in 3.11.

Obviously the W95 and NT comctrl32 has more than W3.x had, but it
could still work without it as a windowing GUI (except those programs
that use the additional bits in comctl32).

> >On Unix/Linux the X windows system itself can provide menus and
> >buttons (they don't look like much) so your attempt to divide it up
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> You're referring to Xt. No one uses it.

Was this universal conclusion about X toolkit drawn from your usual
extensive survey of _every_ draw of your desk ?

Xpdf, xtide, xclock, ..

> >> GTK and PNG on Unix. Web browsers do NOT use that
> >> layer, not even IE.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> toolbars.

I suppose the rendering window(s) could directly bit-bang the graphics
memory, but in fact it also uses the underlying OS supplied drawing
and buffering functions.


> >I don't know what you think 'PNG' is, you obviously don't know that
> >KDE uses Qt and PNG is an image format (like JPEG).
>
> You're correct about Qt.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-01-05T08:08:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com>`

```
In article <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com>,
Scott  <NoSpam@Spamblocker.org> wrote:
>THE biggest problem with the mainframe is that IBM never embraced a GUI 
>screen.

Ummmmm... I was taught, a few decades back, that this wasn't done because 
the overhead necessary to make the mainframe architecture do what it 
wasn't designed to do (graphic display) would render using the 
applications prohibitively expensive.  I remember, a few years before 
that, being informed that a local Corner Office Idiot had sent out a memo 
telling his programmers to reduce their TSO connect-costs... every byte of 
core, every swap to disk, all were kept track of and had to be paid for.

I'm not sure how, nowadays, use of a graphics-intensive Linux application 
gets costed-out to a department... might be interesting to look into.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-05T11:58:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
On Sat, 5 Jan 2008 08:08:50 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com>,
>Scott  <NoSpam@Spamblocker.org> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

And why is GUI the answer?  Have users become so "dumb" they can't
figure out to use a straightforward 3270 screen?  They can't press the
ENTER key on the keyboard, they need a mouse to place a pointer on an
"ENTER" box?  The 3270 screen is too scary? It's ridiculous.  

My employer has an application that "paints" 3270 screens.  That is,
you can use it to design inquiry, data entry or a combination of the
two screens and then run a batch program that will create the copybook
and BMS code.  For certain 3270 type monitors, it can also put color
on the screen.  In the early 90's I and a colleague went to Athens,
Greece to work on a presentation of our software with our partner
there.  The requirement?  Make an inquiry screen on a 3270 monitor
look like a GUI screen.  We did as much as was possible.  After all,
BMS couldn't do graphics.  But the requirement (not presented by any
IT people, by the way) seemed to say, our people aren't smart enough
to work a 3270 screen.  It seems that sentiment is still around in
some corners and I've yet to meet a user who couldn't effectively use
a 3270 screen.

Regards,
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"Research is what I'm doing when I don't know what I'm doing."
--Wernher von Braun
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Scott <NoSpam@Spamblocker.org>
- **Date:** 2008-01-05T11:35:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ypKdnRhZaYl3IuLa4p2dnAA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com>`

```
That's all true. But the fact is people don't want 3270 screens. 
Not because they're not functional or people can't use them but because
3270 screens look "old". It reminds people of MS-DOS. Graphic screens look
"new". They look like Windows. Users and management who hold the purse
strings and make the decisions want their I.T. applications to look "new"
despite everything else.
```

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message 
news:3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 5 Jan 2008 08:08:50 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> "ENTER" box?  The 3270 screen is too scary? It's ridiculous.

No Steve, it isn't ridiculous and it isn't because they're dumb.

It is because it is a more INTUITIVE interface. Why should every user have 
to become a computer programmer in order to understand and interface with a 
computer?

Why should we, as computer professionals, IGNORE what users are asking for 
and tell them "our way, or the highway..."?

Can you honestly say you know EVERY command and parameter for 
DOS/TSO/SQL/NET or any other CLI application?

And if you do, that's fair enough; computers are your job. Why should 
someone who just wants a Sales Report, or to see how a certain department is 
going against budget, have to suffer the same brain damage?

Aren't computer professionals smart enough to make these systems useful for 
the majority of people? Are they so dumb? It's ridiculous...:-)


>
> My employer has an application that "paints" 3270 screens.  That is,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It isn't that people CAN'T be trained to use 3270s; we know from the 1960s 
that that is possible. It is a question of what is EASIER and INUITIVE. 
Icons and windows fit this bill much better than command lines, that's 
all...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <mmattias@talsystems.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-06T17:35:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pj8gj.33918$lD6.25137@newssvr27.news.prodigy.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <5uai8lF1gf8odU1@mid.individual.net> <tc32o3lc0k6seb8ioeqc2mujdhe7ub2656@4ax.com>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote in message 
news:tc32o3lc0k6seb8ioeqc2mujdhe7ub2656@4ax.com...
> However, I > nearly disgorged my breakfast when I saw "Windows" and 
> "Intuitive (I
> spelt it right)" in the same sentence.

Why should you be surprised this is a commonly-held belief?

It is certainly rife enough in our own profession to believe that one 
computer language produces better/faster/smaller code, or that code runs 
faster/better on one operating system than on another.

In fact these things vary a bit by brand name, and in any case the pure 
'source language' or 'hardware platform' effects are essentially nil when 
contrasted with the effects of the individual programmer's application 
design decisions.

It's not the paintbrush, it's the artist.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <5uai8lF1gf8odU1@mid.individual.net> <8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 6 Jan 2008 12:30:27 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"

>
> >It is because it is a more INTUITIVE interface. Why should every user
have
> >to become a computer programmer in order to understand and interface with
a
> >computer?
>
> I disagree that it's more intuitive.   It is, however more familiar -
> which has the same effect.

Tool-tips exist precisely because the icons aren't sufficiently intuitive.
And as Howard implies there's a big difference between what's intuitive if
you are familiar with the situation and when you're not.  Consider a
statistical package - if you've never used one most of the interface would
be wholly meaningless - GUI or not.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-08T02:17:01+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5uen2iF1gojk7U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <5uai8lF1gf8odU1@mid.individual.net> <8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com>`

```


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 6 Jan 2008 12:30:27 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> which has the same effect.

OK, I wasn't going to push this but I guess I need to...:-)

To a person who has no computer training, what "makes sense" is considered 
more intuitive.

It is MUCH more "sensible" to take a little picture which represents a file, 
and drag it to a little picture of a dustbin, when you want to delete or 
remove it, than it is to have to type: del 
c:\mydirectory\path\myfile.whatever  ... with all the unfamiliar symbols of 
colons, backslashes, and dots which must be placed exactly, or the operation 
fails.

Users who are NOT computer programmers can learn in a few minutes how to 
activate applications and how to organise the workspace they have available, 
moving, maximising, minimising, and resizing windows which represent 
applications currently executing. Instead of having to learn a bunch of 
commands for every applcation they run, they can select from a drop down 
window simply by clicking. No spelling mistakes, no command typing errors, 
no wrong parameters to commands, it is simple and easy. And once they learn 
the basics, they can be assured that every GUI application works that way 
and they don't have to be retrained every time they start a new application.

When they start, it is a more intuitive approach; after a few hours it 
becomes "familiar".

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2008-01-07T11:06:05-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcf4o39gtkatokna1ekom68cbacl9besv3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <5uai8lF1gf8odU1@mid.individual.net> <8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com> <5uen2iF1gojk7U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Tue, 8 Jan 2008 02:17:01 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>fails.

This is a false dichotomy.  When I wanted to delete a non-VSAM file or
library member in ISPF, I brought up a list of files or members and
typed D by the name.  (IBM stupidity made it more complicated for
VSAM)  While I could do command line, the menus and panels were a heck
of a lot easier.  I prefer object - action although there are times
action - object works.  I sometimes go to the file list to do things
with files on Windows and other times bring up something like Word
Perfect or Turbotax to work on things.  GUI isn't that much of an
improvement over menuing.  What has made the current GUI environment
so much better is the availability of good screens and really potent
graphics power.  I am typing this on a computer that has a 128 meg
graphics card in addition to the 512 meg main memory.  Compare this to
the 512 meg for an entire server or only 48 meg for a computer that
had at least 100 CICS concurrent uses plus up to 50 TSO users.
Graphics just wasn't feasible for end user interface.  Now it is and
Mainframe systems have been modified to be back-ends to web-serving.
>
>Users who are NOT computer programmers can learn in a few minutes how to 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Pete.
```

```
>To a person who has no computer training, what "makes sense" is considered 
>more intuitive.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>fails.

Once you learn how to use the mouse, trash can, etc, sure.    But it
took me years to learn that I could drag something to the Windows
trash can - it wasn't until I played with a Mac that I wondered if I
could do that with Windows.   I always used the DEL key before.

We still had to learn the interface.   Once we learned it, variations
were intuitive.
```

```
On Mon, 07 Jan 2008 08:38:26 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>Once you learn how to use the mouse, trash can, etc, sure.    But it
>took me years to learn that I could drag something to the Windows
>trash can - it wasn't until I played with a Mac that I wondered if I
>could do that with Windows.   I always used the DEL key before.

I think the reason I never tried this on a Windows machine is because
the trash can is on the desktop - hidden behind my explorer window
where it isn't handy to drag to.   The first Mac I used is my wife's
24" iMac with lots of screen space for windows - and the trash on the
bar on the bottom.

I'm someone who wants my desktop to be empty.  I don't want anything
behind the windows I am using.

An alternative may be because I started using DOS then Windows before
the trash can had its current abilities.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-07T10:30:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<062e840f-9a7f-4148-b1e0-c0ed1149b058@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <5uai8lF1gf8odU1@mid.individual.net> <8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com> <5uen2iF1gojk7U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Jan 8, 2:17 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <how...@brazee.net> wrote in message
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is MUCH more "sensible" to take a little picture which represents a file,

How do they know _which_ picture represents the particular file they
want to delete ?

(Actually with KDE the 'icon' is a preview so it is a small copy of
the image, or the first few words of a document (text or ODF) or tiny
first page of a PDF etc, the 'tool tip' is a larger preview)

> and drag it to a little picture of a dustbin, when you want to delete or
> remove it, than it is to have to type: del
> c:\mydirectory\path\myfile.whatever  ... with all the unfamiliar symbols of
> colons, backslashes, and dots which must be placed exactly, or the operation
> fails.

Have you never used XTreePro or Midnight Commander ?

There are more options than MS-DOS command line vs. Windows GUI.

> Users who are NOT computer programmers can learn in a few minutes how to
> activate applications and how to organise the workspace they have available,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> no wrong parameters to commands, it is simple and easy.

> And once they learn
> the basics, they can be assured that every GUI application works that way
> and they don't have to be retrained every time they start a new application.

That was the dogma when Windows 3.0 was released. Microsoft claimed
that no retraining would be required because everything worked the
same all the time (in spite of Windows3 being completely different
from Windows2).

Then Windows95 got rid of the 'hated Windows 3 interface' (quote from
Ballmer). Then Windows98 introduced 'channels', then WindowsXP changed
everything around and made all the icons look unfamiliar, then
Office2007 changed it all completely.


> When they start, it is a more intuitive approach; after a few hours it
> becomes "familiar".

However, the issue isn't general computing it was the way that IT
designs 'user interface'. They don't do it for file handling, they do
it for the applications.  In the order entry system they don't drag
icons for products to icons of delivery trucks, nor should they. They
don't drag icons for customers to the trash can, no matter how they
feel about him.

In fact the argument is whether the mouse should be usable at all.
Business rules are structured, you can't know the price of a product
without knowing who the customer is. This often means that stuff can't
be done in arbitrary ways as poking around with a mouse allows.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-10T14:29:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<td3do3pg0rifubq4saipi1t3pidg4bdrh8@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <5uai8lF1gf8odU1@mid.individual.net> <8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com> <5uen2iF1gojk7U1@mid.individual.net> <Favgj.20088$m6.12639@newsfe18.lga> <5ug96jF1hvos8U1@mid.individual.net> <fDDgj.20303$m6.6865@newsfe18.lga> <5umdaoF1hk0gdU1@mid.individual.net> <zPphj.30164$L27.2764@bignews9.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Thu, 10 Jan 2008 08:19:12 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>> Neither do you need to be all-knowing to see that OO is the most successful paradigm to hit the computer industry. All you have to 
>> do is look around, and try to accept the reality of what you see.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>because it was superior? :-)

It depends upon what criteria used to define "superior".    Economics
matter, and if you have some projects that are helped by OO and others
that aren't hurt significantly by it - you hire programmers that can
program both ways.   And you hire programmers you don't need to train.

When the rental shops have VHS, Beta recorders aren't superior for
playing rental movies.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-11T09:25:11-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rTLhj.51217$N67.32423@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <5uai8lF1gf8odU1@mid.individual.net> <8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com> <5uen2iF1gojk7U1@mid.individual.net> <Favgj.20088$m6.12639@newsfe18.lga> <5ug96jF1hvos8U1@mid.individual.net> <fDDgj.20303$m6.6865@newsfe18.lga> <5umdaoF1hk0gdU1@mid.individual.net> <zPphj.30164$L27.2764@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <td3do3pg0rifubq4saipi1t3pidg4bdrh8@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Pete Dashwood" wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> playing rental movies.

The problem is, the OO community seems Hell-bent on eliminating anything
out there but OO, and the majority of the IS community is following like
lemmings. Listen to what they're saying. In their minds, if you aren't
using OO for everything you do, you are a stone age nitwit. Such is the
language and attitude of zealots, not reasoned debate, further affirming
my corollary to Communism. It's not rational, it's ideological. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-11T09:31:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dt5fo3lhksje6ngah75jid2p0v1pllbi8i@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <5uai8lF1gf8odU1@mid.individual.net> <8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com> <5uen2iF1gojk7U1@mid.individual.net> <Favgj.20088$m6.12639@newsfe18.lga> <5ug96jF1hvos8U1@mid.individual.net> <fDDgj.20303$m6.6865@newsfe18.lga> <5umdaoF1hk0gdU1@mid.individual.net> <zPphj.30164$L27.2764@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <td3do3pg0rifubq4saipi1t3pidg4bdrh8@4ax.com> <rTLhj.51217$N67.32423@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Fri, 11 Jan 2008 09:25:11 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>The problem is, the OO community seems Hell-bent on eliminating anything
>out there but OO, and the majority of the IS community is following like
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>my corollary to Communism. It's not rational, it's ideological. :-)

Quite a few of our problems are ideological in nature.    But I don't
see that their ideology is effecting this change very much.    Company
managers don't know anything about OO.   They may have heard of Java,
but don't know how it works.

What they care about is whether the data needs are accessible via the
Web the way they want, how they budget their capital expenditures, and
what kind of personnel to hire.   It happens that the tools to give
them what they want are primarily OO.

Also, it's getting harder and harder to hire entry level CoBOL
programmers.  

If proselytizing made that much difference, Apple would have a much
larger percentage of the office computers.
```

```
Funny you should say that... I was going to use that argument and ask if you 
still use Betamax :-)?

It is obvious that VHS could do the job and it could do so more cheaply than

engineers would never allow that their system is/was inferior.)

The world voted with its feet and went VHS.

The parallels to OO programming are pretty obvious.

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-01-11T15:42:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fm82q0$mf5$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <zPphj.30164$L27.2764@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5unk2hF1ivt72U1@mid.individual.net> <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>> It is obvious that VHS could do the job and it could do so more
>> cheaply than Betamax. The actual merit of both systems is a 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>so in things like learning curve.

Ummmm... leaving aside the video-format wars of the 1970s (Betamax started 
with higher resolution in NTSC systems) it might be argued, Mr McClendon, 
that the learning-curve is irrelevant as companies increasingly expect 
knowledge of object-oriented programming to be a hiring-criterion.

In other words... the programmer is expected to have learnt it before 
stepping in the door; how long it takes or how much it costs to learn 
doesn't matter to the organisation as long as job-candidates willing to 
work for (wage) can be found somewhere in the world.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-12T12:30:02+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5uqcftF1imtakU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <zPphj.30164$L27.2764@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5unk2hF1ivt72U1@mid.individual.net> <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <fm82q0$mf5$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```


<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fm82q0$mf5$1@reader2.panix.com...
> In article <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net>,
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> work for (wage) can be found somewhere in the world.

A very good point, Doc.

Sadly, although I don't like this, it is definitely true.

However, I have worked in a few places where they were prepared to re-train 
people to OO, and I have been involved in it.

It really doesn't help or encourage the Company to do it, when the people 
doing it are whinging all the time about how it is really just something 
else re-invented and the "learning curve" is so hard...

In the end (as you pointed out), it is simply easier (and certainly cheaper) 
to employ people who already have the skill and the mindset.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-01-12T01:33:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fm95ck$31i$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <fm82q0$mf5$1@reader2.panix.com> <5uqcftF1imtakU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5uqcftF1imtakU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fm82q0$mf5$1@reader2.panix.com...
>> In article <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net>,
>> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

[snip]

>>>Actually, the reverse is true: OO is the more expensive alternative. 
>>>Vastly more so in things like learning curve.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sadly, although I don't like this, it is definitely true.

'We are in agreement?  One of us must be wrong!', he cried, Wildely.

>
>However, I have worked in a few places where they were prepared to re-train 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>else re-invented and the "learning curve" is so hard...

Horses to water and suchlike, Mr Dashwood; I've found that in all 
situations there are folks who look for and enjoy learning and others who 
resist any change at any cost... down to exerting more effort in order to 
avoid the change than complying with the change would have required.

>
>In the end (as you pointed out), it is simply easier (and certainly cheaper) 
>to employ people who already have the skill and the mindset.

Notice my use of 'somewhere in the world', Mr Dashwood; it was a rather 
oblique reference to the process of outsourcing.  Many businesses are 
saying - or at least giving the message - that employees who understand 
their processes do not matter, what matters is getting the results desired 
in the cheapest fashion possible.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2008-01-11T20:47:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a9go3t5pq09buvomt3f2e68nt0q0odjqr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <zPphj.30164$L27.2764@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5unk2hF1ivt72U1@mid.individual.net> <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <fm82q0$mf5$1@reader2.panix.com>`

```
On Fri, 11 Jan 2008 15:42:56 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net>,
>Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>work for (wage) can be found somewhere in the world.

 In the good old days, before OO, most programmers were self-educated. Those are the guys
now objecting to OO.

Employers solved the problem by requiring a degree in computer science. Not only did it
avoid the training expense, but also insured programmers were all on the same politically
correct page AND it silenced the old timers who protested. 

In that environment, it didn't matter HOW inefficient OO was, there were no alternatives.
They COULDN'T go back to non-OO languages like Cobol or C.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-11T23:05:18-08:00

```
> >In article <MWLhj.51219$N67.12...@bignews5.bellsouth.net>,
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…

Given that OO originated in the 1960s with Simula and smalltalk was
mid-70s, then what did the programmers use for their 'self-
education' ? an etch-a-sketch ?

Most programmers in the 'good old days' were trained in in-house
courses or manufacturers' courses.

> avoid the training expense, but also insured programmers were all on the same politically
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> They COULDN'T go back to non-OO languages like Cobol or C.
```
