###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-01-12T14:53:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fmak8i$fbe$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <fm82q0$mf5$1@reader2.panix.com> <6a9go3t5pq09buvomt3f2e68nt0q0odjqr@4ax.com>`

```
In article <6a9go3t5pq09buvomt3f2e68nt0q0odjqr@4ax.com>,
Robert  <no@e.mail> wrote:
>On Fri, 11 Jan 2008 15:42:56 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

[snip]

>>In other words... the programmer is expected to have learnt it before 
>>stepping in the door; how long it takes or how much it costs to learn 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Those are the guys now objecting to OO.

I'm not quite sure about that, Mr Wagner.  Granted there may have been a 
few folks who stretched out by the fireside and did their octal arithmetic 
on the backs of shovels with a bit of coal (note to non-US readers: this 
is a reference to President Abraham Lincoln) but I recall a fair number of 
folks taking classes in programming.

Is taking a night-course in COBOL while working in a warehouse and paying 
for said course out of one's own pocket 'self education'?  What if the 
company picks up the bill?  How about being a member of a group from the 
Elephant's Knee, Wyoming office sent to Headquarters for Computer 
Operations Training?

However that 'self education' bit is defined... no matter what the 
situation there's often someone, anyone, who will say 'Ah, the old way was 
*so* much better'; to expect otherwise from programmers might result in 
disappointment.

>
>Employers solved the problem by requiring a degree in computer science.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>correct page AND it silenced the old timers who protested. 

Mr Wagner, my own experience contradicts this... but, of course, what have 
I seen?  The requirement for a CS degree is added by the folks in HR, 
along with other bits of foolishness ('must have 6 - 8 years' experience 
in a four-year-old technology'); I do not know of *any* person who 
directly supervises programmers and makes hiring decisions based on 
sheepskins.

>
>In that environment, it didn't matter HOW inefficient OO was, there were
>no alternatives.
>They COULDN'T go back to non-OO languages like Cobol or C. 

'There is no Plan B.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-12T12:25:33+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5uqc7fF1j5kmlU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <5uai8lF1gf8odU1@mid.individual.net> <8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com> <5uen2iF1gojk7U1@mid.individual.net> <Favgj.20088$m6.12639@newsfe18.lga> <5ug96jF1hvos8U1@mid.individual.net> <fDDgj.20303$m6.6865@newsfe18.lga> <5umdaoF1hk0gdU1@mid.individual.net> <zPphj.30164$L27.2764@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5unk2hF1ivt72U1@mid.individual.net> <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> so in things like learning curve.

That depends totally on an individual's ability to learn.

This has little to do with intelligence (although that certainly helps); 
more to do with attitude.

I can state this unequivocally having both learned OO and taught it to 
people with various attitudes and experience.

COBOL people find it hard because of their ingrained ITSA; novices, 
scripters and web programmers find it easy because they have no expectation 
or preconceived ideas about it.

People with an open mind who can suspend ITSA, or commute it to an ITSLIKE, 
usually do pretty well, and the learning curve is NOT "vastly more 
expensive".

If your statements were true, it could not enjoy the popularity that it 
does.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-12T14:34:15+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5uqjopF1hrpivU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <5uai8lF1gf8odU1@mid.individual.net> <8l53o3tfidbenb6jvmt719mv530joti59v@4ax.com> <5uen2iF1gojk7U1@mid.individual.net> <Favgj.20088$m6.12639@newsfe18.lga> <5ug96jF1hvos8U1@mid.individual.net> <fDDgj.20303$m6.6865@newsfe18.lga> <5umdaoF1hk0gdU1@mid.individual.net> <zPphj.30164$L27.2764@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <5unk2hF1ivt72U1@mid.individual.net> <MWLhj.51219$N67.12522@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <5uqc7fF1j5kmlU1@mid.individual.net> <G4Thj.51441$N67.10341@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:G4Thj.51441$N67.10341@bignews5.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> difficulty in learning OO vs. procedural.

And you totally missed my point.

"Complex" and "simple" are subjective terms when used in the way that you 
are using them. "Ability to learn" is subject to a conglomerate of factors, 
and varies with individuals and what they are attempting to learn.

The same person may have great difficulty learning math, but none at all 
when learning programming.

Why do you suppose people have "favourite subjects" (usually the ones they 
learn easiest)?

"No matter how smart or dumb you are, it is going to take longer to learn 
something complex than something simple" does NOT invalidate my point about 
"ability to learn", because "ability to learn" is NOT totally dependent on 
smartness or dumbness, and "complex" and "simple" in this context are 
subjective evaluations.

Whether YOU like it or not, MANY people find OO easy to learn. (The fact 
that YOU don't, says more about you than it does about OO...)

Pete.
```

```
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:5umdaoF1hk0gdU1@mid.individual.net...
>
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> > that a little picture is always fully explicit and has only the
desired
> >> > meaning - well, it ain't going to happen.
> >>
> >> The world outside of mainframes has already decided that it HAS
happened.
> >>
> >> This is a pointless argument; the landslide has started and the pebbles
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Tetchy, tetchy.  If I didn't know better, from reading your posts, I'd
say
> > that you didn't like having your omniscience questioned ...:-)
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Neither do you need to be all-knowing to see that OO is the most
successful
> paradigm to hit the computer industry. All you have to do is look around,
> and try to accept the reality of what you see.

I dunno.  I'd say that the concept of high-level languages is far more
fundamental.  It utterly transformed programming.

>
> As for being tetchy, I simply made an observation. I have no need to get
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

So go back and read what I actually said.  I didn't dispute the concept of
icons: I suggested that text rather than a little picture might be better.
You can have an icon with text on it rather than invoking a tool-tip, after
all.   (If I could find the paper, I'd state that there is research to back
that up).

I am not mainframe-centric.  I've worked on all three levels - mainframe,
minis, PC's, plus to an extent on the internet.  Therefore I feel that I
have the background and knowledge to comment and not be dismissed with an
epigram (disregarding some very silly statements that I've made in error,
which I've acknowledged).

I may be reading too much into your posts, but I frequently seem to detect a
flavour of "this is the last possible word on the subject" in them :-).

PL
```

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:d6uhj.16784$3b7.13296@newsfe23.lga...
>
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A very good point. Certainly it is arguable.

However, I would respond that HLLs are not a paradigm shift, simply an 
extension of what was there already.

Nevertheless, I do take your point.

>>
>> As for being tetchy, I simply made an observation. I have no need to get
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> icons:

Perhaps you might re-read what I actually said :-)

I never suggested that icons alone are a Gui interface.



>I suggested that text rather than a little picture might be better.
> You can have an icon with text on it rather than invoking a tool-tip, 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This goes much deeper than what either of us might prefer. Some people are 
textual others are visual, most of us are both. The problem with text is 
that it represents language, whereas an icon (with other support) can 
represent a concept that transcends language.

> I am not mainframe-centric.  I've worked on all three levels - mainframe,
> minis, PC's, plus to an extent on the internet.  Therefore I feel that I
> have the background and knowledge to comment and not be dismissed with an
> epigram (disregarding some very silly statements that I've made in error,
> which I've acknowledged).

I don't recall "dismissing you with an epigram" and I wouldn't question for 
a minute your qualification to be here. If I offended you, I'm sorry; it was 
not my intent.

>
> I may be reading too much into your posts, but I frequently seem to detect 
> a
> flavour of "this is the last possible word on the subject" in them :-).

In that case, you are definitely reading too much into them :-)

Don't mistake confidence in a position for arrogance. I stand by what I 
state until I change my mind about it, or am persuaded otherwise by better 
arguments. Therefore, I tend to put my position unequivocally.

Don't take it personally :-)

Pete.
```

```
seem reasonable and on topic and Judson, well he is starting to look like 
the new Robert.  Is something of cosmological significance going on?  :-)
```

```
Interesting. Perhaps the icons may also have cultural meanings. We all
have wastebaskets in our home so a little iconic wastebasket seems
intuitive but in Africa and/or India (where the OPD project hopes to
sell it's childrens laptop) wastebaskets are notably absent and the
icon may have no intuitive cultural meaning. Ditto for folder and file
icons.
```

```
And that is the crux of the matter.  The people who have little or no

efficient it is, are the ones who make the decisions.  GUI's are
pretty but many require more user training than a plain old green 3270
screen and take more code to produce and have more overhead.  But they
are pretty.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-01-07T19:57:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<flu06c$9qp$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <flrujb$aen$1@reader2.panix.com> <lau4o3dcnb0ihkcfc9dmhno4r3vt6f6q2a@4ax.com>`

```
In article <lau4o3dcnb0ihkcfc9dmhno4r3vt6f6q2a@4ax.com>,
SkippyPB  <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
>On Mon, 7 Jan 2008 01:17:32 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:
>
>>In article <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com>,
>>SkippyPB  <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
>>>On Sat, 5 Jan 2008 08:08:50 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

[snip]

>>>>I'm not sure how, nowadays, use of a graphics-intensive Linux application 
>>>>gets costed-out to a department... might be interesting to look into.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>efficient it is, are the ones who make the decisions.

I can't make that kind of assertion, either.  For the most part I do not 
know the reasoning behind my client's deciding to give Signing Authority 
to a particular person... and it isn't my job to determine whether that 
decision was justified.  Someone with Signing Authority calls the tune and 
as a professional I may say 'In my experience (reasons) would cause 
(action) to suit your claimed goals in (ways) which (measurements 
indicate) are superior'... 

... but say that or not I then ask myself 'Can I do what they are asking 
and not compromise my standards of Professional Integrity... or not?'

If my standards fit the stretching then I do my best to give folks what 
they pay for and a little bit more... if not then I give my notice and put 
my papers out on the street.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-07T13:08:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1815o3tt0nin2sib31cifititr0hpe7sbr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <flrujb$aen$1@reader2.panix.com> <lau4o3dcnb0ihkcfc9dmhno4r3vt6f6q2a@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 07 Jan 2008 14:14:13 -0500, SkippyPB
<swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>And that is the crux of the matter.  The people who have little or no
>knowledge of what lies beneath the hood, what makes it work and how
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>are pretty.

But all of the code isn't mine.    My application might have some SQL
and the code to create some XML.    Someone else puts this on a
portal, and the user uses a browser that knows all of the GUI stuff.

Next time I create a "screen", I still won't write any of the GUI
stuff, I just handle the data.

Periodically in the past there have been some attempts to move CoBOL
shops out of the report writing business - sometimes with mainframe
programs, and later with Crystal Reports.    Having an applications
programmer spend time in programming for a new page size for one of
our customers was a waste.   This is being achieve now.

The whole system is more complex - we have somebody controlling system
access, another person controlling data access and update, someone
else writing the mainframe data retrieval and update, someone else
running the portal, someone supporting the PC, someone else
controlling PC security - and other software (browsers, report
generators) selected by the user.     But *my* share of that process
is narrower.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-07T14:42:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<q8wgj.42865$Mu4.3307@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <flnduh$oj0$1@reader2.panix.com> <3advn3dqjr3ke83pa789k6i42irdorrpvf@4ax.com> <flrujb$aen$1@reader2.panix.com> <lau4o3dcnb0ihkcfc9dmhno4r3vt6f6q2a@4ax.com>`

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:
>
> And that is the crux of the matter.  The people who have little or no
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> are pretty.

I don't have a problem when they want it "pretty" for commercial reasons,
like sales. But when it's a paid clerk entering data, the questions should
be about efficiency, accuracy, cost (development, training, maintenance)
and reliability. For many business applications, character interfaces beat
GUI hands down in all those areas.

A symptom I often bemoan is that when people become managers, after
a while they tend to forget that what they do is not the business. It's what
the people in the trenches do that is the business. I think the same thing
applies here. Managers don't enter data, they examine processed stats,
graphs, etc. For those kind of things, GUI makes a lot of sense. Once
again, they're forgetting that what they do is not the business. For some
managers it's not forgetting, because they never knew in the first place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2008-01-08T00:21:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<flufmo$h2v$1@reader2.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <flrujb$aen$1@reader2.panix.com> <lau4o3dcnb0ihkcfc9dmhno4r3vt6f6q2a@4ax.com> <q8wgj.42865$Mu4.3307@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <q8wgj.42865$Mu4.3307@bignews7.bellsouth.net>,
Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

[snip]

>A symptom I often bemoan is that when people become managers, after
>a while they tend to forget that what they do is not the business. It's what
>the people in the trenches do that is the business. I think the same thing
>applies here. Managers don't enter data, they examine processed stats,
>graphs, etc.

First, an Olde Jokee:

Upon reaching a certain level of management a fellow is brought into a 
Board Member's office.  The 12-foot-tall doors swing silently shut, yard 
after yard of three-inch-thick carpet is traversed, a butter-soft leather 
chair is settled into... and two bright, sharp, eyes glare down.

'Young man, now that you're at this stage of your career... tell me, what 
does EnormousCo make?'

'Uhhhh... well.... ummmmmm... it sells insurance/makes (product)/refines 
and sells (commodity)/(performs other function).'

'Wrong... EnormousCo makes *money*.'

I don't know how many times I've asked a manager for or been told by a 
manager that I'd be given some time to watch how people enter data/process 
claims/make trades/deal with customer orders... but I know I've never 
actually been allowed to do it.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-05T06:18:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com>`

```
"Scott" <NoSpam@Spamblocker.org> wrote:
> THE biggest problem with the mainframe is that IBM never embraced a GUI
> screen. That's what it's all about. Why is everyone migrating from
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> development on the mainframe, we'd still have strong mainframes today.

You're talking about the user side. On the programmer side the equivalent
of GUI "bells & whistles" is OO. Neither is about performance or
functionality, but all about "Wow! That's neat! I want it!"

And yes, I'm programming using OO every day, have been doing so for over a
year, and I have still yet to see anything I couldn't have done simpler and
easier as procedural. After all, OO is implemented procedurally at the
machine code level. OO is, functionally, just a wrapper, so it's false to
say it couldn't be done procedurally, because it is procedural at the bottom.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-06T12:43:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5uaj21F1hf6q1U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "Scott" <NoSpam@Spamblocker.org> wrote:
>> THE biggest problem with the mainframe is that IBM never embraced a GUI
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> bottom.

If you really believe that, then you have missed the major benefits of OO.

If you are running OO on a mainframe, there is some truth in your points; 
your objects ARE "implemented procedurally at the machine code level." 
(Although there are still conceptual and design benefits from using 
objects.) However, as soon as you start to instantiate objects remotely 
across the network you have a completely different paradigm. Encapsulation 
permits any number of objects to run in parallel, which your procedural code 
has to be specially written to support. (And it adds complexity which 
exacerbates maintaining it...)

OO is a lot more than "just a wrapper" although I agree it can be that, if 
that is how you write it.

You can't drop procedural modules into web pages, or have them run on ANY 
platform across the network, at ANY location anywhere on Earth...

Objects can do this, and do it easily.

Think again. :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-05T19:46:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ApWfj.62806$K27.23987@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5uaj21F1hf6q1U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Scott" <NoSpam@Spamblocker.org> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> If you really believe that, then you have missed the major benefits of OO.

OO is simply a different paradigm for solving problems, no more, no less.
You appear to assign mystical properties to it.

> If you are running OO on a mainframe, there is some truth in your points; your objects ARE "implemented procedurally at the 
> machine code level." (Although there are still conceptual and design benefits from using objects.) However, as soon as you start 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> OO is a lot more than "just a wrapper" although I agree it can be that, if that is how you write it.

The overwhelming majority of PC on the planet are Intel, or Intel compatible.
None of those CPU's knows what an object is. OO *IS* a "wrapper". Q.E.D.

> You can't drop procedural modules into web pages, or have them run on ANY platform across the network, at ANY location anywhere on 
> Earth...

CGI. Pete, you appear to be confusing interrupt driven processing with OO.
There is not a recursive algorithm that cannot be coded iteratively, and there
is not an OO algorithm that cannot be coded procedurally. Just fact. You
appear to be blinded by the dog and pony show.

> Objects can do this, and do it easily.
>
> Think again. :-)

I've been thinking. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-06T19:44:13+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5ubbm4F1giistU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5uaj21F1hf6q1U1@mid.individual.net> <ApWfj.62806$K27.23987@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:ApWfj.62806$K27.23987@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> CGI.

CGI is SERVER SIDE code. You can't drop modules on to Web Pages and have 
them execute.

It isn't me who is confused...

>Pete, you appear to be confusing interrupt driven processing with OO.

Give me a break... I was writing interrupt driven processes long before OO 
was ever considered.

I DO know the difference.

> There is not a recursive algorithm that cannot be coded iteratively, and 
> there
> is not an OO algorithm that cannot be coded procedurally. Just fact. You
> appear to be blinded by the dog and pony show.

Not true,but I see no point in arguing it.  It IS true that there is no 
serial algorithm that cannot be coded procedurally, but there are other 
classes of algorithms (viz. Functional, Cellular, and multithreaded 
Heuristic programming techniques...) which simply cannot be coded serially. 
The Von Neumann model is just one of many.

I see no dog and pony; I see someone who has missed some major concepts...

It really doesn't matter.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-05T19:54:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> And yes, I'm programming using OO every day, have been doing so for over a
> year, and I have still yet to see anything I couldn't have done simpler and
> easier as procedural.

I've been doing it right at 10 months (so I guess I'm a little behind
you), but I'm of a completely different opinion.  OO has saved me
*loads* of time.  In fact, now when I hear a problem being described, my
mind automatically starts breaking it up into the objects that will be
affected, and the information they need to exchange to make it happen.

Just this past week, I had to write some code for a process that was
very similar to another one.  What I did was look at what the old one
did, look at what I needed, *and* look at future known requirements, and
I was able to break the original object up into three objects, then
subclass the second to make mine.  I didn't have to write *any* of that
code, because it had already been written.

(It was a "data extraction" query.  The top-level object is "BaseQuery",
which has a name, how often to run, what user created it, etc.  The next
level is "BaseReportQuery", which is designed to run queries off
reports.  It adds word-search capability, and Word and Excel output
options.  Finally, the next level represents the actual object from the
system ("IllnessQuery", in my case - it searches occupational illness
investigations).  It contains the input for the query.

I've also written an MVC (Model/View/Controller) framework for PHP.  It
took a little while to get it tweaked the way I wanted it for the first
few pages, but once I got that, it actually helped me make further pages
much more quickly.  (As an added bonus, HTML isn't embedded in PHP files!)

The "control script" creates the "UI Service", which provides an XML
tree in memory that has certain page attributes.  Then, the control
script will instantiate "Page" objects if there's dynamic stuff to be
done, and they append data to the XML tree.  Finally, the control script
tells the UI service to render the page, and the XML tree is rendered
using an XSLT stylesheet.  This is done on the server, so I don't have
to rely on the browser to do it.  There are other objects (the ones to
represent the actual data), and a database service that creates an
abstraction layer from the database.

Yes, all this *could* have been done procedurally.  However, I don't
think I would have come up with that design had I been thinking
procedurally.

I don't know if you were around when I posted my "My First C#" post.
The feedback I got from it (in here) was really, really great.  In fact,
I didn't realize the brilliance of some of that advice until I got to my
new job and started doing OO exclusively every day.  The biggest thing
that helped me, I think, is the "one method, one responsibility"
concept.  If the building blocks are small enough, you can put them
together quickly.  If they're too big, you have to take the time to
either split them apart, or add "another" parameter to alter the
behavior under a certain condition.  (And yes, it is valid OO to have a
method whose one responsibility is putting the pieces together - in
fact, that's probably the main method in the class.)

> After all, OO is implemented procedurally at the
> machine code level. OO is, functionally, just a wrapper, so it's false to
> say it couldn't be done procedurally, because it is procedural at the bottom.

For me, it's been a mindset shift - and I have seen it benefit me.
Maybe I'm in a shop where it's done right.  :)  (Not implying anything
about yours, of course...)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-06T08:20:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>>
>> And yes, I'm programming using OO every day, have been doing so for over a
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> code, because it had already been written. <snipped>

Had that code been already written procedural code, with comparably strict
definition and usage attributes, you would have been in the same situation.
It appears to me, from your and Pete's comments, that you may be unfamiliar
with very good modular structured techniques. I have done all of what you
describe above, many times, using well defined, modular procedural code.
And the underlying support system to get it done was far more streamlined
and efficient than the vastly bloated, inefficient OO tools we use now. I
have always worked toward modular, reusable code, with very good result.
IMO, it is using the principles of well designed modules, data hiding, and
other long ago defined structured principles that you are benefitting from,
not OO per se. As I've said, I have yet to see one thing done in OO that
could not have been done as easily, or more easily, and far more efficiently,
using good procedural techniques.

> I don't know if you were around when I posted my "My First C#" post.
> The feedback I got from it (in here) was really, really great.  In fact,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> fact, that's probably the main method in the class.)

I agree. But all of those principles were already part of structured design,
had people actually used it. I've been using them assiduously for decades;
perhaps that's why I see no real benefit to OO. When I look at OO, I see a
vastly overcomplicated technique that gives me nothing I haven't had for a
long time, and comes at a great price in complexity and inefficiency.

>> After all, OO is implemented procedurally at the
>> machine code level. OO is, functionally, just a wrapper, so it's false to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> about yours, of course...)

Mental paradigms can be powerful. I remember a similar epiphany when the
principles of structured design and what it meant really "hit me". I'm glad
the OO paradigm was helpful for you. But perhaps you just haven't been in a
shop where structured procedural was "done right". :-)

Sigh. All of this is merely of academic interest, though, for the world has
well and truly followed the Pied Piper of OO, for better or worse. It does
work, and I'm using it. I just grieve that this over-complex, inefficient
path has been chosen for us, when a better, simpler, much more efficient one
was already in place, had it been used with wisdom and discipline.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-06T10:38:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dd4626b6-fea1-448c-9fb0-d7cf7cf25770@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com> <0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Jan 7, 3:20 am, "Judson McClendon"

> Had that code been already written procedural code, with comparably strict
> definition and usage attributes, you would have been in the same situation.

C++ solved for C many problems which did not exist in Cobol, nor in
applications typically written in Cobol. In fact some of these
problems in C had been solved for years in Cobol (such as by dynamic
loading of modules).

If the problems which OO solves are not problem to a Cobol application
then there is no need to use OO.

However, OO in Cobol does provide one feature that cannot be done
easily other ways and that is that they may be multiple objects,
wheras a module implementation only naturally allows a single use.

For example I developed a 'raw output' module for an application I was
converting to Fujitsu. The application had HPLaser commands built in
(I didn't write it) wheras Fujitsu generates postscript.

Implementing this as a module did the job fine, but one program needed
two streams.  To change the module to handle two outputs would have
required a great deal of work.  Or I could have just generated a 2nd
module from the same source and used different call names.

But using the OO implementation just required another 'new' and just
worked correctly.

If the applications that you design will never need multiple objects
(because it is designed around the limitations of procedural code)
then you will not see the need or use of OO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-06T20:45:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2cWdnUoouf_CPRzanZ2dnUVZ_ternZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com> <0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>>> And yes, I'm programming using OO every day, have been doing so for over a
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> describe above, many times, using well defined, modular procedural code.

I've seen places where the type of programming I'm doing now would fit 
nicely into the system I used to work on.  Of course, getting permission 
to do it would be the tough part.  It's hard to pitch a perceived 
benefit most directly visible to the developers.  (The "increased 
reliability for the users concept" is hard to quantify.  "So, Jim, how 
many problems didn't we have this week?")

> And the underlying support system to get it done was far more streamlined
> and efficient than the vastly bloated, inefficient OO tools we use now.

What tools seem bloated and inefficient to you?  (I'm not saying they 
aren't - I'm just curious as to what you're referring.)

> I
> have always worked toward modular, reusable code, with very good result.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> using good procedural techniques.

Sometimes, though, you have to do the best you can in the environment 
you've been given.  In the Unisys 2200 environment using their Network 
Database Server (DMS), you get a schema work area depending on how you 
invoke the schema.  Your choices are working-storage, linkage, or 
common-storage.  (Common storage is a Unisys thing - all linked modules 
have access to this area of memory.)  However, it not only copies the 
schema area into the program, but the "DMCA" (Database Management 
Communication Area - basically, the block of information that holds your 
connection state) is copied in too.

The problem is, you can't realistically bring it into linkage, because 
the 200+ 01-level record definitions would get copied in.  So, you're 
left with working-storage, which makes it visible to your program only, 
or common-storage, which makes it visible to programs linked in.  Either 
way, the design of it natually lends itself to a tight coupling with the 
database.  Unless subroutines are coded with the schema defined the 
exact same way, they don't have access to it (even though the thread has 
been established).

Now, that being said (the perfect set-up for the "but I just can't do 
it" excuse), it COULD be done.  However, without a complete database 
abstraction layer, these limitations are still going to be there.  So, 
you get these monolithic programs with two or three screenful's worth of 
copy statements.  The data's wide open, because it has to be to work.

I would absolutely love to create a data abstraction layer for that 
system.  :)  But, getting all the program changed to use it, getting 
them tested, then getting them out the door make it not feasible.

<reminiscing>
There was one program that I broke up into nested subprograms.  It was 
our batch routine validator, and it did column-by-column verification of 
inputs, ostensibly to catch errors without having the overhead of 
starting a batch run.  I modified it to have a nested subprogram for 
each transaction code, then within these, I copied in the screen 
working-storage (which had meaningful names), and changed the validation 
to use the names.  However, because of the whole schema thing, I had to 
keep paragraphs in the main program to be able to do data validation 
against the database.  :(  The structure change benefitted no one but me 
(well, and the other folks who got to maintain it), and if I hadn't been 
quick on some other code, I would not have had time to do it.

Ideally, though, each of those would have been its own separate module.
</reminiscing>

> Sigh. All of this is merely of academic interest, though, for the world has
> well and truly followed the Pied Piper of OO, for better or worse. It does
> work, and I'm using it. I just grieve that this over-complex, inefficient
> path has been chosen for us, when a better, simpler, much more efficient one
> was already in place, had it been used with wisdom and discipline.

I really don't see the excessive overhead.  That's why I asked you what 
environment you're working in.  But hey, we've got to have *something* 
for all these cores in our modern computers to do!  ;)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-07T07:36:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FVpgj.66851$L%6.5193@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com> <0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <2cWdnUoouf_CPRzanZ2dnUVZ_ternZ2d@comcast.com>`

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
> *something* for all these cores in our modern computers to do!  ;)

Been a while since I've seen an actual 'core'. :-)

Wow, where do I begin? When you are casually instantiating and disposing
of objects, have you ever stopped to think what is actually going on to make
that happen? How about inheritance, overloading, etc? A truly vast amount
of code is being executed to do it. The OO paradigm is highly abstracted
from how current CPUs actually function, and implementing this high level
of abstraction takes a lot of work, both to develop the OO development
system, and to execute it. The process is, by definition, "very inefficient"
from a hardware perspective.

I'm actually using .NET (Visual Studio 2005). As for "efficiency" in .NET,
the CLR adds a whole new level of inefficiency on top of what I described
above, because it's interpreted code. But I'm experienced and objective
enough not to blame OO for the CLR inefficiencies. :-)

As bone-headed as many of the design decisions were in .NET, I have to be
truly impressed at the massive accomplishment. As it is, there are big gaps
and booboos in .NET. Just write a program using .NET to meticulously set
fairly narrow margins on a printed page and make it look right across
different printers and print preview. Whoever designed that set of classes
was absolutely clueless, and clearly had no concept about how they should
have been implemented.

Understand, I'm not arguing against abstraction. Abstraction can be very
powerful and useful. All HLL compilers are abstractions. Even labels and
macros in assembly are abstractions. But when you create an enormously
complex and expensive abstraction, you need to get a very good return on
the investment, not only in development, but in learning and using it. As
has been pointed out by others in these threads, there just is not good
data to show that OO is worth the enormous price the industry has paid
for it, compared to other, simpler, alternatives. The reverse is closer
to reality. I can already hear the screams of indignation from the OO
proponents. :-) Let's take a look at an earlier "paradigm revolution".

In the 1970s I spearheaded implementing the relatively new structured
methodologies at my shop. Within a year I had solid hard data to prove,
beyond any question, that the structured methodologies were giving big
paybacks in shorter development time, less bugs, easier maintenance, etc.
The data was in no way ambiguous, it was as clearly definitive as one
could want. Not one person involved, even those who had been dead set
against it, failed to be absolutely convinced. The programmers I had had
drag, kicking and screaming, into structured programming, were beyond
enthusiastic to ecstatic. I have seen nothing remotely comparable from OO.
It's clear that the learning curve for OO development is much steeper than
for structured. At the same time, I have seen project after project using
OO go belly up, or vastly over budget and time, at all my clients. Why,
if OO is so much better?

When I protested these things here, I was always told "You just haven't
used OO enough; when you do, you'll see." Well, I've been immersed in
OO for over a year, and I'm now comfortable developing with it. I love
many of the design tools, like Visual Studio's IntelliSense. I can now do
whatever I want using OO without much difficulty. The prime "difficulty"
being finding the specific functions I want among the vast set of .NET
classes. Improved documentation and search would mitigate this a lot.
(Note to Microsoft: when someone searches for "xxx", *first* give them
the definition and usage of "xxx", *followed* by the 4,000 bug reports
and other articles on "xxx", not vice-versa. Duh!)

I would be delighted to discover that OO is, indeed, the way we needed
to go. It's obvious we're all going there, good or bad, like it or not.
Better to be 'good' and 'like' than not. But I'm still unimpressed and
unconvinced.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-08T19:31:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6IqdnXzvIcNDrBnanZ2dnUVZ_sOrnZ2d@comcast.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<FVpgj.66851$L%6.5193@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Wow, where do I begin? When you are casually instantiating and disposing
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> from a hardware perspective.

I'll buy that - but I'm not sure that it's necessarily "very" 
inefficient in all incarnations of OO runtime.  My OO PHP pages actually 
seem to run faster than their "all in one script" counterparts, and the 
CPU usage looks the same.

> I'm actually using .NET (Visual Studio 2005). As for "efficiency" in .NET,
> the CLR adds a whole new level of inefficiency on top of what I described
> above, because it's interpreted code. But I'm experienced and objective
> enough not to blame OO for the CLR inefficiencies. :-)

Well, yeah.  Any VM is going to be more inefficient, especially if 
things aren't run repetitively.  Most VMs cache the "compiled" version 
of the interpreted intermediate language, so the first execution is 
horrid, but future ones are much better.

> As
> has been pointed out by others in these threads, there just is not good
> data to show that OO is worth the enormous price the industry has paid
> for it, compared to other, simpler, alternatives. The reverse is closer
> to reality.

I don't know - it's really helped me.  Maybe it's just something I "get" 
(or that OO helps me "get").

> In the 1970s I spearheaded implementing the relatively new structured
> methodologies at my shop. Within a year I had solid hard data to prove,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> for structured.

I'll agree with that last statement too.  It took me several years to 
come close to understanding it, and several months of actually coding it 
before I understood (and am now comfortable with my level of understanding).

> At the same time, I have seen project after project using
> OO go belly up, or vastly over budget and time, at all my clients. Why,
> if OO is so much better?

I think that some of it is hype, and some of it is the fact that the 
people developing it just didn't understand the paradigm.  It is very 
easy to write poorly-performing OO code.  :)

(I know, that sounds like what you say below...)

> When I protested these things here, I was always told "You just haven't
> used OO enough; when you do, you'll see."

But it's not.  :)  What I'm saying is that the "easy" and "anyone can do 
it" parts are bull - it can be done, but it takes an understanding that 
isn't easy to come by.

> (Note to Microsoft: when someone searches for "xxx", *first* give them
> the definition and usage of "xxx", *followed* by the 4,000 bug reports
> and other articles on "xxx", not vice-versa. Duh!)

heh heh heh...  You know, those are ranked by the order they're most 
needed...  ;)

> I would be delighted to discover that OO is, indeed, the way we needed
> to go. It's obvious we're all going there, good or bad, like it or not.
> Better to be 'good' and 'like' than not. But I'm still unimpressed and
> unconvinced.

Are you dissatisfied with the code you're producing (from a performance, 
maintainability, or clarity standpoint)?  Or, does it just bother you 
that it's generating more machine instructions than you think it needs 
to?  (I don't mean that last question to be smart-alecky...  I tried 5 
different ways of wording it, and that was the best I could come up with.)
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13o8f8nlcm1eud1@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com> <0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <2cWdnUoouf_CPRzanZ2dnUVZ_ternZ2d@comcast.com> <FVpgj.66851$L%6.5193@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6IqdnXzvIcNDrBnanZ2dnUVZ_sOrnZ2d@comcast.com>`

```

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
news:6IqdnXzvIcNDrBnanZ2dnUVZ_sOrnZ2d@comcast.com...
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>

How many OO projects fail because of  these "inefficiencies"?  I believe you 
can still program "procedurally" in most OO languages if necessary. 
Premature optimization is generally a bad thing.  Studies show only 5% of 
the code is where most of the time is spent. Per you argument we should we 
not use compiled or intrepreted languages because they are less efficient 
than assembler. Well think of all the machine cycles wasted in assembler, we 
can save all of them by just coding in machine language.

Or we can realize that higher level abstractions save programmer time and 
lead to fewer errors and programmer time is more important than machine 
time. O fcourse there are exceptions but business programming does not often 
encouter them.

In my experience OO is much harder to learn than a procedural language like 
COBOL and it is easier to make mistakes. Part of this is a more complicated 
environment such as multi-threading. But incremental learning and experience 
can eventually lead to good code which is easier to maintain.  There is a 
lot more to OO programming than what first appears.  Quick analogies between 
procedural concepts and OO concepts at first lead on to think that OO is 
just "new names for old concepts" but OO can lead to design patterns that 
are highly flexible and maintainable. Design patterns like Decorator let you 
add new capabilities without touching the exisiting code.  While you can 
simulate such things with subroutines I do not recall seeing some of these 
design patterns before OO programming came along. I always though 
inheritance was the reason OO was invented (because of the emphasis books 
seemed tom plce on it) but delegation is more flexible and can accomplish 
many of the same objectives with less downside effects.
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2008-01-09T08:52:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Rc5hj.44132$Mu4.9189@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>> Judson McClendon wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> important than machine time. O fcourse there are exceptions but business programming does not often encouter them.

You're right, of course. I wasn't trying to suggest that because something is
machine inefficient it must be bad. I'm only saying that machine efficiency or
inefficiency is a factor that should be weighed into the whole ROI equation.
With OO, I'm for more concerned about people costs.

> In my experience OO is much harder to learn than a procedural language like COBOL and it is easier to make mistakes. Part of this 
> is a more complicated environment such as multi-threading. But incremental learning and experience can eventually lead to good 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> effects.

I agree there are things for which OO paradigms are well suited. Perhaps
the real issue is: Why don't we use OO for the things it's well suited
for, and default to much simpler procedural solutions for everything else?
Is it necessary to make everything more complex just so we can use OO on
those issues for which OO is more effective? Unless the benefits of OO are
truly astronomical, that would seem to be a poor choice, but it's the one
that's apparently been made.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<m6u9o3tk61jqq33jutp0jm51ggv5q8rf81@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com> <0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <2cWdnUoouf_CPRzanZ2dnUVZ_ternZ2d@comcast.com> <FVpgj.66851$L%6.5193@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6IqdnXzvIcNDrBnanZ2dnUVZ_sOrnZ2d@comcast.com> <13o8f8nlcm1eud1@corp.supernews.com> <Rc5hj.44132$Mu4.9189@bignews7.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Wed, 9 Jan 2008 08:52:33 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>I agree there are things for which OO paradigms are well suited. Perhaps
>the real issue is: Why don't we use OO for the things it's well suited
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>that's apparently been made.

We tend to use the tools we have and know.    Scripts and batch files
are going to continue to be procedural, but let me picture an OO shop:

There's a database, programs creating XML, and GUI front ends on the
users' desks.     If they ask me to create a report program, I can
write something in CoBOL, and they can schedule it to run
periodically, picking up the report from distribution.

Or they can ask for the data delivered to their computer in a way that
they can massage it as they wish.

Tools are available to create the database query based upon their
authorized access.   Whether they are OO or procedural doesn't matter
to the users.    But if they are OO, they are easier to integrate with
other OO tools.

What is the advantage in eschewing those tools and writing batch jobs?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-09T20:20:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13oasmna55peb02@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com> <0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <2cWdnUoouf_CPRzanZ2dnUVZ_ternZ2d@comcast.com> <FVpgj.66851$L%6.5193@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6IqdnXzvIcNDrBnanZ2dnUVZ_sOrnZ2d@comcast.com> <13o8f8nlcm1eud1@corp.supernews.com> <d8p9o3tmcurmqf10l0dukksd11fv05gi5m@4ax.com>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
news:d8p9o3tmcurmqf10l0dukksd11fv05gi5m@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 8 Jan 2008 22:18:58 -0500, "Charles Hottel"
> <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> application.

I was slow to get into OO.  I just did not see much benefit over procedural. 
Actually I hated it at first because I focused in on the OO things that were 
more complicated than COBOL.  Things like simply formatting a number. A lot 
of business programs are CRUD (create/read/update/delete ) and COBOL is 
great for that and OO seems like overkill.

I found the learning curve for learning Java to be very steep.  I am not 
sure if my COBOL background had any thing to do with that or not.  Even 
though I already knew C and a lot of Java syntax is modeled on C that did 
not help much. Many times I have thought that I am just getting older and my 
mind is not as sharp as it once was. I have read a shelf full of Java books 
and there are still lots of things I do not know very much about including 
featues  for which I do not understand the rationale.  Actually I read 
several books twice. After finding the indexes wanting and my recall not all 
I hoped, I made a second pass and created notes/index references that I can 
search whenever I cannnot find something I want.   The Java API is huge. 
There is good documentation but it can be hard to locate what you want 
especially at first. Often I knew the class I wanted docs for, but I could 
not remember the package name. Finally I realized that I was just 
overwhelmed with details.  Knowing about the study that shows most people 
can only hold 7 +- 2 new things in their mind helped me understand this. 
Learnig COBOL  was much easier.

When I first tried to read the Design Patterns book by the GOF I could not 
follow it. I was still at a stage were I had to look up definitions of 
terms.  I did not have any code examples in my head or enough coding 
experience.  I was not ready. My first level of breakthrough came when I 
finally really understood class, adstract class, java interfaces, enums and 
type, and inheritance and their relationships, how they are similar and how 
they are different. In OO the world 'interface' is overloaded so as be 
confusing.  Once I realized the various meanings and context it was a big 
help. Next I read Effective Java.  A lot of it I did not completely get but 
a lot of it I did. It contained many of the Java idioms that you need to 
know.  I finally started to like Java a little more. I went back to the GOF 
Design Patterns book. Now I could understand it without looking up 
definitions. I understood it but I found it hard to recall the patterns. 
Many patterns are kind of abstract.  That plus the writing style made 
learning and recall harder. So once again I stopped reading it and switched 
to Head First Design Patterns. I am about half-way through.  I love the 
approach.  There is lots of rartionale and examples and the info is 
presented in a way that makes it easy to recall. I am starting to realize 
and appreciate what you can do with objects.  Eventually I will reread the 
GOF book and Effective Java again and I am sure I will pick up many of the 
things I did not understand before. I am much more enthusiastic about OO 
now, even with all the pitfalls and other confusing corners that I know 
about and have mentioned in other previous postings to this newsgroup.

I think there is a "chicken and egg, which comes first" problem in learning 
OO.  It may be present in other learning situations as well but it 
particularly apparent in learning OO.  There is not step by step, approach 
that guarantees that you will learn at step x everthing you need to learn 
the concepts at step x+1.  I seem to have to keep iterating/reviewing over 
and over again, each time being a little better prepared to understand more 
than the last time.  I also tiknk I would have learned things faster if I 
had had some real world projects to work on at work instead of mosting 
solving exercises from books.

So why did I bore you all with all of this? Well I just thought some of my 
experiences might be helpful for anyone who want to add OO to their 
skillset.  Of course eveyone is different and what works for me may not work 
for you, but it might.  I am still surprised that OO has caught on as it 
has. I have met plenty of mediocre COBOL programmers who I helped with 
things I though any professional COBOL programmer should know.  If they 
can't learn COBOL I don't know how they could learn OO.  I would think that 
the number of people capable of learnig OO well would be far less than those 
capable of learning COBOL. Maybe I just don't have a good aptitude for OO, 
but with persistence I am slowly making progress.  I wish I had gotton into 
it much earlier and learned it in a slower, more incremental way rather that 
trying to play catch up on years of OO development and progress.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2008-01-11T00:56:35+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5umffkF1ij02fU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com> <0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <2cWdnUoouf_CPRzanZ2dnUVZ_ternZ2d@comcast.com> <FVpgj.66851$L%6.5193@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6IqdnXzvIcNDrBnanZ2dnUVZ_sOrnZ2d@comcast.com> <13o8f8nlcm1eud1@corp.supernews.com> <d8p9o3tmcurmqf10l0dukksd11fv05gi5m@4ax.com> <13oasmna55peb02@corp.supernews.com>`

```


"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:13oasmna55peb02@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message 
…[96 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Really interesting post, Charlie.

I am always interested in how people learn stuff and problems they 
encounter.

I also found OO concepts "hard" when I first tried to grapple with them 
(after 30 years of procedural programming using mainly COBOL).

 Using OO COBOL did NOT make it easier.

Fortunately, I decided it might be good to learn Java about that time so I 
decided to do that. As I so often do (did the same with C# and VS 2005) I 
pretended I knew nothing and approached it as an Absolute Beginner.

I don't measure what I'm trying to learn against what I already know 
(ITSA... it's a...) or have any EXPECTATIONS as to how it OUGHT to work... I 
write onto a clean slate.

Also fortunately, I picked what was (for me) the perfect book to start Java 
with. Written by an insightful, capable, and very witty man, with a wicked 
sense of humour, my Java learning progressed in leaps and bounds.

As Java is totally OO, so did my understanding of OO concepts.

I went back to OO COBOL and couldn't understand why I had previously found 
it difficult... :-)

That was about 12 years ago.

I've moved on since then, and I would NEVER go back to procedural coding.

For a number of years I wrote components in OO COBOL.

It was good.

Now I write them in C#.

It's not better... it is LIGHT YEARS better! (Hence, I won't really join the 
"debate" here about OO. For me, it is not debatable...  The proof of the 
pudding is in the eating, and my pudding has become delicious, thank you 
very much :-))

I'm not interested in Batch Processing and I'll be glad when the need for it 
is gone (about 20 years from now...)

I watch the debate here without emotion because I'm beyond feeling anything 
about it.

I see people writing passionately about how unnecessary OO is, as if the 
majority of the programmers in the world are idiots who have accepted a 
gigantic con... Having spent time with quite a few of them, I know they are 
not.:-)

But, at the end of the day, it isn't the OO languages which are in 
decline...

It's a landslide...all the bleating about "Tremor?! What tremor? I felt no 
tremor.... did you feel a tremor???" won't stop the avalanche. Whether you 
felt what caused it or not, if you want to be part of computer programming 
in the 21st century, you better get with the program, literally.

Learn OO. (Or take up blacksmithing, swan upping, fletching, or lace 
making...all respectable and skilled trades, but totally irrelevant to the 
modern world.)

Pete.
```

```
"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> actually seem to run faster than their "all in one script" counterparts, and the CPU usage looks the same.

Scripting is inherently inefficient too, possibly accounting for most of
the overhead in that situation.

>> As
>> has been pointed out by others in these threads, there just is not good
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't know - it's really helped me.  Maybe it's just something I "get" (or that OO helps me "get").

Perhaps so; you're not the only one to express that. We're all different.

>> In the 1970s I spearheaded implementing the relatively new structured
>> methodologies at my shop. Within a year I had solid hard data to prove,
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
> smart-alecky...  I tried 5 different ways of wording it, and that was the best I could come up with.)

No, I'm pretty satisifed with the actual code I'm producing right now,
as to clarity and effectiveness. I've become pretty comfortable in OO,
as far as getting it done.

My biggest concern is that the overall people cost of learning and using
OO is far too great; the ROI is insufficient. OO effectively eliminates
the word "simple" from the process.

Believe me, I don't have a problem with complexity, when it's warranted.
I once wrote a series of reports that had a section so complex that, even
heavily commented, I put a heading over the code something to the effect
"Abandon all hope, ye who enter here." Several years later, a programmer
under a different IS manager decided to modify the code. Naturally, she
paid no attention to my warnings, broke the code and screamed "Help!"
The IS manager called to chew me out. But after I explained the system
requirements that his predecessor had given me, why I had done it that
way, and that I had provided and documented an easy and simple way to
accomplish such changes, he backed off and chewed out his programmer for
ignoring my warnings. I once wrote a compiler and P-code interpreter in
COBOL for a utility program I marketed, because COBOL was the only
compiler available to me across the target platforms. I also wrote a
graphics library in COBOL, from dot plot to line and character draw, and
graphics data compression algorithms, including an assembly language
printer driver on the receiving end so I could send 8 bit binary graphics
data from an EBCDIC mainframe across a 7 bit ASCII connection to an 8 bit
graphics laser printer to print ultrasound obstetrics charts. When I
tried to explain how that all worked to the client, his eyes glazed over.
My point is that I've often dealt with complexity, enjoy solving complex
problems, when the complexity is for very good reason. IMO, OO is not a
sufficient reason for this level of complexity.

Perhaps a more balanced approach than the "anything not an object is Bad"
approach would be better. Just because recursive logic can be wonderfully
simple, elegant and even beautiful for certain things, wo don't chuck
iterative out the window; because it's better for even more things. IOW,
don't throw the baby out with the bathwater.

Again and again, as I encounter new things in OO, once I research how to
approach it, I can't help but think how I would have done the same thing
using structured procedural techniques, and am stunned by how much more
complex the "everything OO or die" approach is. And this impression is
born out by the lack of good data to show that OO makes the process any
easier, quicker, or less expensive. Show me the definitive data, and I'll
admit my impressions are wrong. Since the data backs up my impressions,
I'll continue to believe I'm right. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** tim Josling <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au>
- **Date:** 2008-01-10T20:58:06
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13od1muitt3hofd@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com> <0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <2cWdnUoouf_CPRzanZ2dnUVZ_ternZ2d@comcast.com> <FVpgj.66851$L%6.5193@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6IqdnXzvIcNDrBnanZ2dnUVZ_sOrnZ2d@comcast.com> <fV4hj.44124$Mu4.7226@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5umh13F1iqnuaU1@mid.individual.net> <N3qhj.30175$L27.28676@bignews9.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Thu, 10 Jan 2008 08:36:29 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> You have yet to supply any *hard data* to show your productivity and
> accuracy is better with OO, you have only supplied subjective
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> white, on paper, clear for anyone to see.

Past paradigm shifts were accompanied by hard data eg machine code to
assembler, assembler to high level languages, structured programming.

The switch to OO has not been accompanied by good data to show benefits.

Consider one of the few studies that has been done:

http://page.mi.fu-berlin.de/prechelt/Biblio/jccpprt2_advances2003.pdf

Note that the C++ and Java programs were slower than the C programs *and*
took longer to develop. This is consistent with my experience that OO code
often does struggle with performance. OO projects, especially Java-based
projects, are often horrendously slow and expensive.

Richard Gabriel reports in Patterns of Software that even a year after
conversion from Lisp to C++ ie functional to OO programming, the
productivity was down 30% across the whole project life cycle. The initial
hit was far higher, and this was with an elite group of programmers.

I see three problems at least

1. The fact is that much of reality is not well-modelled using the
paradigm "nouns with verbs attached" which is the OO paradigm. Part of the
problem is trying to shoe-horn problem spaces that don't fit OO into the
OO paradigm.

2. OO languages tend to become very complex due to inheritance,
overloading of operators, virtual methods, attempts to reduce the impact
of static typing (via function overloading), and attempts to reintroduce
macros while pretending not to do so (templates etc). These issues
introduce a blizzard of hard to understand corner cases and obscure bugs.

Java in a Nutshell is now 1,000 pages long. The cognitive demands and the
number of things one needs to memorise create a learning curve and a
barrier to entry, as others have reported here.

3. The tendency to reify everything as objects means that things that
should be in databases end up as classes and objects. The result is that
lots of things are hard-coded and the resulting system is inflexible and
hard to maintain.

If there was any evidence that OO improved productivity they would be
shouting it from the rooftops.

With approaches that actually do offer productivity benefits, the evidence
is out there:

http://norvig.com/java-lisp.html

And see also the original study above for productivity benefits of
languages with dynamic typing. It is interesting that these benefits seem
independent of whether the languages supports OO.

Tim Josling
```

- **Message-ID:** `<13oe2umlhh61q9b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<28b05ca0-c7a7-4ad5-b307-644da0ffc5fa@h11g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <NoednW8wwYa-heLaRVn_vwA@giganews.com> <lAKfj.62062$K27.47894@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <FdidnWXlad8m3x3anZ2dnUVZ_oCvnZ2d@comcast.com> <0t5gj.59398$rc2.24476@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <2cWdnUoouf_CPRzanZ2dnUVZ_ternZ2d@comcast.com> <FVpgj.66851$L%6.5193@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6IqdnXzvIcNDrBnanZ2dnUVZ_sOrnZ2d@comcast.com> <fV4hj.44124$Mu4.7226@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5umh13F1iqnuaU1@mid.individual.net> <N3qhj.30175$L27.28676@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <13od1muitt3hofd@corp.supernews.com> <13odgmt2jefmtc7@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Thu, 10 Jan 2008 20:14:21 -0500, Charles Hottel wrote:

> Interesting but aslo dated by 4-5 years during which time java performance 
> has steadily improved. There is also a Java optimiser and native code 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Undoubtedly scripting languages have their place.

Even with the claimed 4X speedup from java -client that's barely on a par
with native C. And with a tiny toy benchmark (checking the sign of an
integer) that is not a realistic test.

I would be more convinced to see say specint or specfp in Java
running anywhere near C speeds. As a rule Java is 3X slower than a HLL
like C and C or COBOL is 3X slower than assembler. With C/COBOL versus
assembler there is a benefit.

The main issue is not runtime speed, but development productivity. As you
pointed out, that includes maintenance. I haven't seen any signs that Java
maintenance fares any better than start from scratch development though.

To the (surprisingly significant) extent that lines of code is an
indication, we can expect Java will fare poorly. Because of the large
amounts of boilerplate code required to do even simple things.

Tim Josling
```

```
On Fri, 11 Jan 2008 07:23:08 -0700, LX-i wrote:

>> I wasn't as clear as I could have been. I am not talking about individual
>> objects, but classes of objects. 
> 
> Could you give an example?  I'm not quite sure what you mean by that.
>

If you look at most of the texts on Java / OO they are full of examples
like this. 

Class regular-geometric-shape
	field length-of-side.

Class square subclasses regular-geometric-shape...

Class triangle subclasses regular-geometric-shape...

Class pentagon subclasses regular-geometric-shape...

Class hexagon subclasses regular-geometric-shape...

etc.

IMHO these subclasses would be better modelled as a single class with some
data saying how many sides. This makes it a lot easier to add say
octagons.

OR
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Java is becoming the new Cobol

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2008-01-11T12:38:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s6hfo31crb7k42f443063j8sh37i4cdp0k@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<2cWdnUoouf_CPRzanZ2dnUVZ_ternZ2d@comcast.com> <FVpgj.66851$L%6.5193@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <6IqdnXzvIcNDrBnanZ2dnUVZ_sOrnZ2d@comcast.com> <fV4hj.44124$Mu4.7226@bignews7.bellsouth.net> <5umh13F1iqnuaU1@mid.individual.net> <N3qhj.30175$L27.28676@bignews9.bellsouth.net> <13od1muitt3hofd@corp.supernews.com> <-o2dnd6aWtI9eBvanZ2dnUVZ_tzinZ2d@comcast.com> <13oe35j55opag0c@corp.supernews.com> <iYKdnZf_foFX5hranZ2dnUVZ_sbinZ2d@comcast.com> <13offrpnknc146@corp.supernews.com>`

```

I see lots of hard coding from people who find re-use of programmer
supplied objects to be less safe than they want.   They don't want to
modify someone else's code and then test all of the applications that
access that someone else's code.  (Maybe modifying the test
procedures).   Accepting that the automated tests still test all of
the functionality (when that functionality is in another part of the
company's business from yours) can get people into trouble.

Solution?   Ignore the benefits of reusability - keep isolated.
```
